chunks.text: Thus, when implementing extensions with overlapping instructions, there is no redundancy in logic or encoding.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

chunks.text: The bitmanip extension follows the convention in RV64 that w-suffixed instructions (without a dot before the w) ignore the upper 32 bits of their inputs, operate on the least-significant 32 bits as signed values, and produce a 32-bit signed result that is sign-extended to XLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

chunks.text: Bitmanip instructions with the suffix .uw have one operand that is an unsigned 32-bit value that is extracted from the least-significant 32 bits of the specified register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

chunks.text: Bitmanip instructions with the suffixes .b, .h, and .w only look at the least-significant 8 bits, 16 bits, and 32 bits of the input (respectively) and produce an XLEN-wide result that is sign-extended or zero-extended, based on the specific instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions

chunks.text: The slli.uw -- added in this extension -- can be used when the index is to be interpreted as an unsigned word.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zba: Extension for Address generation, Version 1.0.0

chunks.text: These instructions perform the sign extension or zero extension of the least-significant 8 bits or 16 bits of the source register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > ext:zbb[] Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > Sign extension and zero extension

chunks.text: However, where the shift-logical instructions shift in zeros, the rotate instructions shift in the bits that were shifted out of the other side of the value.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > ext:zbb[] Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > Bitwise rotation

chunks.text: One use-case is string-processing functions, such as *strlen* and *strcpy*, which can use *orc.b* to test for the terminating zero byte by counting the set bits in leading non-zero bytes in a word.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > ext:zbb[] Extension for Basic bit-manipulation, Version 1.0.0 > OR Combine

chunks.text: These instructions implement a "lookup table" for 4 and 8 bit elements inside the general purpose registers. rs1 is used as a vector of N-bit words, and rs2 as a vector of N-bit indices into rs1.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkx: Extension for Crossbar permutations, Version 1.0.0

chunks.text: Elements in rs1 are replaced by the indexed element in rs2, or zero if the index into rs2 is out of bounds.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Zbkx: Extension for Crossbar permutations, Version 1.0.0

chunks.text: foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 1) by 1) { output = if ((rs2val >> i) & 1) then output ^ (rs1val << i); else output; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clmul

chunks.text: foreach (i from 1 to xlen by 1) { output = if ((rs2val >> i) & 1) then output ^ (rs1val >> (xlen - i)); else output; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clmulh

chunks.text: foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 1) by 1) { output = if ((rs2val >> i) & 1) then output ^ (rs1val >> (xlen - i - 1)); else output; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clmulr

chunks.text: Accordingly, if the input is 0, the output is XLEN, and if the most-significant bit of the input is a 1, the output is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clz

chunks.text: function HighestSetBit x = { foreach (i from (xlen - 1) to 0 by 1 in dec) if [x[i]] 0b1 then return(i) else (); return -1; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clz

chunks.text: function HighestSetBit32 x = { foreach (i from 31 to 0 by 1 in dec) if [x[i]] 0b1 then return(i) else (); return -1; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > clzw

chunks.text: foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 1) in inc) if rs[i] 0b1 then bitcount = bitcount + 1 else ();
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > cpop

chunks.text: foreach (i from 0 to 31 in inc) if val[i] 0b1 then bitcount = bitcount + 1 else ();
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > cpopw

chunks.text: Accordingly, if the input is 0, the output is XLEN, and if the least-significant bit of the input is a 1, the output is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > ctz

chunks.text: function LowestSetBit x = { foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 1) by 1 in dec) if [x[i]] 0b1 then return(i) else (); return xlen; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > ctz

chunks.text: Accordingly, if the least-significant word is 0, the output is 32, and if the least-significant bit of the input is a 1, the output is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > ctzw

chunks.text: function LowestSetBit32 x = { foreach (i from 0 to 31 by 1 in dec) if [x[i]] 0b1 then return(i) else (); return 32; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > ctzw

chunks.text: let result = if rs1val <s rs2val then rs2val else rs1val;
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > max

chunks.text: let result = if rs1val <u rs2val then rs2val else rs1val;
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > maxu

chunks.text: let result = if rs1val <s rs2val then rs1val else rs2val;
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > min

chunks.text: let result = if rs1val <u rs2val then rs1val else rs2val;
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > minu

chunks.text: This sets the bits of each byte in the result rd to all zeros if no bit within the respective byte of rs is set, or to all ones if any bit within the respective byte of rs is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > orc.b

chunks.text: foreach (i from 0 to (xlen - 8) by 8) { output[(i + 7)..i] = if input[(i + 7)..i] 0 then 0b00000000 else 0b11111111; }
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > orc.b

chunks.text: Hence, for RV32, any extension that contains the `pack` instruction also contains the `zext.h` instruction (but not necessarily the `c.zext.h` instruction, which is only guaranteed to exist if both the Zcb and Zbb extensions are implemented).
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > pack

chunks.text: Hence, for RV64, any extension that contains the `packw` instruction also contains the `zext.h` instruction (but not necessarily the `c.zext.h` instruction, which is only guaranteed to exist if both the Zcb and Zbb extensions are implemented).
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > packw

chunks.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- let shamt = if xlen 32 then X(rs2)[4..0] else X(rs2)[5..0]; let result = (X(rs1) (xlen - shamt));
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rol

chunks.text: The resulting word value is sign-extended by copying bit 31 to all of the more-significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rolw

chunks.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- let shamt = if xlen 32 then X(rs2)[4..0] else X(rs2)[5..0]; let result = (X(rs1) >> shamt) | (X(rs1) << (xlen - shamt));
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > ror

chunks.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- let shamt = if xlen 32 then shamt[4..0] else shamt[5..0]; let result = (X(rs1) >> shamt) | (X(rs1) << (xlen - shamt));
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rori

chunks.text: The resulting word value is sign-extended by copying bit 31 to all of the more-significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > roriw

chunks.text: The resultant word is sign-extended by copying bit 31 to all of the more-significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > rorw

chunks.text: The rs1 register contains a vector of XLEN/8 8-bit elements.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > xperm8

chunks.text: The rs2 register contains a vector of XLEN/8 8-bit indexes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > xperm8

chunks.text: The rs1 register contains a vector of XLEN/4 4-bit elements.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > xperm4

chunks.text: The rs2 register contains a vector of XLEN/4 4-bit indexes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > xperm4

chunks.text: A RISC-V ISA is defined as a base integer ISA, which must be present in any implementation, plus optional extensions to the base ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > RISC-V ISA Overview

chunks.text: For this purpose, we divide each RISC-V instruction-set encoding space (and related encoding spaces such as the CSRs) into three disjoint categories: standard, reserved, and custom.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > RISC-V ISA Overview

chunks.text: Custom encodings shall never be used for standard extensions and are made available for vendor-specific non-standard extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > RISC-V ISA Overview

chunks.text: The base RISC-V ISA has fixed-length 32-bit instructions that must be naturally aligned on 32-bit boundaries.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Base Instruction-Length Encoding

chunks.text: Instruction encodings that are not defined here and not defined by some other means may cause a fatal trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Exceptions, Traps, and Interrupts

chunks.text: All multi-letter extensions, including those with the "Z" prefix, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore, e.g., "RV32IMACZicsrZifencei".
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Additional Standard Unprivileged Extension Names

chunks.text: Standard user-level extensions should be listed after standard unprivileged extensions, and like other multi-letter extensions, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > User-Level Instruction-Set Extension Names

chunks.text: Standard supervisor-level extensions should be listed after standard unprivileged and user-level extensions, and like other multi-letter extensions, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Supervisor-Level Instruction-Set Extension Names

chunks.text: Standard hypervisor-level extensions should be listed after standard unprivileged, user-level and supervisor-level extensions, and like other multi-letter extensions, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Hypervisor-Level Instruction-Set Extension Names

chunks.text: Standard machine-level extensions should be listed after standard lesser-privileged extensions, and like other multi-letter extensions, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Machine-Level Instruction-Set Extension Names

chunks.text: Non-standard extensions must be listed after all standard extensions, and, like other multi-letter extensions, must be separated from other multi-letter extensions by an underscore.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Non-Standard Extension Names

chunks.text: Because the "P" extension for Packed SIMD can be confused for the decimal point in a version number, it must be preceded by an underscore if it follows another extension with a version number.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Version Numbers

chunks.text: Changes in major version numbers imply a loss of backwards compatibility, whereas changes in only the minor version number must be backwards-compatible.
chunks.section: Preamble > ISA Extension Naming Conventions > Version Numbers

chunks.text: The term `xLPE` is used to determine if forward-edge CFI using landing pads provided by the Zicfilp extension is enabled at a privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State

chunks.text: When S-mode is implemented, it is determined as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State

chunks.text: When S-mode is not implemented, it is determined as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State

chunks.text: A trap may need to be delivered to the same or to a higher privilege mode upon completion of `JALR`/`C.JALR`/`C.JR`, but before the instruction at the target of indirect call/jump was decoded, due to:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: Asynchronous interrupts. * Synchronous exceptions with priority higher than that of a software-check exception with `xtval` set to "landing pad fault (code=2)" (See of Privileged Specification).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: The software-check exception caused by Zicfilp has higher priority than an illegal-instruction exception but lower priority than instruction access-fault.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: The software-check exception due to the instruction not being an `LPAD` instruction when `ELP` is `LPEXPECTED` or a software-check exception caused by the `LPAD` instruction itself leads to a trap being delivered to the same or to a higher privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: In such cases, the `ELP` prior to the trap, the previous `ELP`, must be preserved by the trap delivery such that it can be restored on a return from the trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: To store the previous `ELP` state on trap delivery to M-mode, an `MPELP` bit is provided in the `mstatus` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: To store the previous `ELP` state on trap delivery to S/HS-mode, an `SPELP` bit is provided in the `mstatus` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: The `SPELP` bit in `mstatus` can be accessed through the `sstatus` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: To store the previous `ELP` state on traps to VS-mode, a `SPELP` bit is defined in the `vsstatus` (VS-modes version of `sstatus`).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: To store the previous `ELP` state on transition to Debug Mode, a `pelp` bit is defined in the `dcsr` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: When a trap is taken into privilege mode `x`, the `xPELP` is set to `ELP` and `ELP` is set to `NOLPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: An `MRET` or `SRET` instruction is used to return from a trap in M-mode or S-mode, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: Upon entry into Debug Mode, the `pelp` bit in `dcsr` is updated with the `ELP` at the privilege level the hart was previously in, and the `ELP` is set to `NOLPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: See also for semantics added to the RNMI trap and the MNRET instruction when this extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing-Pad-Enabled (LPE) State > Preserving Expected Landing Pad State on Traps

chunks.text: Attempts to access the `ssp` CSR may result in either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction exception, contingent upon the state of the *x*`envcfg.SSE` fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: If the privilege mode is less than M and `menvcfg.SSE` is 0, an illegal-instruction exception is raised. *
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: Otherwise, if in U-mode and `senvcfg.SSE` is 0, an illegal-instruction exception is raised. *
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: Otherwise, if in VS-mode and `henvcfg.SSE` is 0, a virtual-instruction exception is raised. *
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: Otherwise, if in VU-mode and either `henvcfg.SSE` or `senvcfg.SSE` is 0, a virtual-instruction exception is raised. *
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: Otherwise, the access is allowed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`) CSR access control

chunks.text: The term `xSSE` is used to determine if backward-edge CFI using shadow stacks provided by the Zicfiss extension is enabled at a privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow-Stack-Enabled (SSE) State

chunks.text: When S-mode is implemented, it is determined as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow-Stack-Enabled (SSE) State

chunks.text: When S-mode is not implemented, then `xSSE` is 0 at both M and U privilege modes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow-Stack-Enabled (SSE) State

chunks.text: When `menvcfg.SSE=0`, this encoding remains reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Similarly, when `V=1` and `henvcfg.SSE=0`, this encoding remains reserved at `VS` and `VU` levels.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: If `satp.MODE` (or `vsatp.MODE` when `V=1`) is set to `Bare` and the effective privilege mode is less than M, shadow stack instructions raise a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: When the effective privilege mode is M, memory access by an `SSAMOSWAP.W/D` instruction results in a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Attempts will raise a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Access to a SS page using cache-block operation (`CBO.*`) instructions is not permitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Such accesses will raise a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Implicit accesses, including instruction fetches to an SS page, are not permitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Such accesses will raise an access-fault exception appropriate to the access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: If a shadow stack (SS) instruction raises an access-fault, page-fault, or guest-page-fault exception that is supposed to indicate the original instruction type (load or store/AMO), then the reported exception cause is respectively a store/AMO access fault (code 7), a store/AMO page fault (code 15), or a store/AMO guest-page fault (code 23).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Should a shadow stack instruction access a page that is not designated as a shadow stack page and is not marked as read-only (`pte.xwr=001`), a store/AMO access-fault exception will be invoked.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: Conversely, if the page being accessed by a shadow stack instruction is a read-only page, a store/AMO page-fault exception will be triggered.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: If the virtual address in `ssp` is not `XLEN` aligned, then the `SSPUSH`/ `C.SSPUSH`/`SSPOPCHK`/`C.SSPOPCHK` instructions cause a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: If the memory referenced by `SSPUSH`/`C.SSPUSH`/`SSPOPCHK`/`C.SSPOPCHK`/`SSAMOSWAP.W/D` instructions is not idempotent, then the instructions cause a store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: When G-stage page tables are active, the shadow stack instructions that access memory require the G-stage page table to have read-write permission for the accessed memory; else a store/AMO guest-page-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: The PMA checks are extended to require memory referenced by shadow stack instructions to be idempotent.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: The PMP checks are extended to require read-write permission for memory accessed by shadow stack instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: If the PMP does not provide read-write permissions or if the accessed memory is not idempotent then a store/AMO access-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Memory Protection

chunks.text: The SYSTEM major opcode is used to encode all privileged instructions in the RISC-V ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: These can be divided into two main classes: those that atomically read-modify-write control and status registers (CSRs), which are defined in the Zicsr extension, and all other privileged instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: The privileged architecture requires the Zicsr extension; which other privileged instructions are required depends on the privileged-architecture feature set.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: In addition to the unprivileged state described in Volume I of this manual, an implementation may contain additional CSRs, accessible by some subset of the privilege levels using the CSR instructions described in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: In this chapter, we map out the CSR address space.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: The following chapters describe the function of each of the CSRs according to privilege level, as well as the other privileged instructions which are generally closely associated with a particular privilege level.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: Standard CSRs do not have side effects on reads but may have side effects on writes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs)

chunks.text: The standard RISC-V ISA sets aside a 12-bit encoding space (csr[11:0]) for up to 4,096 CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Instructions that access a non-existent CSR are reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Attempts to access a CSR without appropriate privilege level raise illegal-instruction exceptions or, as described in , virtual-instruction exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Attempts to write a read-only register raise illegal-instruction exceptions. A read/write register might also contain some bits that are read-only, in which case writes to the read-only bits are ignored.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: also indicates the convention to allocate CSR addresses between standard and custom uses.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: The CSR addresses designated for custom uses will not be redefined by future standard extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Machine-mode standard read-write CSRs `0x7A0`-`0x7BF` are reserved for use by the debug system.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Of these CSRs, `0x7A0`-`0x7AF` are accessible to machine mode, whereas `0x7B0`-`0x7BF` are only visible to debug mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: Implementations should raise illegal-instruction exceptions on machine-mode access to the latter set of registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Address Mapping Conventions

chunks.text: list the CSRs that have currently been allocated CSR addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Listing

chunks.text: The timers, counters, and floating-point CSRs are standard unprivileged CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Listing

chunks.text: The other registers are used by privileged code, as described in the following chapters.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Listing

chunks.text: The following definitions and abbreviations are used in specifying the behavior of fields within the CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications

chunks.text: Some whole read/write fields are reserved for future use.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Reserved Writes Preserve Values, Reads Ignore Values (WPRI)

chunks.text: Software should ignore the values read from these fields, and should preserve the values held in these fields when writing values to other fields of the same register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Reserved Writes Preserve Values, Reads Ignore Values (WPRI)

chunks.text: For forward compatibility, implementations that do not furnish these fields must make them read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Reserved Writes Preserve Values, Reads Ignore Values (WPRI)

chunks.text: These fields are labeled *WPRI* in the register descriptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Reserved Writes Preserve Values, Reads Ignore Values (WPRI)

chunks.text: Some read/write CSR fields specify behavior for only a subset of possible bit encodings, with other bit encodings reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write/Read Only Legal Values (WLRL)

chunks.text: These fields are labeled *WLRL* in the register descriptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write/Read Only Legal Values (WLRL)

chunks.text: Implementations are permitted but not required to raise an illegal-instruction exception if an instruction attempts to write a non-supported value to a *WLRL* field.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write/Read Only Legal Values (WLRL)

chunks.text: Implementations can return arbitrary bit patterns on the read of a *WLRL* field when the last write was of an illegal value, but the value returned should deterministically depend on the illegal written value and the value of the field prior to the write.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write/Read Only Legal Values (WLRL)

chunks.text: Some read/write CSR fields are only defined for a subset of bit encodings, but allow any value to be written while guaranteeing to return a legal value whenever read.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write Any Values, Reads Legal Values (WARL)

chunks.text: Assuming that writing the CSR has no other side effects, the range of supported values can be determined by attempting to write a desired setting then reading to see if the value was retained.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write Any Values, Reads Legal Values (WARL)

chunks.text: These fields are labeled *WARL* in the register descriptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write Any Values, Reads Legal Values (WARL)

chunks.text: Implementations will not raise an exception on writes of unsupported values to a *WARL* field.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write Any Values, Reads Legal Values (WARL)

chunks.text: Implementations can return any legal value on the read of a *WARL* field when the last write was of an illegal value, but the legal value returned should deterministically depend on the illegal written value and the architectural state of the hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Specifications > Write Any Values, Reads Legal Values (WARL)

chunks.text: If a write to one CSR changes the set of legal values allowed for a field of a second CSR, then unless specified otherwise, the second CSR's field immediately gets an `UNSPECIFIED` value from among its new legal values.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Modulation

chunks.text: This is true even if the field's value before the write remains legal after the write; the value of the field may be changed in consequence of the write to the controlling CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Modulation

chunks.text: A change to the value of a CSR for this reason is not a write to the affected CSR and thus does not trigger any side effects specified for that CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Field Modulation

chunks.text: Unless otherwise specified, the value returned by an implicit read of a CSR is the same value that would have been returned by an explicit read of the CSR, using a CSR-access instruction in a sufficient privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > Implicit Reads of CSRs

chunks.text: The value of the previous-width CSR is copied to a temporary register of the same width. .
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: For the read-only bits of the previous-width CSR, the bits at the same positions in the temporary register are set to zeros. .
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: The width of the temporary register is changed to the new width.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: If the new width W is narrower than the previous width, the least-significant W bits of the temporary register are retained and the more-significant bits are discarded.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: If the new width is wider than the previous width, the temporary register is zero-extended to the wider width. .
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: Each writable field of the new-width CSR takes the value of the bits at the same positions in the temporary register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: Changing the width of a CSR is not a read or write of the CSR and thus does not trigger any side effects.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > CSR Width Modulation

chunks.text: If a standard CSR is wider than XLEN bits, then an explicit read of the CSR returns the register's least-significant XLEN bits, and an explicit write to the CSR modifies only the register's least-significant XLEN bits, leaving the upper bits unchanged.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > Explicit Accesses to CSRs Wider than XLEN

chunks.text: Some standard CSRs, such as the counter CSRs of extension Zicntr, are always 64 bits, even when XLEN=32 (RV32).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > Explicit Accesses to CSRs Wider than XLEN

chunks.text: The high-half CSR aliases bits 63:32 of its namesake 64-bit CSR, thus providing a way for RV32 software to read and modify the otherwise-unreachable 32 bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > Explicit Accesses to CSRs Wider than XLEN

chunks.text: Standard high-half CSRs are accessible only when the base RISC-V instruction set is RV32 (XLEN=32).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control and Status Registers (CSRs) > Explicit Accesses to CSRs Wider than XLEN

chunks.text: The hypervisor extension also adds another stage of address translation, from guest physical addresses to supervisor physical addresses, to virtualize the memory and memory-mapped I/O subsystems for a guest operating system. HS-mode acts the same as S-mode, but with additional instructions and CSRs that control the new stage of address translation and support hosting a guest OS in virtual S-mode (VS-mode).
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The hypervisor extension depends on an "I" base integer ISA with 32 `x` registers (RV32I or RV64I), not RV32E or RV64E, which have only 16 `x` registers. CSR `mtval` must not be read-only zero, and standard page-based address translation must be supported, either Sv32 for RV32, or a minimum of Sv39 for RV64.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The hypervisor extension is enabled by setting bit 7 in the `misa` CSR, which corresponds to the letter H. RISC-V harts that implement the hypervisor extension are encouraged not to hardwire `misa`[7], so that the extension may be disabled.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When V=1, the hart is either in virtual S-mode (VS-mode), or in virtual U-mode (VU-mode) atop a guest OS running in VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Privilege Modes

chunks.text: When V=0, the hart is either in M-mode, in HS-mode, or in U-mode atop an OS running in HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Privilege Modes

chunks.text: HS-mode is more privileged than VS-mode, and VS-mode is more privileged than VU-mode. VS-mode interrupts are globally disabled when executing in U-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Privilege Modes

chunks.text: An OS or hypervisor running in HS-mode uses the supervisor CSRs to interact with the exception, interrupt, and address-translation subsystems.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Additional CSRs are provided to HS-mode, but not to VS-mode, to manage two-stage address translation and to control the behavior of a VS-mode guest: `hstatus`, `hedeleg`, `hideleg`, `hvip`, `hip`, `hie`, `hgeip`, `hgeie`, `henvcfg`, `henvcfgh`, `hcounteren`, `htimedelta`, `htimedeltah`, `htval`, `htinst`, and `hgatp`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Furthermore, several virtual supervisor CSRs (VS CSRs) are replicas of the normal supervisor CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: When V=1, the VS CSRs substitute for the corresponding supervisor CSRs, taking over all functions of the usual supervisor CSRs except as specified otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Instructions that normally read or modify a supervisor CSR shall instead access the corresponding VS CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: When V=1, an attempt to read or write a VS CSR directly by its own separate CSR address causes a virtual-instruction exception. (Attempts from U-mode cause an illegal-instruction exception as usual.) The VS CSRs can be accessed as themselves only from M-mode or HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: While V=1, the normal HS-level supervisor CSRs that are replaced by VS CSRs retain their values but do not affect the behavior of the machine unless specifically documented to do so.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Conversely, when V=0, the VS CSRs do not ordinarily affect the behavior of the machine other than being readable and writable by CSR instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Some standard supervisor CSRs (`senvcfg`, `scounteren`, and `scontext`, possibly others) have no matching VS CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: These supervisor CSRs continue to have their usual function and accessibility even when V=1, except with VS-mode and VU-mode substituting for HS-mode and U-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: Hypervisor software is expected to manually swap the contents of these registers as needed.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: In this chapter, we use the term HSXLEN to refer to the effective XLEN when executing in HS-mode, and VSXLEN to refer to the effective XLEN when executing in VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs

chunks.text: The `hstatus` register is an HSXLEN-bit read/write register formatted as shown in when HSXLEN=32 and when HSXLEN=64.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The `hstatus` register provides facilities analogous to the `mstatus` register for tracking and controlling the exception behavior of a VS-mode guest.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The VSXL field controls the effective XLEN for VS-mode (known as VSXLEN), which may differ from the XLEN for HS-mode (HSXLEN).
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When HSXLEN=32, the VSXL field does not exist, and VSXLEN=32.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When HSXLEN=64, VSXL is a *WARL* field that is encoded the same as the MXL field of `misa`, shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: In particular, an implementation may make VSXL be a read-only field whose value always ensures that VSXLEN=HSXLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: If HSXLEN is changed from 32 to a wider width, and if field VSXL is not restricted to a single value, it gets the value corresponding to the widest supported width not wider than the new HSXLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The `hstatus` fields VTSR, VTW, and VTVM are defined analogously to the `mstatus` fields TSR, TW, and TVM, but affect execution only in VS-mode, and cause virtual-instruction exceptions instead of illegal-instruction exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When VTSR=1, an attempt in VS-mode to execute SRET raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When VTW=1 (and assuming `mstatus`.TW=0), an attempt in VS-mode to execute WFI raises a virtual-instruction exception if the WFI does not complete within an implementation-specific, bounded time limit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: An implementation may have WFI always raise a virtual-instruction exception in VS-mode when VTW=1 (and `mstatus`.TW=0), even if there are pending globally-disabled interrupts when the instruction is executed.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When VTVM=1, an attempt in VS-mode to execute SFENCE.VMA or SINVAL.VMA or to access CSR `satp` raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The VGEIN (Virtual Guest External Interrupt Number) field selects a guest external interrupt source for VS-level external interrupts. VGEIN is a *WLRL* field that must be able to hold values between zero and the maximum guest external interrupt number (known as GEILEN), inclusive.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When VGEIN=0, no guest external interrupt source is selected for VS-level external interrupts. GEILEN may be zero, in which case VGEIN may be read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: Guest external interrupts are explained in , and the use of VGEIN is covered further in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: Field HU (Hypervisor in U-mode) controls whether the virtual-machine load/store instructions, HLV, HLVX, and HSV, can be used also in U-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When HU=1, these instructions can be executed in U-mode the same as in HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When HU=0, all hypervisor instructions cause an illegal-instruction exception in U-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When the Ssnpm extension is not implemented, the `HUPMM` field is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The `HUPMM` field is read-only zero for RV32.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The SPV bit (Supervisor Previous Virtualization mode) is written by the implementation whenever a trap is taken into HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: Just as the SPP bit in `sstatus` is set to the (nominal) privilege mode at the time of the trap, the SPV bit in `hstatus` is set to the value of the virtualization mode V at the time of the trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When an SRET instruction is executed when V=0, V is set to SPV.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: When V=1 and a trap is taken into HS-mode, bit SPVP (Supervisor Previous Virtual Privilege) is set to the nominal privilege mode at the time of the trap, the same as `sstatus`.SPP.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: But if V=0 before a trap, SPVP is left unchanged on trap entry. SPVP controls the effective privilege of explicit memory accesses made by the virtual-machine load/store instructions, HLV, HLVX, and HSV.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: Field GVA (Guest Virtual Address) is written by the implementation whenever a trap is taken into HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: For any trap (breakpoint, address misaligned, access fault, page fault, or guest-page fault) that writes a guest virtual address to `stval`, GVA is set to 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: For any other trap into HS-mode, GVA is set to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: The VSBE bit is a *WARL* field that controls the endianness of explicit memory accesses made from VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: If VSBE=0, explicit load and store memory accesses made from VS-mode are little-endian, and if VSBE=1, they are big-endian. VSBE also controls the endianness of all implicit accesses to VS-level memory management data structures, such as page tables.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: An implementation may make VSBE a read-only field that always specifies the same endianness as HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Status (`hstatus`) Register

chunks.text: Register `hedeleg` is a 64-bit read/write register, formatted as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Register `hideleg` is an HSXLEN-bit read/write register, formatted as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: By default, all traps at any privilege level are handled in M-mode, though M-mode usually uses the `medeleg` and `mideleg` CSRs to delegate some traps to HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: The `hedeleg` and `hideleg` CSRs allow these traps to be further delegated to a VS-mode guest; their layout is the same as `medeleg` and `mideleg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: A synchronous trap that has been delegated to HS-mode (using `medeleg`) is further delegated to VS-mode if V=1 before the trap and the corresponding `hedeleg` bit is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Each bit of `hedeleg` shall be either writable or read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Many bits of `hedeleg` are required specifically to be writable or zero, as enumerated in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Bit 0, corresponding to instruction address-misaligned exceptions, must be writable if IALIGN=32.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: When XLEN=32, `hedelegh` is a 32-bit read/write register that aliases bits 63:32 of `hedeleg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Register `hedelegh` does not exist when XLEN=64.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: An interrupt that has been delegated to HS-mode (using `mideleg`) is further delegated to VS-mode if the corresponding `hideleg` bit is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Among bits 15:0 of `hideleg`, bits 10, 6, and 2 (corresponding to the standard VS-level interrupts) are writable, and bits 12, 9, 5, and 1 (corresponding to the standard S-level interrupts) are read-only zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: When a virtual supervisor external interrupt (code 10) is delegated to VS-mode, it is automatically translated by the machine into a supervisor external interrupt (code 9) for VS-mode, including the value written to `vscause` on an interrupt trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Likewise, a virtual supervisor timer interrupt (6) is translated into a supervisor timer interrupt (5) for VS-mode, and a virtual supervisor software interrupt (2) is translated into a supervisor software interrupt (1) for VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Similar translations may or may not be done for platform interrupt causes (codes 16 and above).
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Trap Delegation (`hedeleg` and `hideleg`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits of `hvip` that are not writable are read-only zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The standard portion (bits 15:0) of `hvip` is formatted as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits VSEIP, VSTIP, and VSSIP of `hvip` are writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Setting VSEIP=1 in `hvip` asserts a VS-level external interrupt; setting VSTIP asserts a VS-level timer interrupt; and setting VSSIP asserts a VS-level software interrupt.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Registers `hip` and `hie` are HSXLEN-bit read/write registers that supplement HS-level’s `sip` and `sie` respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The `hip` register indicates pending VS-level and hypervisor-specific interrupts, while `hie` contains enable bits for the same interrupts.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: For each writable bit in `sie`, the corresponding bit shall be read-only zero in both `hip` and `hie`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Hence, the nonzero bits in `sie` and `hie` are always mutually exclusive, and likewise for `sip` and `hip`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: An interrupt i will trap to HS-mode whenever all of the following are true: (a) either the current operating mode is HS-mode and the SIE bit in the `sstatus` register is set, or the current operating mode has less privilege than HS-mode; (b) bit i is set in both `sip` and `sie`, or in both `hip` and `hie`; and (c) bit i is not set in `hideleg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: If bit i of `sie` is read-only zero, the same bit in register `hip` may be writable or may be read-only.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: When bit i in `hip` is writable, a pending interrupt i can be cleared by writing 0 to this bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: If interrupt i can become pending in `hip` but bit i in `hip` is read-only, then either the interrupt can be cleared by clearing bit i of `hvip`, or the implementation must provide some other mechanism for clearing the pending interrupt (which may involve a call to the execution environment).
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: A bit in `hie` shall be writable if the corresponding interrupt can ever become pending in `hip`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits of `hie` that are not writable shall be read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The standard portions (bits 15:0) of registers `hip` and `hie` are formatted as shown in and respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits `hip`.VSEIP and `hie`.VSEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for VS-level external interrupts. VSEIP is read-only in `hip`, and is the logical-OR of these interrupt sources:
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: bit VSEIP of `hvip`; * the bit of `hgeip` selected by `hstatus`.VGEIN; and * any other platform-specific external interrupt signal directed to VS-level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits `hip`.VSTIP and `hie`.VSTIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for VS-level timer interrupts. VSTIP is read-only in `hip`, and is the logical-OR of `hvip`.VSTIP and, when the Sstc extension is implemented, the timer interrupt signal resulting from `vstimecmp`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The `hip`.VSTIP bit, in response to timer interrupts generated by `vstimecmp`, is set by writing `vstimecmp` with a value that is less than or equal to the sum of `time` and `htimedelta`, truncated to 64 bits; it is cleared by writing `vstimecmp` with a greater value.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The `hip`.VSTIP bit remains defined while V=0 as well as V=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Bits `hip`.VSSIP and `hie`.VSSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for VS-level software interrupts. VSSIP in `hip` is an alias (writable) of the same bit in `hvip`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: Multiple simultaneous interrupts destined for HS-mode are handled in the following decreasing priority order: SEI, SSI, STI, SGEI, VSEI, VSSI, VSTI, LCOFI.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Interrupt (`hvip`, `hip`, and `hie`) Registers

chunks.text: The `hgeip` register is an HSXLEN-bit read-only register, formatted as shown in , that indicates pending guest external interrupts for this hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: The `hgeie` register is an HSXLEN-bit read/write register, formatted as shown in , that contains enable bits for the guest external interrupts at this hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: Guest external interrupt number i corresponds with bit i in both `hgeip` and `hgeie`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: Guest external interrupts represent interrupts directed to individual virtual machines at VS-level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: Each bit of `hgeip` summarizes all pending interrupts directed to one virtual hart, as collected and reported by an interrupt controller.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: To distinguish specific pending interrupts from multiple devices, software must query the interrupt controller.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: The number of bits implemented in `hgeip` and `hgeie` for guest external interrupts is UNSPECIFIED and may be zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: This number is known as GEILEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: The least-significant bits are implemented first, apart from bit 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: Hence, if GEILEN is nonzero, bits GEILEN:1 shall be writable in `hgeie`, and all other bit positions shall be read-only zeros in both `hgeip` and `hgeie`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: Register `hgeie` selects the subset of guest external interrupts that cause a supervisor-level (HS-level) guest external interrupt.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: The enable bits in `hgeie` do not affect the VS-level external interrupt signal selected from `hgeip` by `hstatus`.VGEIN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Guest External Interrupt Registers (`hgeip` and `hgeie`)

chunks.text: The `henvcfg` CSR is a 64-bit read/write register, formatted as shown in , that controls certain characteristics of the execution environment when virtualization mode V=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If bit FIOM (Fence of I/O implies Memory) is set to one in `henvcfg`, FENCE instructions executed when V=1 are modified so the requirement to order accesses to device I/O implies also the requirement to order main memory accesses. details the modified interpretation of FENCE instruction bits PI, PO, SI, and SO when FIOM=1 and V=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: Similarly, when FIOM=1 and V=1, if an atomic instruction that accesses a region ordered as device I/O has its aq and/or rl bit set, then that instruction is ordered as though it accesses both device I/O and memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The PBMTE bit controls whether the Svpbmt extension is available for use in VS-stage address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When PBMTE=1, Svpbmt is available for VS-stage address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When PBMTE=0, the implementation behaves as though Svpbmt were not implemented for VS-stage address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If Svpbmt is not implemented, PBMTE is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If the Svadu extension is implemented, the ADUE bit controls whether hardware updating of PTE A/D bits is enabled for VS-stage address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When ADUE=1, hardware updating of PTE A/D bits is enabled during VS-stage address translation, and the implementation behaves as though the Svade extension were not implemented for VS-mode address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When ADUE=0, the implementation behaves as though Svade were implemented for VS-stage address translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If Svadu is not implemented, ADUE is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The Sstc extension adds the `STCE` (STimecmp Enable) bit to `henvcfg` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When the Sstc extension is not implemented, `STCE` is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The `STCE` bit enables `vstimecmp` for VS-mode when set to one.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When `STCE` bit is `henvcfg` is zero, an attempt to access `stimecmp` (really `vstimecmp`) when V=1 raises a virtual-instruction exception, and `VSTIP` in `hip` reverts to its defined behavior as if this extension is not implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The Zicboz extension adds the `CBZE` (Cache Block Zero instruction enable) field to `henvcfg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The `CBZE` field applies to execution of the cache block zero instruction (`CBO.ZERO`) in privilege modes VS and VU, and only when the instruction is HS-qualified.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If the instruction is not HS-qualified, it raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If the instruction is HS-qualified and the `CBZE` field is set to 1, the instruction is enabled for execution; otherwise, if the `CBZE` field is set to 0, it raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When the Zicboz extension is not implemented, `CBZE` is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The Zicbom extension adds the `CBCFE` (Cache Block Clean and Flush instruction Enable) field to `henvcfg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When V=1, if the `CBO.CLEAN` and `CBO.FLUSH` instructions are not HS-qualified, they raise an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If the instructions are HS-qualified and the `CBCFE` field is set to 1, the instructions are enabled for execution; otherwise, if the `CBCFE` field is set to 0, they raise a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When the Zicbom extension is not implemented, `CBCFE` is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The Zicbom extension adds the `CBIE` (Cache Block Invalidate instruction Enable) WARL field to `henvcfg`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The `CBIE` field controls execution of the cache block invalidate instruction (`CBO.INVAL`) in privilege modes VS and VU.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The encoding `10b` is reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When the Zicbom extension is not implemented, `CBIE` is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When V=1, if the `CBO.INVAL` instruction is not HS-qualified, it raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If the instruction is HS-qualified and the `CBIE` field is set to `01b` or `11b`, the instruction is enabled for execution; otherwise, it raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: If `CBO.INVAL` is enabled in HS-mode to perform a flush operation, then when the instruction is enabled in VS- or VU-mode it performs a flush operation, even if `CBIE` is set to `11b`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: Otherwise, when the instruction is enabled for execution, its behavior depends on the `CBIE` encoding, as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: `01b` -- The instruction is executed and performs a flush operation, even if configured by VS-mode to perform an invalidate operation. * `11b` -- The instruction is executed and performs an invalidate operation, unless configured by VS-mode to perform a flush operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: When the Ssnpm extension is not implemented, the `PMM` field is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "H" Extension for Hypervisor Support, Version 1.0 > Hypervisor and Virtual Supervisor CSRs > Hypervisor Environment Configuration Register (`henvcfg`)

chunks.text: The `PMM` field is read-only zero for RV32.
