chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

chunks.text: Main memory and I/O regions may only support a subset or none of the processor-supported atomic operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Atomicity PMAs > AMO PMA

chunks.text: This PMA, if present, specifies the size of a misaligned atomicity granule, a naturally aligned power-of-two number of bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

chunks.text: If a misaligned AMO accesses a region that does not specify a misaligned atomicity granule PMA, or if not all accessed bytes lie within the same misaligned atomicity granule, then an exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

chunks.text: For regular loads and stores that access such a region or for which not all accessed bytes lie within the same atomicity granule, then either an exception is raised, or the access proceeds but is not guaranteed to be atomic.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

chunks.text: Implementations may raise access-fault exceptions instead of address-misaligned exceptions for some misaligned accesses, indicating the instruction should not be emulated by a trap handler.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

chunks.text: Vector memory accesses are also unaffected, so might execute non-atomically even when contained within a misaligned atomicity granule.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Misaligned Atomicity Granule PMA

chunks.text: Accesses by one hart to an I/O region are observable not only by other harts and bus mastering devices but also by the targeted I/O devices, and I/O regions may be accessed with either relaxed or strong ordering.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Memory-Ordering PMAs

chunks.text: The cacheability of a memory region should not affect the software view of the region except for differences reflected in other PMAs, such as main memory versus I/O classification, memory ordering, supported accesses and atomic operations, and coherence.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

chunks.text: Where a platform supports configurable cacheability settings for a memory region, a platform-specific machine-mode routine will change the settings and flush caches if necessary, so the system is only incoherent during the transition between cacheability settings.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

chunks.text: This transitory state should not be visible to lower privilege levels.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

chunks.text: If a PMA indicates non-cacheability, then accesses to that region must be satisfied by the memory itself, not by any caches.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Coherence and Cacheability PMAs

chunks.text: If accesses are non-idempotent, i.e., there is potentially a side effect on any read or write access, then speculative or redundant accesses must be avoided.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

chunks.text: For non-idempotent regions, implicit reads and writes must not be performed early or speculatively, with the following exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

chunks.text: The results of these additional reads may be used to satisfy subsequent early or speculative implicit reads.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

chunks.text: The size of these naturally aligned power-of-2 regions is implementation-defined, but, for systems with page-based virtual memory, must not exceed the smallest supported page size.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Attributes > Idempotency PMAs

chunks.text: To support secure processing and contain faults, it is desirable to limit the physical addresses accessible by software running on a hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

chunks.text: PMP checks are applied to all accesses whose effective privilege mode is S or U, including instruction fetches and data accesses in S and U mode, and data accesses in M-mode when the MPRV bit in `mstatus` is set and the MPP field in `mstatus` contains S or U. PMP checks are also applied to page-table accesses for virtual-address translation, for which the effective privilege mode is S.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

chunks.text: Optionally, PMP checks may additionally apply to M-mode accesses, in which case the PMP registers themselves are locked, so that even M-mode software cannot change them until the hart is reset.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

chunks.text: In effect, PMP can grant permissions to S and U modes, which by default have none, and can revoke permissions from M-mode, which by default has full permissions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

chunks.text: PMP violations are always trapped precisely at the processor.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection

chunks.text: PMP entries are described by an 8-bit configuration register and one MXLEN-bit address register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Some PMP settings additionally use the address register associated with the preceding PMP entry.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Up to 64 PMP entries are supported.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Implementations may implement zero, 16, or 64 PMP entries; the lowest-numbered PMP entries must be implemented first.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: All PMP CSR fields are *WARL* and may be read-only zero. PMP CSRs are only accessible to M-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The PMP configuration registers are densely packed into CSRs to minimize context-switch time.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: For RV32, sixteen CSRs, `pmpcfg0`–`pmpcfg15`, hold the configurations `pmp0cfg`–`pmp63cfg` for the 64 PMP entries, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: For RV64, eight even-numbered CSRs, `pmpcfg0`, `pmpcfg2`, …, `pmpcfg14`, hold the configurations for the 64 PMP entries, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: For RV64, the odd-numbered configuration registers, `pmpcfg1`, `pmpcfg3`, …, `pmpcfg15`, are illegal.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The PMP address registers are CSRs named `pmpaddr0`-`pmpaddr63`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Each PMP address register encodes bits 33-2 of a 34-bit physical address for RV32, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: For RV64, each PMP address register encodes bits 55-2 of a 56-bit physical address, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Not all physical address bits may be implemented, and so the `pmpaddr` registers are *WARL*.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: shows the layout of a PMP configuration register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The R, W, and X bits, when set, indicate that the PMP entry permits read, write, and instruction execution, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: When one of these bits is clear, the corresponding access type is denied.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The R, W, and X fields form a collective *WARL* field for which the combinations with R=0 and W=1 are reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The remaining two fields, A and L, are described in the following sections.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Attempting to fetch an instruction from a PMP region that does not have execute permissions raises an instruction access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Attempting to execute a load, load-reserved, or cache-block management instruction which accesses a physical address within a PMP region without read permissions raises a load access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: Attempting to execute a store, store-conditional, AMO, or cache-block zero instruction which accesses a physical address within a PMP region without write permissions raises a store access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs

chunks.text: The A field in a PMP entry's configuration register encodes the address-matching mode of the associated PMP address register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: The encoding of this field is shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: When A=0, this PMP entry is disabled and matches no addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: Two other address-matching modes are supported: naturally aligned power-of-2 regions (NAPOT), including the special case of naturally aligned four-byte regions (NA4); and the top boundary of an arbitrary range (TOR).
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: These modes support four-byte granularity.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: NAPOT ranges make use of the low-order bits of the associated address register to encode the size of the range, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: If TOR is selected, the associated address register forms the top of the address range, and the preceding PMP address register forms the bottom of the address range.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: If PMP entry i's A field is set to TOR, the entry matches any address y such that `pmpaddr~i-1~`{le}y<``pmpaddr~i~`` (irrespective of the value of `pmpcfg~i-1~`).
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: If PMP entry 0's A field is set to TOR, zero is used for the lower bound, and so it matches any address `y<pmpaddr~0~`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: Although the PMP mechanism supports regions as small as four bytes, platforms may specify coarser PMP regions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: In general, the PMP grain is 2^G+2^ bytes and must be the same across all PMP regions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: When G {ge} 1, the NA4 mode is not selectable.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: Although changing pmpcfg~i~.A[1] affects the value read from pmpaddr~i~, it does not affect the underlying value stored in that register—in particular, pmpaddr~i~[G-1] retains its original value when pmpcfg~i~.A is changed from NAPOT to TOR/OFF then back to NAPOT.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Address Matching

chunks.text: Locked PMP entries remain locked until the hart is reset.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: If PMP entry i is locked, writes to ``pmp``i``cfg`` and ``pmpaddr``i are ignored.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: Additionally, if PMP entry i is locked and ``pmp``i``cfg.A`` is set to TOR, writes to ``pmpaddr``i-1 are ignored.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: In addition to locking the PMP entry, the L bit indicates whether the R/W/X permissions are additionally enforced on M-mode accesses.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: When the L bit is set, these permissions are enforced for all privilege modes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: When the L bit is clear, any M-mode access matching the PMP entry will succeed; the R/W/X permissions apply only to S and U modes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Locking and Privilege Mode

chunks.text: On some implementations, misaligned loads, stores, and instruction fetches may be decomposed into multiple memory operations, some of which may succeed before an access-fault exception occurs, as described in the RVWMO specification. PMP checking is performed on each memory operation independently.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: In particular, a portion of a misaligned store that passes the PMP check may become visible, even if another portion fails the PMP check.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: PMP entries are statically prioritized.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: The lowest-numbered PMP entry that matches any byte of a memory operation determines whether that operation succeeds or fails.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: The matching PMP entry must match all bytes of a memory operation, or the operation fails, irrespective of the L, R, W, and X bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: If a PMP entry matches all bytes of a memory operation, then the L, R, W, and X bits determine whether the operation succeeds or fails.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: If the L bit is clear and the privilege mode of the access is M, the operation succeeds.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: Otherwise, if the L bit is set or the privilege mode of the access is S or U, then the operation succeeds only if the R, W, or X bit corresponding to the access type is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: If no PMP entry matches an M-mode memory operation, the operation succeeds.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: If no PMP entry matches an S-mode or U-mode memory operation, but at least one PMP entry is implemented, the operation fails.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: Failed memory operations generate an instruction, load, or store access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: An access-fault exception is generated if at least one memory operation generated by an instruction fails, though other memory operations generated by that instruction may succeed with visible side effects.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: Notably, instructions that reference virtual memory are decomposed into multiple memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection CSRs > Priority and Matching Logic

chunks.text: When paging is enabled, instructions that access virtual memory may result in multiple physical-memory accesses, including implicit references to the page tables.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: The PMP settings for the resulting physical address may be checked (and possibly cached) at any point between the address translation and the explicit memory access.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: Hence, when the PMP settings are modified, M-mode software must synchronize the PMP settings with the virtual memory system and any PMP or address-translation caches.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: This is accomplished by executing an SFENCE.VMA instruction with rs1=`x0` and rs2=`x0`, after the PMP CSRs are written.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: See for additional synchronization requirements when the hypervisor extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: If page-based virtual memory is not implemented, memory accesses check the PMP settings synchronously, so no SFENCE.VMA is needed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Machine-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Physical Memory Protection > Physical Memory Protection and Paging

chunks.text: Defined the `misa`.B field to reflect that the B extension has been implemented. * Defined the `misa`.V field to reflect that the V extension has been implemented. * Defined the RV32-only `medelegh` and `hedelegh` CSRs. * Defined the misaligned atomicity granule PMA, superseding the proposed Zam extension. * Allocated interrupt 13 for Sscofpmf LCOFI interrupt. * Defined hardware-error and software-check exception codes. * Specified synchronization requirements when changing the PBMTE and ADUE fields in `menvcfg` and `henvcfg`. * Exposed count-overflow interrupts to VS-mode via the Shlcofideleg extension. * Relaxed behavior of some HINTs when MXLEN > XLEN. * Defined the format of the memory-mapped `msip` registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: The previous specification implied that PPO rules other than fences and acquire/release annotations did not apply. * Constrained the LR/SC reservation set size and shape when using page-based virtual memory. * PMP changes require an SFENCE.VMA on any hart that implements page-based virtual memory, even if VM is not currently enabled. * Allowed for speculative updates of page table entry A bits. * Clarify that if the address-translation algorithm non-speculatively reaches a PTE in which a bit reserved for future standard use is set, a page-fault exception must be raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: Removed the N extension. * Defined the mandatory RV32-only CSR `mstatush`, which contains most of the same fields as the upper 32 bits of RV64’s `mstatus`. * Defined the mandatory CSR `mconfigptr`, which if nonzero contains the address of a configuration data structure. * Defined `mseccfg` and `mseccfgh` CSRs, which control the machine’s security configuration. * Defined `menvcfg`, `henvcfg`, and `senvcfg` CSRs (and RV32-only `menvcfgh` and `henvcfgh` CSRs), which control various characteristics of the execution environment. * Designated part of SYSTEM major opcode for custom use. * Permitted the unconditional delegation of less-privileged interrupts. * Added optional big-endian and bi-endian support. * Made priority of load/store/AMO address-misaligned exceptions implementation-defined relative to load/store/AMO page-fault and access-fault exceptions. * PMP reset values are now platform-defined. * An additional 48 optional PMP registers have been defined. * Slightly relaxed the atomicity requirement for A and D bit updates performed by the implementation. * Clarify the architectural behavior of address-translation caches * Added Sv57 and Sv57x4 address translation modes. * Software breakpoint exceptions are permitted to write either 0 or the `pc` to `xtval`. * Clarified that bare S-mode need not support the SFENCE.VMA instruction. * Specified relaxed constraints for implicit reads of non-idempotent regions. * Added the Svnapot Standard Extension, along with the N bit in Sv39, Sv48, and Sv57 PTEs. * Added the Svpbmt Standard Extension, along with the PBMT bits in Sv39, Sv48, and Sv57 PTEs. * Added the Svinval Standard Extension and associated instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: The encoding space has been reserved and may be repurposed at a later date. * A mechanism to improve virtualization performance by trapping S-mode virtual-memory management operations has been added. * The Supervisor Binary Interface (SBI) chapter has been removed, so that it can be maintained as a separate specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: Numerous additions and improvements to the commentary sections. * Change configuration string proposal to be use a search process that supports various formats including Device Tree String and flattened Device Tree. * Made `misa` optionally writable to support modifying base and supported ISA extensions. CSR address of `misa` changed. * Added description of debug mode and debug CSRs. * Added a hardware performance monitoring scheme.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: Since a CSR for security and / or global PMP behavior settings is not available with the current spec, we needed to define a new `mseccfg` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: This new CSR will allow us to add further security configuration options in the future and also allow developers to verify the existence of the new mechanisms defined on this extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: This is a waste of PMP rules and since it’s only needed during boot, ``mseccfg.RLB`` is a simple workaround that can be used temporarily and then disabled and locked down. + Also when ``mseccfg.MML`` is set, according to 4b it’s not possible to add a Shared-Region rule with executable privileges.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: If developers / vendors have no use for such functionality, they should never set ``mseccfg.RLB`` and if possible hard-wire it to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: In any case *RLB should be disabled and locked as soon as possible*. + + [IMPORTANT] Since PMP rules with a higher priority override rules with a lower priority, locked rules must precede non-locked rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: With the current spec M-mode can access any memory region unless restricted by a PMP rule with the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit set.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: Having the option to block anything by default, and use PMP as an allowlist for M-mode is considered a safer approach.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: This functionality may be used during the boot process or upon *PMP reset*, using initial register settings. +
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: The current dual meaning of the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit that marks a rule as Locked and *enforced* on all modes is neither flexible nor clean.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: With the introduction of Machine Mode Lock-down the ``pmpcfg.L`` bit distinguishes between rules that are *enforced* *only* in M-mode (M-mode-only) or *only* in S/U-modes (S/U-mode-only).
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: The rule locking becomes part of the definition of an M-mode-only rule, since when a rule is added in M mode, if not locked, can be modified or removed in a few instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: This separation between M-mode-only and S/U-mode-only rules also allows us to distinguish which regions are to be used by processes in Machine mode (``pmpcfg.L 1``) and which by Supervisor or User mode processes (``pmpcfg.L 0``), in the same way the U bit on the Virtual Memory’s PTEs marks which Virtual Memory pages are to be used by User mode applications (U=1) and which by the Supervisor / OS (U=0).
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: With this distinction in place we are able to implement memory access and execution prevention in M-mode for any physical memory region that is not M-mode-only. + An attacker that manages to tamper with a memory region used by S/U mode, even after successfully tricking a process running in M-mode to use or execute that region, will fail to perform a successful attack since that region will be S/U-mode-only hence any access when in M-mode will trigger an access exception. + +
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: This is similar to the vDSO approach followed on Linux, that allows user space code to execute kernel code without having to perform a system call. + To make sure that shared data regions can’t be executed and shared code regions can’t be modified, the encoding changes the meaning of the ``pmpcfg.X bit``.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: In case of shared data regions, with the exception of the ``pmpcfg.LRWX=1111`` encoding, the ``pmpcfg.X`` bit marks the capability of S/U-mode to write to that region, so it’s not possible to encode an executable shared data region.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: In case of shared code regions, the ``pmpcfg.X`` bit marks the capability of M-mode to read from that region, and since ``pmpcfg.RW=01`` is used for encoding the shared region, it’s not possible to encode a shared writable code region. + + + ..
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: Since we want to limit the attack surface of the system as much as possible, it makes sense to disallow any new code regions which may include malicious code, to be added/executed in M-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: We are only using the encoding ``pmpcfg.RW=01`` together with ``mseccfg.MML``, if ``mseccfg.MML`` is not set the encoding remains usable for future use.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode

chunks.text: If the Shcounterenw extension is implemented, then for any `hpmcounter` that is not read-only zero, the corresponding bit in `hcounteren` must be writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shcounterenw Extension for Counter-Enable Writability, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Shgatpa extension is implemented, then for each supported virtual memory scheme SvNN supported in `satp`, the corresponding hgatp SvNNx4 mode must be supported.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shgatpa Extension for Translation Mode Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Furthermore, the `hgatp` mode Bare must also be supported.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shgatpa Extension for Translation Mode Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Shtvala extension is implemented, `htval` must be written with the faulting guest physical address in all circumstances permitted by the ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shtvala Extension for Trap Value Reporting, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Shvsatpa extension is implemented, all translation modes supported in `satp` must be supported in `vsatp`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shvsatpa Extension for Translation Mode Support, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Shvstvala extension is implemented, `vstval` must be written in all cases described in for `stval`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shvstvala Extension for Trap Value Reporting, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Shvstvecd extension is implemented, then `vstvec.MODE` must be capable of holding the value 0 (Direct).
chunks.section: Preamble > Shvstvecd Extension for Direct Trap Vectoring, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Furthermore, when `vstvec.MODE`=Direct, `vstvec.BASE` must be capable of holding any valid four-byte-aligned address.
chunks.section: Preamble > Shvstvecd Extension for Direct Trap Vectoring, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Counters may be configured with differing scopes, in some cases counting events system-wide, while in others counting events on behalf of a single virtual machine or application.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: These extensions also defines one new CSR, scountinhibit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: For a Machine-level environment, extension *Smcdeleg* (‘Sm’ for Privileged architecture and Machine-level extension, ‘cdeleg’ for Counter Delegation) encompasses all added CSRs and all behavior modifications for a hart, over all privilege levels.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: For a Supervisor-level environment, extension *Ssccfg* (‘Ss’ for Privileged architecture and Supervisor-level extension, ‘ccfg’ for Counter Configuration) provides access to delegated counters, and to new supervisor-level state.For a RISC-V hardware platform, Smcdeleg and Ssccfg must always be implemented in tandem.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Smcdeleg and Ssccfg extensions both depend on the Sscsrind extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The `mcounteren` register allows M-mode to provide the next-lower privilege mode with read access to select counters.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: When the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg extensions are enabled (`menvcfg`.CDE=1), it further allows M-mode to delegate select counters to S-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: The counter state accessible via alias CSRs is shown in the table below.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: `hpmeventi` may represent a subset of the state accessed by the `mhpmeventi` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: Specifically, if Sscofpmf is implemented, event selector bit 62 (MINH) is read-only 0 when accessed through `sireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: Likewise, `cyclecfg` and `instretcfg` may represent a subset of the state accessed by the `mcyclecfg` and `minstretcfg` registers, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: If Smcntrpmf is implemented, counter configuration register bit 62 (MINH) is read-only 0 when accessed through `sireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: If extension Smstateen is implemented, refer to extensions Smcsrind/Sscsrind () for how setting bit 60 of CSR `mstateen0` to zero prevents access to registers `siselect`, `sireg*`, `vsiselect`, and `vsireg*` from privileged modes less privileged than M-mode, and likewise how setting bit 60 of `hstateen0` to zero prevents access to `siselect` and `sireg*` (really `vsiselect` and `vsireg*`) from VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: The remaining rules of this section apply only when access to a CSR is not blocked by `mstateen0`[60] = 0 or `hstateen0`[60] = 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: While the privilege mode is M or S and `siselect` holds a value in the range 0x40-0x5F, illegal-instruction exceptions are raised for the following cases:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: attempts to access any `sireg*` when `menvcfg`.CDE = 0; * attempts to access `sireg3` or `sireg6`; * attempts to access `sireg4` or `sireg5` when XLEN = 64; * attempts to access `sireg*` when `siselect` = 0x41, or when the counter selected by `siselect` is not delegated to S-mode (the corresponding bit in `mcounteren` = 0).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: If any extension upon which the underlying state depends is not implemented, an attempt from M or S mode to access the given state through `sireg*` raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: If the hypervisor (H) extension is also implemented, then as specified by extensions Smcsrind/Sscsrind, a virtual-instruction exception is raised for attempts from VS-mode or VU-mode to directly access `vsiselect` or `vsireg*`, or attempts from VU-mode to access `siselect` or `sireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: An attempt to access any `vsireg*` from M or S mode raises an illegal-instruction exception. * An attempt from VS-mode to access any `sireg*` (really `vsireg*`) raises an illegal-instruction exception if `menvcfg`.CDE = 0, or a virtual-instruction exception if `menvcfg`.CDE = 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation

chunks.text: Smcdeleg/Ssccfg defines a new `scountinhibit` register, a masked alias of `mcountinhibit`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Supervisor Counter Inhibit (`scountinhibit`) Register

chunks.text: For counters delegated to S-mode, the associated `mcountinhibit` bits can be accessed via `scountinhibit`.For counters not delegated to S-mode, the associated bits in `scountinhibit` are read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Supervisor Counter Inhibit (`scountinhibit`) Register

chunks.text: When `menvcfg`.CDE=0, attempts to access `scountinhibit` raise an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Supervisor Counter Inhibit (`scountinhibit`) Register

chunks.text: When Supervisor Counter Delegation is enabled, attempts to access `scountinhibit` from VS-mode or VU-mode raise a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Supervisor Counter Inhibit (`scountinhibit`) Register

chunks.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg/Ssccfg, Sscofpmf, and the H extension, when `menvcfg`.CDE=1, attempts to read `scountovf` from VS-mode or VU-mode raise a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Virtualizing `scountovf`

chunks.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg, Sscofpmf, and Smaia, the local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) bit (bit 13) in each of CSRs `mvip` and `mvien` is implemented and writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Virtualizing Local-Counter-Overflow Interrupts

chunks.text: For implementations that support Smcdeleg/Ssccfg, Sscofpmf, Smaia/Ssaia, and the H extension, the LCOFI bit (bit 13) in each of `hvip` and `hvien` is implemented and writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcdeleg/Ssccfg" Counter Delegation Extensions, Version 1.0 > Counter Delegation > Virtualizing Local-Counter-Overflow Interrupts

chunks.text: mcyclecfg and minstretcfg are 64-bit registers that configure privilege mode filtering for the cycle and instret counters, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in M-mode is inhibited | SINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in S/HS-mode is inhibited | UINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in U-mode is inhibited | VSINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in VS-mode is inhibited | VUINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in VU-mode is inhibited |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: When all xINH bits are zero, event counting is enabled in all modes.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: For each bit in 61:58, if the associated privilege mode is not implemented, the bit is read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: For RV32, bits 63:32 of mcyclecfg can be accessed via the mcyclecfgh CSR, and bits 63:32 of minstretcfg can be accessed via the minstretcfgh CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: The content of these registers may be accessible from Supervisor level if the Smcdeleg/Ssccfg extensions are implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > CSRs > Machine Counter Configuration (`mcyclecfg`, `minstretcfg`) Registers

chunks.text: Mode transition operations (traps and trap returns) may take multiple clock cycles, and the change of privilege mode may be reported as occurring in any one of those cycles (possibly different for each occurrence of a trap or trap return).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Counter Behavior

chunks.text: There are two types of instructions that can affect a privilege mode change: instructions that cause synchronous exceptions to a more privileged mode, and xRET instructions that return to a less privileged mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Counter Behavior

chunks.text: The latter do retire, and should increment instret only if the originating privilege mode is not inhibited.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcntrpmf" Cycle and Instret Privilege Mode Filtering, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Counter Behavior

chunks.text: Smcsrind/Sscsrind is an ISA extension that extends the indirect CSR access mechanism originally defined as part of the https://github.com/riscv/riscv-aia[[.underline]Smaia/Ssaia extensions], in order to make it available for use by other extensions without creating an unnecessary dependence on Smaia/Ssaia.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: It provides a means to access an array of registers via CSRs without requiring allocation of large chunks of the limited CSR address space.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: It enables software to access each of an array of registers by index, without requiring a switch statement with a case for each register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: The machine-level extension *Smcsrind* encompasses all added CSRs and all behavior modifications for a hart, over all privilege levels.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: For a supervisor-level environment, extension *Sscsrind* is essentially the same as Smcsrind except excluding the machine-level CSRs and behavior not directly visible to supervisor level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: [width="100%",cols="15%,12%,12%,15%,46%",options="header",] | |*Number* |*Privilege* |*Width* |*Name* |*Description* |0x350 |MRW |XLEN |`miselect` |Machine indirect register select |0x351 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg` |Machine indirect register alias |0x352 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg2` |Machine indirect register alias 2 |0x353 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg3` |Machine indirect register alias 3 |0x355 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg4` |Machine indirect register alias 4 |0x356 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg5` |Machine indirect register alias 5 |0x357 |MRW |XLEN |`mireg6` |Machine indirect register alias 6 |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: The CSRs listed in the table above provide a window for accessing register state indirectly.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: The value of `miselect` determines which register is accessed upon read or write of each of the machine indirect alias CSRs (`mireg*`). `miselect` value ranges are allocated to dependent extensions, which specify the register state accessible via each `miregi` register, for each `miselect` value. `miselect` is a WARL register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: The `miselect` register implements at least enough bits to support all implemented `miselect` values (corresponding to the implemented extensions that utilize `miselect`/`mireg*` to indirectly access register state).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: The `miselect` register may be read-only zero if there are no extensions implemented that utilize it.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `miselect` with the most-significant bit set (bit XLEN - 1 = 1) are designated only for custom use, presumably for accessing custom registers through the alias CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `miselect` with the most-significant bit clear are designated only for standard use and are reserved until allocated to a standard architecture extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: If XLEN is changed, the most-significant bit of `miselect` moves to the new position, retaining its value from before.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: The behavior upon accessing `mireg*` from M-mode, while `miselect` holds a value that is not implemented, is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: Attempts to access `mireg*` while `miselect` holds a number in an allocated and implemented range results in a specific behavior that, for each combination of `miselect` and `miregi`, is defined by the extension to which the `miselect` value is allocated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Machine-level CSRs

chunks.text: [width="100%",cols="15%,12%,12%,15%,46%",options="header",] | |*Number* |*Privilege* |*Width* |*Name* |*Description* |0x150 |SRW |XLEN |`siselect` |Supervisor indirect register select |0x151 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg` |Supervisor indirect register alias |0x152 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg2` |Supervisor indirect register alias 2 |0x153 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg3` |Supervisor indirect register alias 3 |0x155 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg4` |Supervisor indirect register alias 4 |0x156 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg5` |Supervisor indirect register alias 5 |0x157 |SRW |XLEN |`sireg6` |Supervisor indirect register alias 6 |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The CSRs in the table above are required if S-mode is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The `siselect` register will support the value range 0..0xFFF at a minimum. A future extension may define a value range outside of this minimum range.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Only if such an extension is implemented will `siselect` be required to support larger values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `siselect` with the most-significant bit set (bit XLEN - 1 = 1) are designated only for custom use, presumably for accessing custom registers through the alias CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `siselect` with the most-significant bit clear are designated only for standard use and are reserved until allocated to a standard architecture extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: If XLEN is changed, the most-significant bit of `siselect` moves to the new position, retaining its value from before.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The behavior upon accessing `sireg*` from M-mode or S-mode, while `siselect` holds a value that is not implemented at supervisor level, is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Otherwise, attempts to access `sireg*` from M-mode or S-mode while `siselect` holds a number in a standard-defined and implemented range result in specific behavior that, for each combination of `siselect` and `siregi`, is defined by the extension to which the `siselect` value is allocated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: [width="100%",cols="15%,12%,12%,15%,46%",options="header",] | |*Number* |*Privilege* |*Width* |*Name* |*Description* |0x250 |HRW |XLEN |`vsiselect` |Virtual supervisor indirect register select
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x251 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x252 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg2` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias 2
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x253 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg3` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias 3
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x255 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg4` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias 4
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x256 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg5` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias 5
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: |0x257 |HRW |XLEN |`vsireg6` |Virtual supervisor indirect register alias 6 |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The CSRs in the table above are required if the hypervisor extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: These VS CSRs all match supervisor CSRs, and substitute for those supervisor CSRs when executing in a virtual machine (in VS-mode or VU-mode).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The `vsiselect` register will support the value range 0..0xFFF at a minimum. A future extension may define a value range outside of this minimum range.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Only if such an extension is implemented will `vsiselect` be required to support larger values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `vsiselect` with the most-significant bit set (bit XLEN - 1 = 1) are designated only for custom use, presumably for accessing custom registers through the alias CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Values of `vsiselect` with the most-significant bit clear are designated only for standard use and are reserved until allocated to a standard architecture extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: If XLEN is changed, the most-significant bit of `vsiselect` moves to the new position, retaining its value from before.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: For alias CSRs `sireg*` and `vsireg*`, the hypervisor extension’s usual rules for when to raise a virtual-instruction exception (based on whether an instruction is HS-qualified) are not applicable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The rules given in this section for `sireg` and `vsireg` apply instead, unless overridden by the requirements specified in the section below, which take precedence over this section when extension Smstateen is also implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: A virtual-instruction exception is raised for attempts from VS-mode or VU-mode to directly access `vsiselect` or `vsireg*`, or attempts from VU-mode to access `siselect` or `sireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: The behavior upon accessing `vsireg*` from M-mode or HS-mode, or accessing `sireg*` (really `vsireg*`) from VS-mode, while `vsiselect` holds a value that is not implemented at HS level, is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Otherwise, while `vsiselect` holds a number in a standard-defined and implemented range, attempts to access `vsireg*` from a sufficiently privileged mode, or to access `sireg*` (really `vsireg*`) from VS-mode, result in specific behavior that, for each combination of `vsiselect` and `vsiregi`, is defined by the extension to which the `vsiselect` value is allocated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: Like `siselect` and `sireg*`, the widths of `vsiselect` and `vsireg*` are always the current XLEN rather than VSXLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Virtual Supervisor-level CSRs

chunks.text: If extension Smstateen is implemented together with Smcsrind, bit 60 of state-enable register `mstateen0` controls access to `siselect`, `sireg*`, `vsiselect`, and `vsireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`[60]=0, an attempt to access one of these CSRs from a privilege mode less privileged than M-mode results in an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: As always, the state-enable CSRs do not affect the accessibility of any state when in M-mode, only in less privileged modes.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: For more explanation, see the documentation for extension Smstateen in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: Other extensions may specify that certain mstateen bits control access to registers accessed indirectly through `siselect` + `sireg*`, and/or `vsiselect` + `vsireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: If the hypervisor extension is implemented, the same bit is defined also in hypervisor CSR `hstateen0`, but controls access to only `siselect` and `sireg*` (really `vsiselect` and `vsireg*`), which is the state potentially accessible to a virtual machine executing in VS or VU-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: When `hstateen0`[60]=0 and `mstateen0`[60]=1, all attempts from VS or VU-mode to access `siselect` or `sireg*` raise a virtual-instruction exception, not an illegal-instruction exception, regardless of the value of `vsiselect` or any other mstateen bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: Extension Ssstateen is defined as the supervisor-level view of Smstateen.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: Therefore, the combination of Sscsrind and Ssstateen incorporates the bit defined above for `hstateen0` but not that for `mstateen0`, since machine-level CSRs are not visible to supervisor level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smcsrind/Sscsrind" Indirect CSR Access, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Access control by the state-enable CSRs

chunks.text: Control flow transfers refer to jump instructions (including function calls and returns), taken branch instructions, traps, and trap returns.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Profiling tools, such as Linux perf, collect control transfer history when sampling software execution, thereby enabling tools, like AutoFDO, to identify hot paths for optimization.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Recorded transfers are inserted at the write pointer, which is then incremented, while older recorded transfers may be overwritten once the buffer is full.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The CTR buffer is accessible through an indirect CSR interface, such that software can specify which logical entry in the buffer it wishes to read or write.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The machine-level extension, *Smctr*, encompasses all newly added Control Status Registers (CSRs), instructions, and behavior modifications for a hart across all privilege levels.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Smctr and Ssctr depend on both the implementation of S-mode and the Sscsrind extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The `mctrctl` register is a 64-bit read/write register that enables and configures the CTR capability.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |RASEMU |Enables RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |MTE |Enables recording of traps to M-mode when M=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |STE |Enables recording of traps to S-mode when S=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |BPFRZ |Set `sctrstatus`.FROZEN on a breakpoint exception that traps to M-mode or S-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |LCOFIFRZ |Set `sctrstatus`.FROZEN on local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) that traps to M-mode or S-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |EXCINH |Inhibit recording of exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |INTRINH |Inhibit recording of interrupts.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |TRETINH |Inhibit recording of trap returns.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |NTBREN |Enable recording of not-taken branches.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |TKBRINH |Inhibit recording of taken branches.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |INDCALLINH |Inhibit recording of indirect calls.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |DIRCALLINH |Inhibit recording of direct calls.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |INDJMPINH |Inhibit recording of indirect jumps (without linkage).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |DIRJMPINH |Inhibit recording of direct jumps (without linkage).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |CORSWAPINH |Inhibit recording of co-routine swaps.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |RETINH |Inhibit recording of function returns.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |INDLJMPINH |Inhibit recording of other indirect jumps (with linkage).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: |DIRLJMPINH |Inhibit recording of other direct jumps (with linkage).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: The value 0 must correspond to standard behavior.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: -- All fields are optional except for M, S, U, and BPFRZ.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: All unimplemented fields are read-only 0, while all implemented fields are writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, LCOFIFRZ must be writable. --
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`)

chunks.text: The `sctrctl` register provides supervisor mode access to a subset of `mctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`sctrctl`)

chunks.text: Bits 2 and 9 in `sctrctl` are read-only 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`sctrctl`)

chunks.text: As a result, the M and MTE fields in `mctrctl` are not accessible through `sctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`sctrctl`)

chunks.text: All other `mctrctl` fields are accessible through `sctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`sctrctl`)

chunks.text: If the H extension is implemented, the `vsctrctl` register is a 64-bit read/write register that is VS-mode's version of supervisor register `sctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Virtual Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`vsctrctl`)

chunks.text: When V=1, `vsctrctl` substitutes for the usual `sctrctl`, so instructions that normally read or modify `sctrctl` actually access `vsctrctl` instead.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Virtual Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`vsctrctl`)

chunks.text: The optional fields implemented in `vsctrctl` should match those implemented in `sctrctl`. |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Virtual Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`vsctrctl`)

chunks.text: [NOTE] Unlike the CTR status register or the CTR entry registers, the CTR control register has a VS-mode version.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Virtual Supervisor Control Transfer Records Control Register (`vsctrctl`)

chunks.text: The 32-bit `sctrdepth` register specifies the depth of the CTR buffer.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: The depth of the CTR buffer dictates the number of entries to which the hardware records transfers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: For a depth of N, the hardware records transfers to entries 0..N-1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: All read as '0' and are read-only when the selected entry is in the range N to 255.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: When the depth is increased, the newly accessible entries contain unspecified but legal values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: It is implementation-specific which DEPTH value(s) are supported. |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: Attempts to access `sctrdepth` from VS-mode or VU-mode raise a virtual-instruction exception, unless CTR state enable access restrictions apply.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: [NOTE] It is expected that operating systems (OSs) will access `sctrdepth` only at boot, to select the maximum supported depth value.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: More frequent accesses may result in reduced performance in virtualization scenarios, as a result of traps from VS-mode incurred.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: There may be scenarios where software chooses to operate on only a subset of the entries, to reduce overhead.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: In such cases tools may choose to read only the lower entries, and OSs may choose to save/restore only on the lower entries while using SCTRCLR to clear the others.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: The value in configurable depth lies in supporting VM migration.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: It is expected that a platform spec may specify that one or more CTR depth values must be supported. A hypervisor may wish to restrict guests to using one of these required depths, in order to ensure that such guests can be migrated to any system that complies with the platform spec.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: The trapping behavior specified for VS-mode accesses to `sctrdepth` ensures that the hypervisor can impose such restrictions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Depth Register (`sctrdepth`)

chunks.text: The 32-bit `sctrstatus` register grants access to CTR status information and is updated by the hardware whenever CTR is active. CTR is active when the current privilege mode is enabled for recording and CTR is not frozen.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: For a given CTR depth (where depth = 2^(DEPTH+4)^), WRPTR wraps to 0 on an increment when the value matches depth-1, and to depth-1 on a decrement when the value is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: On depth changes, WRPTR holds an unspecified but legal value. |FROZEN |Inhibit transfer recording.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Undefined bits in `sctrstatus` are WPRI.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Status fields may be added by future extensions, and software should ignore but preserve any fields that it does not recognize.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: [NOTE] Logical entry 0, accessed via `sireg*` when `siselect`=0x200, is always the physical buffer entry preceding the WRPTR entry.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: More generally, the physical buffer entry Y associated with logical entry X (X < depth) can be determined using the formula Y = (WRPTR - X - 1) % depth, where depth = 2^(DEPTH+4)^.
