chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Software may wish to ensure that CTR is inactive before performing a read-modify-write, by ensuring that either `sctrstatus`.FROZEN=1, or that the current privilege mode is not enabled for recording.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: When restoring CTR state, `sctrstatus` should be written before CTR entry state is restored.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: [NOTE] Exposing the WRPTR provides a more efficient means for synthesizing CTR entries.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: If a qualified control transfer is emulated, the emulator can simply increment the WRPTR, then write the synthesized record to logical entry 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: If a qualified function return is emulated while RASEMU=1, the emulator can clear `ctrsource`.V for logical entry 0, then decrement the WRPTR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Exposing the WRPTR may also allow support for Linux perf's https://lwn.net/Articles/802821[[.underline]stack stitching] capability.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: If, in the future, it becomes desirable to remove this dependency, an extension could add `mctrdepth` and `mctrstatus` CSRs that reflect the same state as `sctrdepth` and `sctrstatus`, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Further, such an extension should make CTR entries accessible via `miselect`/`mireg*`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Machine Control Transfer Records Control Register (`mctrctl`) > Supervisor Control Transfer Records Status Register (`sctrstatus`)

chunks.text: Control transfer records are stored in a CTR buffer, such that each buffer entry stores information about a single transfer.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: The CTR buffer entries are logically accessed via the indirect register access mechanism defined by the Sscsrind extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: The `siselect` index range 0x200 through 0x2FF is reserved for CTR logical entries 0 through 255.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: When `siselect` holds a value in this range, `sireg` provides access to `ctrsource`, `sireg2` provides access to `ctrtarget`, and `sireg3` provides access to `ctrdata`. `sireg4`, `sireg5`, and `sireg6` are read-only 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: When `vsiselect` holds a value in 0x200..0x2FF, the `vsireg*` registers provide access to the same CTR entry register state as the analogous `sireg*` registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: There is not a separate set of entry registers for V=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: See for cases where CTR accesses from S-mode and VS-mode may be restricted.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers

chunks.text: The `ctrsource` register contains the source program counter, which is the `pc` of the recorded control transfer instruction, or the epc of the recorded trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: The valid (V) bit is set by the hardware when a transfer is recorded in the selected CTR buffer entry, and implies that data in `ctrsource`, `ctrtarget`, and `ctrdata` is valid for this entry.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: `ctrsource` is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a `pc`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (for recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: [NOTE] CTR entry registers are defined as MXLEN, despite the `xireg*` CSRs used to access them being XLEN, to ensure that entries recorded in RV64 are not truncated, as a result of CSR Width Modulation, on a transition to RV32.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Source Register (`ctrsource`)

chunks.text: The `ctrtarget` register contains the target (destination) program counter of the recorded transfer.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: For a not-taken branch, `ctrtarget` holds the PC of the next sequential instruction following the branch.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: The optional MISP bit is set by the hardware when the recorded transfer is an instruction whose target or taken/not-taken direction was mispredicted by the branch predictor. MISP is read-only 0 when not implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: `ctrtarget` is an MXLEN-bit WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual or physical addresses that can serve as a `pc`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: It need not be able to hold any invalid addresses; implementations may convert an invalid address into a valid address that the register is capable of holding.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: When XLEN < MXLEN, both explicit writes (by software) and implicit writes (by recorded transfers) will be zero-extended.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Target Register (`ctrtarget`)

chunks.text: The `ctrdata` register contains metadata for the recorded transfer.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: This register must be implemented, though all fields within it are optional.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: Unimplemented fields are read-only 0. `ctrdata` is a 64-bit register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: Implementations that do not support this field will report 0. |WARL
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: |CC[15:0] |Cycle Count, composed of the Cycle Count Exponent (CCE, in CC[15:12]) and Cycle Count Mantissa (CCM, in CC[11:0]).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: Undefined bits in `ctrdata` are WPRI.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: Undefined bits must be implemented as read-only 0, unless a is implemented and enabled.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Entry Registers > Control Transfer Record Metadata Register (`ctrdata`)

chunks.text: The SCTRCLR instruction performs the following operations:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Instructions > Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction

chunks.text: Any read of `ctrsource`, `ctrtarget`, or `ctrdata` that follows SCTRCLR, such that it precedes the next qualified control transfer, will return the value 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Instructions > Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction

chunks.text: Further, the first recorded transfer following SCTRCLR will have `ctrdata`.CCV=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Instructions > Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction

chunks.text: SCTRCLR raises an illegal-instruction exception in U-mode, and a virtual-instruction exception in VU-mode, unless CTR state enable access restrictions apply.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Instructions > Supervisor CTR Clear Instruction

chunks.text: When Smstateen is implemented, the `mstateen0`.CTR bit controls access to CTR register state from privilege modes less privileged than M-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`.CTR=1, accesses to CTR register state behave as described in and above, while SCTRCLR behaves as described in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`.CTR=0 and the privilege mode is less privileged than M-mode, the following operations raise an illegal-instruction exception:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: Attempts to access `sctrctl`, `vsctrctl`, `sctrdepth`, or `sctrstatus` * Attempts to access `sireg*` when `siselect` is in 0x200..0x2FF, or `vsireg*` when `vsiselect` is in 0x200..0x2FF * Execution of the SCTRCLR instruction
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`.CTR=0, qualified control transfers executed in privilege modes less privileged than M-mode will continue to implicitly update entry registers and `sctrstatus`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: If the H extension is implemented and `mstateen0`.CTR=1, the `hstateen0`.CTR bit controls access to supervisor CTR state when V=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: This state includes `sctrctl` (really `vsctrctl`), `sctrstatus`, and `sireg*` (really `vsireg*`) when `siselect` (really `vsiselect`) is in 0x200..0x2FF. `hstateen0`.CTR is read-only 0 when `mstateen0`.CTR=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`.CTR=1 and `hstateen0`.CTR=1, VS-mode accesses to supervisor CTR state behave as described in and above, while SCTRCLR behaves as described in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `mstateen0`.CTR=1 and `hstateen0`.CTR=0, both VS-mode accesses to supervisor CTR state and VS-mode execution of SCTRCLR raise a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: [NOTE] `sctrdepth` is not included in the above list of supervisor CTR state controlled by `hstateen0`.CTR since accesses to `sctrdepth` from VS-mode raise a virtual-instruction exception regardless of the value of `hstateen0`.CTR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: When `hstateen0`.CTR=0, qualified control transfers executed while V=1 will continue to implicitly update entry registers and `sctrstatus`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: [NOTE] See for how bit 60 in `mstateen0` and `hstateen0` can also restrict access to `sireg*`/`siselect` and `vsireg*`/`vsiselect` from privilege modes less privileged than M-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: [NOTE] Implementations that support Smctr/Ssctr but not Smstateen/Ssstateen may observe reduced performance.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: Without Smstateen/Ssstateen, a hypervisor is required to save/restore guest CTR state on every context switch.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > State Enable Access Control

chunks.text: CTR records qualified control transfers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: Control transfers are qualified if they meet the following criteria:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: The current privilege mode is enabled * The transfer type is not inhibited * `sctrstatus`.FROZEN is not set * The transfer completes/retires
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: Such qualified transfers update the at logical entry 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: As a result, older entries are pushed down the stack; the record previously in logical entry 0 moves to logical entry 1, the record in logical entry 1 moves to logical entry 2, and so on.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: If the CTR buffer is full, the oldest recorded entry (previously at entry depth-1) is lost.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: Recorded transfers will set the `ctrsource`.V bit to 1, and will update all implemented record fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior

chunks.text: Transfers that change the privilege mode are a special case.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: What is recorded, if anything, depends on whether the source privilege mode and/or target privilege mode are enabled for recording, and on the transfer type (trap or trap return).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Traps between enabled privilege modes are recorded as normal.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Traps from a disabled privilege mode to an enabled privilege mode are partially recorded, such that the `ctrsource`.PC is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Traps from an enabled mode to a disabled mode, known as external traps, are not recorded by default.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: See for how they can be recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Trap returns have similar treatment.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Trap returns between enabled privilege modes are recorded as normal.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Trap returns from an enabled mode back to a disabled mode are partially recorded, such that `ctrtarget`.PC is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Trap returns from a disabled mode to an enabled mode are not recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Recording in Debug Mode is always inhibited.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Transfers into and out of Debug Mode are never recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: The table below provides details on recording of privilege mode transitions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Standard dependencies on FROZEN and transfer type inhibits also apply, but are not covered by the table.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: |*Disabled* |Recorded, `ctrsource`.PC is 0. |Not recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions

chunks.text: Transitions between VS/VU-mode and M/HS-mode are unique in that they effect a change in the active CTR control register, and hence the CTR configuration.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > Virtualization Mode Transitions

chunks.text: What is recorded, if anything, on these virtualization mode transitions depends upon fields from both `[ms]ctrctl` and `vsctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > Virtualization Mode Transitions

chunks.text: External traps are traps from a privilege mode enabled for CTR recording to a privilege mode that is not enabled for CTR recording.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: By default external traps are not recorded, but privileged software running in the target mode of the trap can opt-in to allowing CTR to record external traps into that mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: The `xctrctl`.xTE bits allow M-mode, S-mode, and VS-mode to opt-in separately.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: External trap recording depends not only on the target mode, but on any intervening modes, which are modes that are more privileged than the source mode but less privileged than the target mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: Not only must the external trap enable bit for the target mode be set, but the external trap enable bit(s) for any intervening modes must also be set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: See the table below for details.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: [NOTE] Requiring intervening modes to be enabled for external traps simplifies software management of CTR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: Consider a scenario where S-mode software is configuring CTR for U-mode contexts A and B, such that external traps (to any mode) are enabled for A but not for B.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: When switching between the two contexts, S-mode can simply toggle `sctrctl`.STE, rather than requiring a trap to M-mode to additionally toggle `mctrctl`.MTE.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: This method does not provide the flexibility to record external traps to a more privileged mode but not to all intervening mode(s).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: In records for external traps, the `ctrtarget`.PC is 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: Thus, when external traps are enabled, both external interrupts and external exceptions are recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: STE allows recording of traps from U-mode to S-mode as well as from VS/VU-mode to HS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: The hypervisor can flip `sctrctl`.STE before entering a guest if it wants different behavior for U-to-S vs VS/VU-to-HS.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: If external trap recording is implemented, `mctrctl`.MTE and `sctrctl`.STE must be implemented, while `vsctrctl`.STE must be implemented if the H extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Privilege Mode Transitions > External Traps

chunks.text: By setting transfer type filter bits, software can opt out of recording select transfer types, or opt into recording non-default operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: All transfer type filter bits are optional.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: Non-default operations require opt-in rather than opt-out.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: For completeness, the definitions are reproduced below.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: Encodings 8 through 15 refer to various encodings of jump instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: The types are distinguished as described below.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: If implementation of any transfer type filter bit results in reduced software performance, perhaps due to additional retirement restrictions, it is strongly recommended that this reduced performance apply only when the bit is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: Alternatively, support for the bit may be omitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: Maintaining software performance for the default CTR configuration, when all transfer type bits are cleared, is recommended.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Transfer Type Filtering

chunks.text: The `ctrdata` register may optionally include a count of CPU cycles elapsed since the prior CTR record.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The elapsed cycle count value is represented by the CC field, which has a 12-bit mantissa component (Cycle Count Mantissa, or CCM) and a 4-bit exponent component (Cycle Count Exponent, or CCE).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The elapsed cycle counter (CtrCycleCounter) increments at the same rate as the `mcycle` counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: Only cycles while CTR is active are counted, where active implies that the current privilege mode is enabled for recording and CTR is not frozen.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The CC field is encoded such that CCE holds 0 if the CtrCycleCounter value is less than 4096, otherwise it holds the index of the most significant one bit in the CtrCycleCounter value, minus 11. CCM holds CtrCycleCounter bits CCE+10:CCE-1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The elapsed cycle count can then be calculated by software using the following formula:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: [subs="specialchars,quotes"] ---- if (CCE0): return CCM else: return (2^12^ + CCM) << CCE-1 endif ----
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The CtrCycleCounter is reset on writes to `xctrctl`, and on execution of SCTRCLR, to ensure that any accumulated cycle counts do not persist across a context switch.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: An implementation that supports cycle counting must implement CCV and all CCM bits, but may implement 0..4 exponent bits in CCE.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: Unimplemented CCE bits are read-only 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: For implementations that support transfer type filtering, it is recommended to implement at least 3 exponent bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The size of the CtrCycleCounter required to support each CCE width is given in the table below.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: When CCE>1, the granularity of the reported cycle count is reduced.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: As a result, the reported value represents an undercount of elapsed cycles for most cases (when the unreported bits are non-zero).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: On average, the undercount will be (2^CCE-1^-1)/2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: Software can reduce the average undercount to 0 by adding (2^CCE-1^-1)/2 to each computed cycle count value when CCE>1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: Though this compressed method of representation results in some imprecision for larger cycle count values, it produces meaningful area savings, reducing storage per entry from 27 bits to 16.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The CC value saturates when all implemented bits in CCM and CCE are 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The CC value is valid only when the Cycle Count Valid (CCV) bit is set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: If CCV=0, the CC value might not hold the correct count of elapsed active cycles since the last recorded transfer.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: The next record will have CCV=0 after a write to `xctrctl`, or execution of SCTRCLR, since CtrCycleCounter is reset. CCV should additionally be cleared after any other implementation-specific scenarios where active cycles might not be counted in CtrCycleCounter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Cycle Counting

chunks.text: When the optional `xctrctl`.RASEMU bit is implemented and set to 1, transfer recording behavior is altered to emulate the behavior of a return-address stack (RAS).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: Indirect and direct calls are recorded as normal * Function returns pop the most recent call, by decrementing the WRPTR then invalidating the WRPTR entry (by setting ctrsource.V=0).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: As a result, logical entry 0 is invalidated and moves to logical entry depth-1, while logical entries 1..depth-1 move to 0..depth-2. * Co-routine swaps affect both a return and a call.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: [NOTE] Profiling tools often collect call stacks along with each sample.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: With RAS emulation, tools can ask CTR hardware to save call stacks even for unmodified code.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: CTR RAS emulation has limitations.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: The CTR buffer will contain only partial stacks in cases where the call stack depth was greater than the CTR depth, CTR recording was enabled at a lower point in the call stack than main(), or where the CTR buffer was cleared since main().
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: [NOTE] As described in , when CCV=1, the CC field provides the elapsed cycles since the prior CTR entry was recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: The implication is that returns that pop a call entry not only do not reset the cycle counter, but instead add the CC field from the popped entry to the counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: For simplicity, an implementation may opt to record CCV=0 for all calls, or those whose parent call was popped, when RASEMU=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > RAS (Return Address Stack) Emulation Mode

chunks.text: When `sctrstatus`.FROZEN=1, transfer recording is inhibited.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: This bit can be set by hardware, as described below, or by software.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: When `sctrctl`.LCOFIFRZ=1 and a local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) traps (as a result of an HPM counter overflow) to M-mode or to S-mode, `sctrstatus`.FROZEN is set by hardware.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: This inhibits CTR recording until software clears FROZEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: The LCOFI trap itself is not recorded. [NOTE] Freeze on LCOFI ensures that the execution path leading to the sampled instruction (`xepc`) is preserved, and that the local-counter-overflow interrupt (LCOFI) and associated Interrupt Service Routine (ISR) do not displace any recorded transfer history state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: It is the responsibility of the ISR to clear FROZEN before xRET, if continued control transfer recording is desired.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: LCOFI refers only to architectural traps directly caused by a local counter overflow.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: If a local-counter-overflow interrupt is recognized without a trap, FROZEN is not automatically set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: As a result, some or all CTR history may be overwritten while handling the LCOFI.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: Similarly, on a breakpoint exception that traps to M-mode or S-mode with `sctrctl`.BPFRZ=1, FROZEN is set by hardware.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: The breakpoint exception itself is not recorded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: [NOTE] Breakpoint exception refers to synchronous exceptions with a cause value of Breakpoint (3), regardless of source (ebreak, c.ebreak, Sdtrig); it does not include entry into Debug Mode, even in cores where this is implemented as an exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: If the H extension is implemented, freeze behavior for LCOFIs and breakpoint exceptions that trap to VS-mode is determined by the LCOFIFRZ and BPFRZ values, respectively, in `vsctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: This includes virtual LCOFIs pended by a hypervisor.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: When a guest uses the SBI Supervisor Software Events (SSE) extension, the LCOFI will trap to HS-mode, which will then invoke a registered VS-mode LCOFI handler routine.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: If `vsctrctl`.LCOFIFRZ=1, the HS-mode handler will need to emulate the freeze by setting `sctrstatus`.FROZEN=1 before invoking the registered handler routine.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Behavior > Freeze

chunks.text: Any custom CTR extension must be associated with a non-zero value within the designated custom bits in `xctrctl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Custom Extensions

chunks.text: When the custom bits hold a non-zero value that enables a custom extension, the extension may alter standard CTR behavior, and may define new custom status fields within `sctrstatus` or the CTR .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Custom Extensions

chunks.text: All custom status fields, and standard status fields whose behavior is altered by the custom extension, must revert to standard behavior when the custom bits hold zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Custom Extensions

chunks.text: This includes read-only 0 behavior for any bits undefined by any implemented standard extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smctr" Control Transfer Records Extension, Version 1.0 > CSRs > Custom Extensions

chunks.text: When the Smrnmi extension () is implemented, it enables invocation of the RNMI handler on a double trap in M-mode to handle the critical error.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smdbltrp" Double Trap Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Smrnmi extension is not implemented or if a double trap occurs during the RNMI handler's execution, this extension helps transition the hart to a critical error state and enables signaling the critical error to the platform.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smdbltrp" Double Trap Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The extension introduces a mechanism to enter Debug Mode instead of asserting a critical-error signal to the platform when the hart is in a critical error state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smdbltrp" Double Trap Extension, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Being able to access the memory of a process running at a high privileged execution mode, such as the Supervisor or Machine mode, from a lower privileged mode such as the User mode, introduces an obvious attack vector since it allows for an attacker to perform privilege escalation, and tamper with the code and/or data of that process. A less obvious attack vector exists when the reverse happens, in which case an attacker instead of tampering with code and/or data that belong to a high-privileged process, can tamper with the memory of an unprivileged / less-privileged process and trick the high-privileged process to use or execute it.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The first one prevents the OS from accessing the memory of an unprivileged process unless a specific code path is followed, and the second one prevents the OS from executing the memory of an unprivileged process at all times. RISC-V already includes support for the former through the ``sstatus.SUM`` bit, and for the latter by always denying supervisor execution of virtual memory pages marked with the U bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0

chunks.text: To address the threat model outlined in Section , this extension introduces the `RLB`, `MMWP`, and `MML` fields in the `mseccfg` CSR and their associated rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0 > Threat model > Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules

chunks.text: See for the detailed specification of these fields and the corresponding rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0 > Threat model > Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules

chunks.text: The physical memory protection rules when `mseccfg.MML` is set to 1 are summarized in the truth table below.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0 > Threat model > Smepmp Physical Memory Protection Rules

chunks.text: Since all fields defined in ``mseccfg`` as part of this extension are locked when set (``MMWP``/``MML``) or locked when cleared (``RLB``), software can't poll them for determining the presence of Smepmp.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smepmp" Extension for PMP Enhancements for memory access and execution prevention in Machine mode, Version 1.0 > Threat model > Smepmp software discovery

chunks.text: The base machine-level architecture supports only unresumable non-maskable interrupts (UNMIs), where the NMI jumps to a handler in machine mode, overwriting the current `mepc` and `mcause` register values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the hart had been executing machine-mode code in a trap handler, the previous values in `mepc` and `mcause` would not be recoverable and so execution is not generally resumable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Smrnmi extension adds support for resumable non-maskable interrupts (RNMIs) to RISC-V.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The extension adds four new CSRs (`mnepc`, `mncause`, `mnstatus`, and `mnscratch`) to hold the interrupted state, and one new instruction, MNRET, to resume from the RNMI handler.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The `rnmi` interrupt signals are inputs to the hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals

chunks.text: These interrupts have higher priority than any other interrupt or exception on the hart and cannot be disabled by software.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals

chunks.text: Specifically, they are not disabled by clearing the `mstatus`.MIE register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals

chunks.text: The RNMI interrupt trap handler address is implementation-defined.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Handler Addresses

chunks.text: RNMI also has an associated exception trap handler address, which is implementation defined.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Handler Addresses

chunks.text: This extension adds additional M-mode CSRs to enable a resumable non-maskable interrupt (RNMI).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The `mnscratch` CSR holds an MXLEN-bit read-write register which enables the RNMI trap handler to save and restore the context that was interrupted.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The `mnepc` CSR is an MXLEN-bit read-write register which on entry to the RNMI trap handler holds the PC of the instruction that took the interrupt.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The low bit of `mnepc` (`mnepc[0]`) is always zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: This masking occurs also for the implicit read by the MNRET instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: Though masked, `mnepc[1]` remains writable when IALIGN=32.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: `mnepc` is a *WARL* register that must be able to hold all valid virtual addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: It need not be capable of holding all possible invalid addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: Prior to writing `mnepc`, implementations may convert an invalid address into some other invalid address that `mnepc` is capable of holding.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The `mncause` CSR holds the reason for the RNMI.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: If the reason is an interrupt, bit MXLEN-1 is set to 1, and the RNMI cause is encoded in the least-significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: If the reason is an interrupt and RNMI causes are not supported, bit MXLEN-1 is set to 1, and zero is written to the least-significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: If the reason is an exception within M-mode that results in a double trap as specified in the Smdbltrp extension, bit MXLEN-1 is set to 0 and the least-significant bits are set to the cause code corresponding to the exception that precipitated the double trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The `mnstatus` CSR holds a two-bit field, MNPP, which on entry to the RNMI trap handler holds the privilege mode of the interrupted context, encoded in the same manner as `mstatus`.MPP.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: If the H extension is also implemented, `mnstatus` also holds a one-bit field, MNPV, which on entry to the RNMI trap handler holds the virtualization mode of the interrupted context, encoded in the same manner as `mstatus`.MPV.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: If the Zicfilp extension is implemented, `mnstatus` also holds the MNPELP field, which on entry to the RNMI trap handler holds the previous `ELP` state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: When an RNMI trap is taken, MNPELP is set to `ELP` and `ELP` is set to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: `mnstatus` also holds the NMIE bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: When NMIE=1, non-maskable interrupts are enabled.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: When NMIE=0, all interrupts are disabled.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: When NMIE=0, the hart behaves as though `mstatus`.MPRV were clear, regardless of the current setting of `mstatus`.MPRV.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: Upon reset, NMIE contains the value 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: Software can set NMIE to 1, but attempts to clear NMIE have no effect.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: For the purposes of the WFI instruction, NMIE is a global interrupt enable, meaning that the setting of NMIE does not affect the operation of the WFI instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: The other bits in `mnstatus` are reserved; software should write zeros and hardware implementations should return zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI CSRs

chunks.text: MNRET is an M-mode-only instruction that uses the values in `mnepc` and `mnstatus` to return to the program counter, privilege mode, and virtualization mode of the interrupted context.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > MNRET Instruction

chunks.text: This instruction also sets `mnstatus`.NMIE.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > MNRET Instruction

chunks.text: If MNRET changes the privilege mode to a mode less privileged than M, it also sets `mstatus`.MPRV to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > MNRET Instruction

chunks.text: When an RNMI interrupt is detected, the interrupted PC is written to the `mnepc` CSR, the type of RNMI to the `mncause` CSR, and the privilege mode of the interrupted context to the `mnstatus` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Operation

chunks.text: The `mnstatus`.NMIE bit is cleared, masking all interrupts.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Operation

chunks.text: The hart then enters machine-mode and jumps to the RNMI trap handler address.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Operation

chunks.text: The RNMI handler can resume original execution using the new MNRET instruction, which restores the PC from `mnepc`, the privilege mode from `mnstatus`, and also sets `mnstatus`.NMIE, which re-enables interrupts.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Operation

chunks.text: If the hart encounters an exception while executing in M-mode with the `mnstatus`.NMIE bit clear, the actions taken are the same as if the exception had occurred while `mnstatus`.NMIE were set, except that the program counter is set to the RNMI exception trap handler address.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smrnmi" Extension for Resumable Non-Maskable Interrupts, Version 1.0 > RNMI Interrupt Signals > RNMI Operation

chunks.text: The problem occurs when an extension adds processor state -- usually explicit registers, but possibly other forms of state -- that the main OS or hypervisor is unaware of (and hence won't context-switch) but that can be modified/written by one user thread or guest OS and perceived/examined/read by another.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The `f` registers of the RISC-V floating-point extensions and the `v` registers of the vector extension would similarly be potential covert channels between user threads, except for the existence of the FS and VS fields in the `sstatus` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Even if an OS is unaware of, say, the vector extension and its `v` registers, access to those registers is blocked when the VS field is initialized to zero, either at machine level or by the OS itself initializing `sstatus`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Obviously, one way to prevent the use of new user-level CSRs as covert channels would be to add to `mstatus` or `sstatus` an "XS" field for each relevant extension, paralleling the V extension's VS field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: However, this is not considered a general solution to the problem due to the number of potential future extensions that may add small amounts of state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Even with a 64-bit `sstatus` (necessitating adding `sstatush` for RV32), it is not certain there are enough remaining bits in `sstatus` to accommodate all future user-level extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: In any event, there is no need to strain `sstatus` (and add `sstatush`) for this purpose.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The "enable" flags that are needed to plug covert channels are not generally expected to require swapping on context switches of user threads, making them a less-than-compelling candidate for inclusion in `sstatus`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Smstateen extension specification comprises the mstateen*, sstateen*, and hstateen* CSRs and their functionality.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The Ssstateen extension specification comprises only the sstateen* and hstateen* CSRs and their functionality.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For RV64 harts, this extension adds four new 64-bit CSRs at machine level: `mstateen0` (Machine State Enable 0), `mstateen1`, `mstateen2`, and `mstateen3`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: If supervisor mode is implemented, another four CSRs are defined at supervisor level: `sstateen0`, `sstateen1`, `sstateen2`, and `sstateen3`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: And if the hypervisor extension is implemented, another set of CSRs is added: `hstateen0`, `hstateen1`, `hstateen2`, and `hstateen3`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For RV32, there are CSR addresses for accessing the upper 32 bits of corresponding machine-level and hypervisor CSRs: `mstateen0h`, `mstateen1h`, `mstateen2h`, `mstateen3h`, `hstateen0h`, `hstateen1h`, `hstateen2h`, and `hstateen3h`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Each bit of a `stateen` CSR controls less-privileged access to an extension's state, for an extension that was not deemed "worthy" of a full XS field in `sstatus` like the FS and VS fields for the F and V extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The exact number four is an attempted compromise between providing too few bits on the one hand and going overboard with CSRs that will never be used on the other. A possible future doubling of the number of `stateen` CSRs is covered later.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: This is analogous to how the existing `counteren` CSRs control access to performance counter registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Just as with the `counteren` CSRs, when a `stateen` CSR prevents access to state by less-privileged levels, an attempt in one of those privilege modes to execute an instruction that would read or write the protected state raises an illegal-instruction exception, or, if executing in VS or VU mode and the circumstances for a virtual-instruction exception apply, raises a virtual-instruction exception instead of an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: When this extension is not implemented, all state added by an extension is accessible as defined by that extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: When a `stateen` CSR prevents access to state for a privilege mode, attempting to execute in that privilege mode an instruction that implicitly updates the state without reading it may or may not raise an illegal-instruction or virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Such cases must be disambiguated by being explicitly specified one way or the other.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: In some cases, the bits of the `stateen` CSRs will have a dual purpose as enables for the ISA extensions that introduce the controlled state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Each bit of a supervisor-level `sstateen` CSR controls user-level access (from U-mode or VU-mode) to an extension's state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The intention is to allocate the bits of `sstateen` CSRs starting at the least-significant end, bit 0, through to bit 31, and then on to the next-higher-numbered `sstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For every bit with a defined purpose in an `sstateen` CSR, the same bit is defined in the matching `mstateen` CSR to control access below machine level to the same state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The upper 32 bits of an `mstateen` CSR (or for RV32, the corresponding high-half CSR) control access to state that is inherently inaccessible to user level, so no corresponding enable bits in the supervisor-level `sstateen` CSR are applicable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The intention is to allocate bits for this purpose starting at the most-significant end, bit 63, through to bit 32, and then on to the next-higher `mstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: If the rate that bits are being allocated from the least-significant end for `sstateen` CSRs is sufficiently low, allocation from the most-significant end of `mstateen` CSRs may be allowed to encroach on the lower 32 bits before jumping to the next-higher `mstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: In that case, the bit positions of "encroaching" bits will remain forever read-only zeros in the matching `sstateen` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: With the hypervisor extension, the `hstateen` CSRs have identical encodings to the `mstateen` CSRs, except controlling accesses for a virtual machine (from VS and VU modes).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Each standard-defined bit of a `stateen` CSR is WARL and may be read-only zero or one, subject to the following conditions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Bits in any `stateen` CSR that are defined to control state that a hart doesn't implement are read-only zeros for that hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Likewise, all reserved bits not yet given a defined meaning are also read-only zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For every bit in an `mstateen` CSR that is zero (whether read-only zero or set to zero), the same bit appears as read-only zero in the matching `hstateen` and `sstateen` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For every bit in an `hstateen` CSR that is zero (whether read-only zero or set to zero), the same bit appears as read-only zero in `sstateen` when accessed in VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: A bit in a supervisor-level `sstateen` CSR cannot be read-only one unless the same bit is read-only one in the matching `mstateen` CSR and, if it exists, in the matching `hstateen` CSR. A bit in an `hstateen` CSR cannot be read-only one unless the same bit is read-only one in the matching `mstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: On reset, all writable `mstateen` bits are initialized by the hardware to zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: If machine-level software changes these values, it is responsible for initializing the corresponding writable bits of the `hstateen` and `sstateen` CSRs to zeros too.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Software at each privilege level should set its respective `stateen` CSRs to indicate the state it is prepared to allow less-privileged software to access.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: For each `mstateen` CSR, bit 63 is defined to control access to the matching `sstateen` and `hstateen` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Likewise, bit 63 of each `hstateen` correspondingly controls access to the matching `sstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: A hypervisor may need this control over accesses to the `sstateen` CSRs if it ever must emulate for a virtual machine an extension that is supposed to be affected by a bit in an `sstateen` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Even if such emulation is uncommon, it should not be excluded.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: That is, machine level needs control over accesses to the supervisor-level `sstateen` CSRs in order to emulate the `hstateen` CSRs, which have such control.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Bit 63 of each `mstateen` CSR may be read-only zero only if the hypervisor extension is not implemented and the matching supervisor-level `sstateen` CSR is all read-only zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: In that case, machine-level software should emulate attempts to access the affected `sstateen` CSR from S-mode, ignoring writes and returning zero for reads.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: Bit 63 of each `hstateen` CSR is always writable (not read-only).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions

chunks.text: The C bit controls access to any and all custom state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The C bit of these registers is not custom state itself; it is a standard field of a standard CSR, either `mstateen0`, `hstateen0`, or `sstateen0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The FCSR bit controls access to `fcsr` for the case when floating-point instructions operate on `x` registers instead of `f` registers as specified by the Zfinx and related extensions (Zdinx, etc.). [mstateen0fcsrroz]Whenever `misa.F` = 1, FCSR bit of `mstateen0` is read-only zero (and hence read-only zero in `hstateen0` and `sstateen0` too).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: For convenience, when the `stateen` CSRs are implemented and `misa.F` = 0, then if the FCSR bit of a controlling `stateen0` CSR is zero, all floating-point instructions cause an illegal-instruction exception (or virtual-instruction exception, if relevant), as though they all access `fcsr`, regardless of whether they really do.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The JVT bit controls access to the `jvt` CSR provided by the Zcmt extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The SE0 bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `hstateen0`, `hstateen0h`, and the `sstateen0` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The SE0 bit in `hstateen0` controls access to the `sstateen0` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The ENVCFG bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `henvcfg`, `henvcfgh`, and the `senvcfg` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The ENVCFG bit in `hstateen0` controls access to the `senvcfg` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The CSRIND bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `siselect`, `sireg*`, `vsiselect`, and the `vsireg*` CSRs provided by the Sscsrind extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The CSRIND bit in `hstateen0` controls access to the `siselect` and the `sireg*`, (really `vsiselect` and `vsireg*`) CSRs provided by the Sscsrind extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The IMSIC bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the IMSIC state, including CSRs `stopei` and `vstopei`, provided by the Ssaia extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The IMSIC bit in `hstateen0` controls access to the guest IMSIC state, including CSRs `stopei` (really `vstopei`), provided by the Ssaia extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The AIA bit in `mstateen0` controls access to all state introduced by the Ssaia extension and not controlled by either the CSRIND or the IMSIC bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The AIA bit in `hstateen0` controls access to all state introduced by the Ssaia extension and not controlled by either the CSRIND or the IMSIC bits of `hstateen0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The CONTEXT bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `scontext` and `hcontext` CSRs provided by the Sdtrig extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The CONTEXT bit in `hstateen0` controls access to the `scontext` CSR provided by the Sdtrig extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The P1P13 bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `hedelegh` introduced by Privileged Specification Version 1.13.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: The SRMCFG bit in `mstateen0` controls access to the `srmcfg` CSR introduced by the Ssqosid extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > State Enable 0 Registers

chunks.text: After the writable bits of the machine-level `mstateen` CSRs are initialized to zeros on reset, machine-level software can set bits in these registers to enable less-privileged access to the controlled state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: This may be either because machine-level software knows how to swap the state or, more likely, because machine-level software isn't swapping supervisor-level environments. (Recall that the main reason the `mstateen` CSRs must exist is so machine level can emulate the hypervisor extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: When machine level isn't emulating the hypervisor extension, it is likely there will be no need to keep any implemented `mstateen` bits zero.)
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: If machine level sets any writable `mstateen` bits to nonzero, it must initialize the matching `hstateen` CSRs, if they exist, by writing zeros to them.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: And if any `mstateen` bits that are set to one have matching bits in the `sstateen` CSRs, machine-level software must also initialize those `sstateen` CSRs by writing zeros to them.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: Ordinarily, machine-level software will want to set bit 63 of all `mstateen` CSRs, necessitating that it write zero to all `hstateen` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: Software should ensure that all writable bits of `sstateen` CSRs are initialized to zeros when an OS at supervisor level is first entered.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: For the `sstateen` CSRs whose access by a guest OS is permitted by bit 63 of the corresponding `hstateen` CSRs, a hypervisor must include the `sstateen` CSRs in the context it swaps for a guest OS.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: When it starts a new guest OS, it must ensure the writable bits of those `sstateen` CSRs are initialized to zeros, and it must emulate accesses to any other `sstateen` CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: If software at any privilege level does not support multiple contexts for less-privilege levels, then it may choose to maximize less-privileged access to all state by writing a value of all ones to the `stateen` CSRs at its level (the `mstateen` CSRs for machine level, the `sstateen` CSRs for an OS, and the `hstateen` CSRs for a hypervisor), without knowing all the state to which it is granting access.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Smstateen/Ssstateen" Extensions, Version 1.0 > State Enable Extensions > Usage

chunks.text: If the Ssccptr extension is implemented, then main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support hardware page-table reads.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ssccptr Extension for Main Memory Page-Table Reads, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The current Privileged specification defines mhpmevent CSRs to select and control event counting by the associated hpmcounter CSRs, but provides no standardization of any fields within these CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0

chunks.text: This extension serves to accomplish exactly this within the existing mhpmevent CSRs (and correspondingly avoids the unnecessary creation of whole new sets of CSRs - past just one new CSR).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The following bits are added to `mhpmevent`:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: [cols="^1,^1,^1,^1,^1,^1,^1,^1",stripes=even,options="header"] | |63 |62 |61 |60 |59 |58 |57 |56 |OF |MINH |SINH |UINH |VSINH |VUINH |WPRI |WPRI |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: [cols="15%,85%",options="header"] | | Field | Description | OF | Overflow status and interrupt disable bit that is set when counter overflows | MINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in M-mode is inhibited | SINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in S/HS-mode is inhibited | UINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in U-mode is inhibited | VSINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control

chunks.text: If set, then counting of events in VS-mode is inhibited | VUINH |
chunks.section: Preamble > "Sscofpmf" Extension for Count Overflow and Mode-Based Filtering, Version 1.0 > Count Overflow Control
