chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: All harts in a system must employ the same PTE-update scheme as each other.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Any level of PTE may be a leaf PTE, so in addition to 4 KiB pages, Sv32 supports 4 MiB megapages. A megapage must be virtually and physically aligned to a 4 MiB boundary; a page-fault exception is raised if the physical address is insufficiently aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Until their use is defined by a standard extension, they must be cleared by software for forward compatibility.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: For implementations with both page-based virtual memory and the "A" standard extension, the LR/SC reservation set must lie completely within a single base physical page (i.e., a naturally aligned 4 KiB physical-memory region).
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: On some implementations, misaligned loads, stores, and instruction fetches may also be decomposed into multiple accesses, some of which may succeed before a page-fault exception occurs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: In particular, a portion of a misaligned store that passes the exception check may become visible, even if another portion fails the exception check.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Let a be ``satp``.ppn×PAGESIZE, and let i=LEVELS-1. (For Sv32, PAGESIZE=2^12^ and LEVELS=2.) The `satp` register must be active, i.e., the effective privilege mode must be S-mode or U-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Let pte be the value of the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE. (For Sv32, PTESIZE=4.)
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If accessing pte violates a PMA or PMP check, raise an access-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If pte.v=0, or if pte.r=0 and pte.w=1, or if any bits or encodings that are reserved for future standard use are set within pte, stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If pte.r=1 or pte.x=1, go to step 5.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If i<0, stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If i>0 and pte.ppn[i-1:0] != 0, this is a misaligned superpage; stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Determine if the requested memory access is allowed by the pte.u bit, given the current privilege mode and the value of the SUM and MXR fields of the *mstatus* register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If not, stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Determine if the requested memory access is allowed by the pte.r, pte.w, and pte.x bits, given the Shadow Stack Memory Protection rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If not, stop and raise an access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Determine if the requested memory access is allowed by the pte.r, pte.w, and pte.x bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If not, stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If pte.a=0, or if the original memory access is a store and pte.d=0:
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If the Svade extension is implemented, stop and raise a page-fault exception corresponding to the original access type. *
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If a store to the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE would violate a PMA or PMP check, raise an access-fault exception corresponding to the original access type. * Perform the following steps atomically: ** Compare pte to the value of the PTE at address a+va.vpn[i]×PTESIZE. **
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If the values match, set pte.a to 1 and, if the original memory access is a store, also set pte.d to 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If the comparison fails, return to step 2.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: If i>0, then this is a superpage translation and pa.ppn[i-1:0] = va.vpn[i-1:0]. * pa.ppn[LEVELS-1:i] = pte.ppn[LEVELS-1:i].
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: The results of implicit address-translation reads in step 2 may be held in a read-only, incoherent address-translation cache but not shared with other harts.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: The address-translation cache may hold an arbitrary number of entries, including an arbitrary number of entries for the same address and ASID.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Entries in the address-translation cache may then satisfy subsequent step 2 reads if the ASID associated with the entry matches the ASID loaded in step 0 or if the entry is associated with a global mapping.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: To ensure that implicit reads observe writes to the same memory locations, an SFENCE.VMA instruction must be executed after the writes to flush the relevant cached translations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: The address-translation cache cannot be used in step 9; accessed and dirty bits may only be updated in memory directly.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Implementations may also execute the address-translation algorithm speculatively at any time, for any virtual address, as long as `satp` is active (as defined in ).
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: Speculative executions of the address-translation algorithm behave as non-speculative executions of the algorithm do, except that they must not set the dirty bit for a PTE, they must not trigger an exception, and they must not create address-translation cache entries if those entries would have been invalidated by any SFENCE.VMA instruction executed by the hart since the speculative execution of the algorithm began.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv32: Page-Based 32-bit Virtual-Memory Systems > Virtual Address Translation Process

chunks.text: This section describes a simple paged virtual-memory system for SXLEN=64, which supports 39-bit virtual address spaces.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System

chunks.text: Sv39 implementations support a 39-bit virtual address space, divided into 4 KiB pages.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Instruction fetch addresses and load and store effective addresses, which are 64 bits, must have bits 63–39 all equal to bit 38, or else a page-fault exception will occur.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: The 27-bit VPN is translated into a 44-bit PPN via a three-level page table, while the 12-bit page offset is untranslated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Sv39 page tables contain 2^9^ page table entries (PTEs), eight bytes each. A page table is exactly the size of a page and must always be aligned to a page boundary.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: The physical page number of the root page table is stored in the `satp` register's PPN field.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: If Svnapot is not implemented, bit 63 remains reserved and must be zeroed by software for forward compatibility, or else a page-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: If Svpbmt is not implemented, bits 62-61 remain reserved and must be zeroed by software for forward compatibility, or else a page-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Bits 60-54 are reserved for future standard use and, until their use is defined by some standard extension, must be zeroed by software for forward compatibility.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: If any of these bits are set, a page-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Any level of PTE may be a leaf PTE, so in addition to 4 KiB pages, Sv39 supports 2 MiB megapages and 1 GiB gigapages, each of which must be virtually and physically aligned to a boundary equal to its size. A page-fault exception is raised if the physical address is insufficiently aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv39: Page-Based 39-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: This section describes a simple paged virtual-memory system for SXLEN=64, which supports 48-bit virtual address spaces.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System

chunks.text: Implementations that support Sv48 must also support Sv39.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System

chunks.text: Sv48 implementations support a 48-bit virtual address space, divided into 4 KiB pages.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Instruction fetch addresses and load and store effective addresses, which are 64 bits, must have bits 63–48 all equal to bit 47, or else a page-fault exception will occur.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: The 36-bit VPN is translated into a 44-bit PPN via a four-level page table, while the 12-bit page offset is untranslated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Any level of PTE may be a leaf PTE, so in addition to 4 KiB pages, Sv48 supports 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, and 512 GiB terapages, each of which must be virtually and physically aligned to a boundary equal to its size. A page-fault exception is raised if the physical address is insufficiently aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv48: Page-Based 48-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: This section describes a simple paged virtual-memory system designed for RV64 systems, which supports 57-bit virtual address spaces.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System

chunks.text: Implementations that support Sv57 must also support Sv48.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System

chunks.text: Sv57 implementations support a 57-bit virtual address space, divided into 4 KiB pages.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Instruction fetch addresses and load and store effective addresses, which are 64 bits, must have bits 63–57 all equal to bit 56, or else a page-fault exception will occur.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: The 45-bit VPN is translated into a 44-bit PPN via a five-level page table, while the 12-bit page offset is untranslated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: Any level of PTE may be a leaf PTE, so in addition to 4 KiB pages, Sv57 supports 2 MiB megapages, 1 GiB gigapages, 512 GiB terapages, and 256 TiB petapages, each of which must be virtually and physically aligned to a boundary equal to its size. A page-fault exception is raised if the physical address is insufficiently aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Supervisor-Level ISA, Version 1.13 > Sv57: Page-Based 57-bit Virtual-Memory System > Addressing and Memory Protection

chunks.text: The Svadu extension adds support and CSR controls for hardware updating of PTE A/D bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svadu" Extension for Hardware Updating of A/D Bits, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Svadu extension is implemented, the `menvcfg`.ADUE field is writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svadu" Extension for Hardware Updating of A/D Bits, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the hypervisor extension is additionally implemented, the `henvcfg`.ADUE field is also writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svadu" Extension for Hardware Updating of A/D Bits, Version 1.0

chunks.text: See and for the definitions of those fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svadu" Extension for Hardware Updating of A/D Bits, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When hardware updating of A/D bits is disabled, the Svade extension, which mandates exceptions when A/D bits need be set, instead takes effect.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svadu" Extension for Hardware Updating of A/D Bits, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When executed in order (but not necessarily consecutively) by a single hart, the sequence SFENCE.W.INVAL, SINVAL.VMA, and SFENCE.INVAL.IR has the same effect as a hypothetical SFENCE.VMA instruction in which:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the hypervisor extension is implemented, the Svinval extension also provides two additional instructions: HINVAL.VVMA and HINVAL.GVMA.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: SINVAL.VMA, HINVAL.VVMA, and HINVAL.GVMA require the same permissions and raise the same exceptions as SFENCE.VMA, HFENCE.VVMA, and HFENCE.GVMA, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: In particular, an attempt to execute any of these instructions in U-mode always raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: An attempt to execute SINVAL.VMA or HINVAL.GVMA in S-mode or HS-mode when `mstatus`.TVM=1 also raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: An attempt to execute HINVAL.VVMA or HINVAL.GVMA in VS-mode or VU-mode, or to execute SINVAL.VMA in VU-mode, raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When `hstatus`.VTVM=1, an attempt to execute SINVAL.VMA in VS-mode also raises a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Attempting to execute SFENCE.W.INVAL or SFENCE.INVAL.IR in U-mode raises an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Doing so in VU-mode raises a virtual-instruction exception. SFENCE.W.INVAL and SFENCE.INVAL.IR are unaffected by the `mstatus`.TVM and `hstatus`.VTVM fields and hence are always permitted in S-mode and VS-mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svinval" Extension for Fine-Grained Address-Translation Cache Invalidation, Version 1.0

chunks.text: In Sv39, Sv48, and Sv57, when a PTE has N=1, the PTE represents a translation that is part of a range of contiguous virtual-to-physical translations with the same values for PTE bits 5–0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svnapot" Extension for NAPOT Translation Contiguity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Such ranges must be of a naturally aligned power-of-2 (NAPOT) granularity larger than the base page size.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svnapot" Extension for NAPOT Translation Contiguity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the encoding in pte is reserved according to , then a page-fault exception must be raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svnapot" Extension for NAPOT Translation Contiguity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Implicit reads of NAPOT page table entries may create address-translation cache entries mapping a + j×PTESIZE to a copy of pte in which pte.ppn[i][pte.napotbits-1:0] is replaced by vpn[i][pte.napotbits-1:0], for any or all j such that j >> napotbits = vpn[i] >> napotbits, all for the address space identified in satp as loaded by step 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svnapot" Extension for NAPOT Translation Contiguity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the hypervisor extension is also implemented, Svnapot is also supported in G-stage translation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svnapot" Extension for NAPOT Translation Contiguity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Svpbmt extension depends on the Sv39 extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Implementations may override additional PMAs not explicitly listed in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Until their use is defined by a standard extension, they must be cleared by software for forward compatibility, or else a page-fault exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Until this value is defined by a standard extension, using this reserved value in a leaf PTE raises a page-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When PBMT settings override a main memory page into I/O or vice versa, memory accesses to such pages obey the memory ordering rules of the final effective attribute, as follows.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the underlying physical memory attribute for a page is I/O, and the page has PBMT=NC, then accesses to that page obey RVWMO.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the underlying physical memory attribute for a page is main memory, and the page has PBMT=IO, then accesses to that page obey strong channel 0 I/O ordering rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When Svpbmt is used with non-zero PBMT encodings, it is possible for multiple virtual aliases of the same physical page to exist simultaneously with different memory attributes.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: It is also possible for a U-mode or S-mode mapping through a PTE with Svpbmt enabled to observe different memory attributes for a given region of physical memory than a concurrent access to the same page performed by M-mode or when MODE=Bare.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: In such cases, the behaviors dictated by the attributes (including coherence, which is otherwise unaffected) may be violated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Accessing the same location using different cacheability attributes may cause loss of coherence.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When two-stage address translation is enabled within the H extension, the page-based memory types are also applied in two stages.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: First, if `hgatp`.MODE is not equal to zero, non-zero G-stage PTE PBMT bits override the attributes in the PMA to produce an intermediate set of attributes.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Second, if `vsatp`.MODE is not equal to zero, non-zero VS-stage PTE PBMT bits override the intermediate attributes to produce the final set of attributes used by accesses to the page in question.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svpbmt" Extension for Page-Based Memory Types, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Svrsw60t59b extension is implemented, then bits 60-59 of the page table entries (PTEs) are reserved for use by supervisor software and are ignored by the implementation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svrsw60t59b" Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Hypervisor (H) extension is also implemented, then bits 60-59 of the G-stage PTEs are reserved for use by supervisor software and are ignored by the implementation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svrsw60t59b" Extension for PTE Reserved-for-Software Bits 60-59, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When the Svvptc extension is implemented, explicit stores by a hart that update the Valid bit of leaf and/or non-leaf PTEs from 0 to 1 and are visible to a hart will eventually become visible within a bounded timeframe to subsequent implicit accesses by that hart to such PTEs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Svvptc" Extension for Obviating Memory-Management Instructions after Marking PTEs Valid, Version 1.0

chunks.text: RISC-V Pointer Masking (PM) is a feature that, when enabled, causes the CPU to ignore the upper bits of the effective address (these terms will be defined more precisely in the Background section).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: When an address is accessed, the tag stored in the masked bits can be compared against a range-based tag.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: HWASAN leverages tags in the upper bits of the address to identify memory errors such as use-after-free or buffer overflow errors.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: By storing a *pointer tag* in the upper bits of the address and checking it against a *memory tag* stored in a side table, it can identify whether a pointer is pointing to a valid location.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: If implemented in software, pointer masking still provides performance benefits since non-checked accesses do not need to transform the address before every memory access.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: We anticipate that future extensions may build on pointer masking to support this functionality in hardware.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: It is worth mentioning that while HWASAN is the primary use-case for the current pointer masking extension, a number of other hardware/software features may be implemented leveraging Pointer Masking.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction

chunks.text: For a virtual address to be valid, all bits in the unused portion of the address must be the same as the Most Significant Bit (MSB) of the used portion.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Definitions

chunks.text: For example, when page-based 48-bit virtual memory (Sv48) is used, load/store effective addresses, which are 64 bits, must have bits 63–48 all set to bit 47, or else a page-fault exception will occur.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Definitions

chunks.text: For physical addresses, validity means that bits XLEN-1 to PABITS are zero, where PABITS is the number of physical address bits supported by the processor.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Definitions

chunks.text: For virtual addresses, it replaces the upper PMLEN bits with the sign extension of the PMLEN+1st bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > The “Ignore” Transformation

chunks.text: Pointer masking with the same value of PMLEN always has the same effect for the same type of address (virtual or physical).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > The “Ignore” Transformation

chunks.text: shows an example of the pointer masking transformation on a virtual address when PM is enabled for RV64 under Sv57 (PMLEN=7).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Example

chunks.text: If the address was a physical address rather than a virtual address with Sv57, the transformed address with PMLEN=7 would be 0x1FFFFFF12345678.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Example

chunks.text: In particular, applying the above transformation is cheap if it covers only bits that are not used by **any** supported address translation mode (as it is equivalent to switching off validity checks).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Determining the Value of PMLEN

chunks.text: Masking NVBITS beyond those bits is more expensive as it requires ignoring them in the TLB tag, and even more expensive if the masked bits extend into the VBITS portion of the address (as it requires performing the actual sign extension).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Determining the Value of PMLEN

chunks.text: Similarly, when running in Bare or M mode, it is common for implementations to not use a particular number of bits at the top of the physical address range and fix them to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Determining the Value of PMLEN

chunks.text: Applying the ignore transformation to those bits is cheap as well, since it will result in a valid physical address with all the upper bits fixed to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Determining the Value of PMLEN

chunks.text: In such future standards, different supported values of PMLEN may be defined for each privilege mode (U/VU, S/HS, and M).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Determining the Value of PMLEN

chunks.text: Different privilege modes may have different pointer masking settings active simultaneously and the hardware will automatically apply the pointer masking settings of the currently active privilege mode. A privilege mode's pointer masking setting is configured by bits in configuration registers of the next-higher privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Pointer Masking and Privilege Modes

chunks.text: For other extensions, pointer masking applies to all explicit memory accesses by default.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: Future extensions may add specific language to indicate whether particular accesses are or are not included in pointer masking.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: In other words, the accessed bytes should be identical to the bytes that would be accessed if the pointer masking transformation was individually applied to every byte of the access without pointer masking.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: No pointer masking operations are applied when software reads/writes to CSRs, including those meant to hold addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: If software stores tagged addresses into such CSRs, data load or data store operations based on those addresses are subject to pointer masking only if they are explicit () and pointer masking is enabled for the privilege mode that performs the access.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: Pointer masking is also applied, when applicable, to the memory access address when matching address triggers in debug.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: However, when delivering an exception, the hardware applies pointer masking to any address written into `stval` if pointer masking is applicable to that address.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Memory Accesses Subject to Pointer Masking

chunks.text: Profiles and implementations may choose to support an arbitrary subset of these extensions and must define valid ranges for their corresponding values of PMLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Pointer Masking Extensions

chunks.text: See , , , and . * **Smnpm**: A machine-level extension that provides pointer masking for the next lower privilege mode (S/HS if S-mode is implemented, or U-mode otherwise).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Pointer Masking Extensions

chunks.text: In RV32, trying to enable pointer masking will result in an illegal WARL write and not update the pointer masking configuration bits (see , , , and for details).
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Pointer Masking Extensions

chunks.text: The same is the case on RV64 or larger systems when UXL/SXL/MXL is set to 1 for the corresponding privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Pointer Masking Extensions

chunks.text: As described in , the supported values of PMLEN may depend on the effective privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Number of Masked Bits

chunks.text: The current standard only defines PMLEN=XLEN-48 and PMLEN=XLEN-57, but this assumption may be relaxed in future extensions and profiles.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Number of Masked Bits

chunks.text: Trying to enable pointer masking in an unsupported scenario represents an illegal write to the corresponding pointer masking enable bit and follows WARL semantics.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Number of Masked Bits

chunks.text: Future profiles may choose to define certain combinations of privilege modes and supported values of PMLEN as mandatory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Pointer Masking Extensions, Version 1.0.0 > Introduction > Background > Number of Masked Bits

chunks.text: In addition, users may add their own custom extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction

chunks.text: Similarly, hardware vendors should aim to structure their offerings around standard profiles to increase the likelihood their designs will have mainstream software support.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction

chunks.text: A profile family may be updated no more than annually, and the release calendar year is treated as part of the profile family name.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Family

chunks.text: A profile may specify that certain conditions will cause a requested trap (such as an `ecall` made in the highest-supported privilege mode) or fatal trap to the enclosing execution environment.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

chunks.text: The profile does not specify the behavior of the enclosing execution environment in handling requested or fatal traps.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

chunks.text: A profile does not specify any invisible traps.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Privilege Mode

chunks.text: Implementations of the profile must provide these.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: The Optional category (also known as options) contains extensions that may be added as options, and which are expected to be generally supported as options by the software ecosystem for this profile.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: Users would expect that software claiming compatibility with a profile would make use of any available supported options, but as a bare minimum software should not report errors or warnings when supported options are present in a system.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: An optional extension may comprise many individually named and ratified extensions but a profile option requires all constituent extensions are present.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: In particular, unless explicitly listed as a profile option, individual extensions are not by themselves a profile option even when required as part of a profile option.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: All components of a ratified profile must themselves have been ratified.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: Platforms may provide a discovery mechanism to determine what optional extensions are present.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile ISA Features

chunks.text: RVI20U32 basic unprivileged instructions for RV32I - RVI20U64 basic unprivileged instructions for RV64I - RVA20U64, RVA20S64 64-bit application-processor profiles
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > Profile Naming Convention

chunks.text: The RISC-V International ISA extension ratification process ensures that all processor vendors have agreed to the specification of a standard extension if present.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Without proactive alignment through RVA profiles, RISC-V will be uncompetitive, as even if a particular vendor implements a certain feature, if other vendors do not, then binary distributions will not generally use that feature and all implementations will suffer.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: While certain features may be discoverable, and alternate code provided in case of presence/absence of a feature, the added cost to support such options is only justified for certain limited cases, and binary app markets will not support a wide range of optional features, particularly for the nascent RISC-V binary app ecosystems.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: To maintain alignment and increase RISC-V competitiveness over time, the mandatory set of extensions must increase over time in successive generations of RVA profile. (RVA profiles may eventually have to deprecate previously mandatory instructions, but that is unlikely in the near future.) Note that the RISC-V ISA will continue to evolve, regardless of whether a given software ecosystem settles on a certain generation of profile as the baseline for their ecosystem for many years or even decades.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Processor vendors and software toolchain providers will have varying development schedules, and providing an optional phase in a new extension's lifecycle provides some flexibility while maintaining overall alignment, and is particularly appropriate when hardware or software development for the extension is complex.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Denoting an extension as a development option signals to the community that development should be prioritized for such extensions as they will become mandatory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: The third kind of optional extension are expansion options, which are those that may have a large implementation cost but are not always needed in a particular platform, and which can be readily handled by discovery.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Some unprivileged extensions that may fall into this category are possible future matrix extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: The fourth kind of optional extensions are transitory options, where it is not clear if the extension will change to a mandatory, localized, or expansion option, or be possibly dropped over time.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Denoting an option as transitory signals to the community that this extension may be removed in a future profile, though the time scale may span many years.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Binary distributions of applications willing to invest in discovery can use an optional extension, and customers compiling their own applications can take advantage of the feature on a particular implementation, even when that system is mostly running binary distributions that ignore the new extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Introduction > Components of a Profile > RVA Profiles Rationale

chunks.text: Clarified that profile name can be used as ISA base string - Renamed Ssptead to Svade - Fixed Ssu64xl to make supporting UXL=64 mandatory - Added section listing new extension names in profiles document - Added new extension name Sscounterenw - Removed outdated text on Zicntr/Zihpm ratification plan
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: The RVA20U64 profile specifies the ISA features available to user-mode execution environments in 64-bit applications processors.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA20 Profiles > RVA20U64 Profile

chunks.text: The RVA20S64 profile specifies the ISA features available to a supervisor-mode execution environment in 64-bit applications processors. RVA20S64 is based on privileged architecture version 1.11.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA20 Profiles > RVA20S64 Profile

chunks.text: The RVA22U64 profile specifies the ISA features available to user-mode execution environments in 64-bit applications processors.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA22 Profiles > RVA22U64 Profile

chunks.text: Profile implementers should provide all of the instructions in a given algorithm suite as part of the Zkn or Zks supported options.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA22 Profiles > RVA22U64 Profile > RVA22U64 Optional Extensions

chunks.text: The RVA22S64 profile specifies the ISA features available to a supervisor-mode execution environment in 64-bit applications processors. RVA22S64 is based on privileged architecture version 1.12.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA22 Profiles > RVA22S64 Profile

chunks.text: The RVA23U64 profile specifies the ISA features available to user-mode execution environments in 64-bit applications processors.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA23 Profiles > RVA23U64 Profile

chunks.text: These are implied by presence of F. - Base counters and timers. - Hardware performance counters. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support instruction fetch, and any instruction fetches of naturally aligned power-of-2 sizes up to min(ILEN,XLEN) (i.e., 32 bits for RVA23) are atomic. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support RsrvEventual. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support all atomics in the Zaamo extension. - Misaligned loads and stores to main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must be supported. - Reservation sets are contiguous, naturally aligned, and a maximum of 64 bytes. - Pause hint. - Cache blocks must be 64 bytes in size, naturally aligned in the address space. - Cache-block management instructions. - Cache-block prefetch instructions. - Cache-Block Zero Instructions. - ext:zfhmin[] Half-precision floating-point. - Data-independent execution latency.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA23 Profiles > RVA23U64 Profile > RVA23U64 Mandatory Extensions

chunks.text: The RVA23S64 profile specifies the ISA features available to a supervisor-mode execution environment in 64-bit applications processors. RVA23S64 is based on privileged architecture version 1.13.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVA23 Profiles > RVA23S64 Profile

chunks.text: However, individual software ecosystems may build upon RVB profiles to produce a more targeted standard interface for a certain market.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles

chunks.text: The RVB23U64 profile specifies the ISA features available to user-mode execution environments in 64-bit RVB applications processors.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles > RVB23U64 Profile

chunks.text: These are implied by presence of F. - Base counters and timers. - Hardware performance counters. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support instruction fetch, and any instruction fetches of naturally aligned power-of-2 sizes up to min(ILEN,XLEN) (i.e., 32 bits for RVB23) are atomic. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support RsrvEventual. - Main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support all atomics in the Zaamo extension. - Misaligned loads and stores to main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must be supported. - Reservation sets are contiguous, naturally aligned, and a maximum of 64 bytes. - Pause hint. - Cache blocks must be 64 bytes in size, naturally aligned in the address space. - Cache-block management instructions. - Cache-block prefetch instructions. - Cache-block zero instructions. - Data-independent execution latency.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles > RVB23U64 Profile > RVB23U64 Mandatory Extensions

chunks.text: The RVB23S64 profile specifies the ISA features available to a supervisor-mode execution environment in 64-bit applications processors. RVB23S64 is based on privileged architecture version 1.13.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles > RVB23S64 Profile

chunks.text: When the hypervisor extension is implemented, the following are also mandatory:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles > RVB23S64 Profile > RVB23S64 Optional Extensions > Expansion Options

chunks.text: If the hypervisor extension is implemented and pointer masking (Ssnpm) is supported then `henvcfg.PMM` must support at minimum, settings PMLEN=0 and PMLEN=7.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVB23 Profiles > RVB23S64 Profile > RVB23S64 Optional Extensions > Expansion Options

chunks.text: Code using this profile can run in any privilege mode, and so requested and fatal traps may be horizontal traps into an execution environment running in the same privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVI20 Profiles

chunks.text: All are a fixed 32 bits in length.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: The base ISA has `IALIGN=32`, meaning that instructions must be aligned on a four-byte boundary in memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: An instruction-address-misaligned exception is generated on a taken branch or unconditional jump if the target address is not `IALIGN-bit` aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: This exception is reported on the branch or jump instruction, not on the target instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: No instruction-address-misaligned exception is generated for a conditional branch that is not taken.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: Except for the 5-bit immediates used in , immediates are always sign-extended, and are generally packed towards the leftmost available bits in the instruction and have been allocated to reduce hardware complexity.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: In particular, the sign bit for all immediates is always in bit 31 of the instruction to speed sign-extension circuitry.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Base Instruction Formats

chunks.text: The only difference between the S and B formats is that the 12-bit immediate field is used to encode branch offsets in multiples of 2 in the B format.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Immediate Encoding Variants

chunks.text: Similarly, the only difference between the U and J formats is that the 20-bit immediate is shifted left by 12 bits to form U immediates and by 1 bit to form J immediates.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Immediate Encoding Variants

chunks.text: The fields are labeled with the instruction bits used to construct their value.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Immediate Encoding Variants

chunks.text: Integer computational instructions are either encoded as register-immediate operations using the I-type format or as register-register operations using the R-type format.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions

chunks.text: No integer computational instructions cause arithmetic exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions

chunks.text: ADDI adds the sign-extended 12-bit immediate to register rs1.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: ANDI, ORI, XORI are logical operations that perform bitwise AND, OR, and XOR on register rs1 and the sign-extended 12-bit immediate and place the result in rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: Shifts by a constant are encoded as a specialization of the I-type format.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The operand to be shifted is in rs1, and the shift amount is encoded in the lower 5 bits of the I-immediate field.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The right-shift type is encoded in bit 30. SLLI is a logical left shift (zeros are shifted into the lower bits); SRLI is a logical right shift (zeros are shifted into the upper bits); and SRAI is an arithmetic right shift (the original sign bit is copied into the vacated upper bits).
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: LUI (load upper immediate) is used to build 32-bit constants and uses the U-type format. LUI places the 32-bit U-immediate value into the destination register rd, filling in the lowest 12 bits with zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: AUIPC (add upper immediate to `pc`) is used to build `pc`-relative addresses and uses the U-type format. AUIPC forms a 32-bit offset from the U-immediate, filling in the lowest 12 bits with zeros, adds this offset to the address of the AUIPC instruction, then places the result in register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: RV32I defines several arithmetic R-type operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: All operations read the rs1 and rs2 registers as source operands and write the result into register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: The funct7 and funct3 fields select the type of operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: ADD performs the addition of rs1 and rs2. SUB performs the subtraction of rs2 from rs1.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: Overflows are ignored and the low XLEN bits of results are written to the destination rd. SLT and SLTU perform signed and unsigned compares respectively, writing 1 to rd if rs1 < rs2, 0 otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: SLL, SRL, and SRA perform logical left, logical right, and arithmetic right shifts on the value in register rs1 by the shift amount held in the lower 5 bits of register rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Instructions

chunks.text: The NOP instruction does not change any architecturally visible state, except for advancing the `pc` and incrementing any applicable performance counters. NOP is encoded as ADDI x0, x0, 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > NOP Instruction

chunks.text: If an instruction access-fault or instruction page-fault exception occurs on the target of a jump or taken branch, the exception is reported on the target instruction, not on the jump or branch instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions

chunks.text: The jump and link (JAL) instruction uses the J-type format, where the J-immediate encodes a signed offset in multiples of 2 bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: The offset is sign-extended and added to the address of the jump instruction to form the jump target address.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: Plain unconditional jumps (assembler pseudoinstruction J) are encoded as a JAL with rd=`x0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: The target address is obtained by adding the sign-extended 12-bit I-immediate to the register rs1, then setting the least-significant bit of the result to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: Plain unconditional indirect jumps (assembler pseudoinstruction JR) are encoded as a JALR with rd=`x0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: Procedure returns in the standard calling convention (assembler pseudoinstruction RET) are encoded as a JALR with rd=`x0`, rs1=`x1`, and imm=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: The JAL and JALR instructions will generate an instruction-address-misaligned exception if the target address is not aligned to a four-byte boundary.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: For RISC-V, hints as to the instructions' usage are encoded implicitly via the register numbers used. A JAL instruction should push the return address onto a return-address stack (RAS) only when rd is `x1` or `x5`. JALR instructions should push/pop a RAS as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Unconditional Jumps

chunks.text: The 12-bit B-immediate encodes signed offsets in multiples of 2 bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: The offset is sign-extended and added to the address of the branch instruction to give the target address.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: Branch instructions compare two registers. BEQ and BNE take the branch if registers rs1 and rs2 are equal or unequal respectively. BLT and BLTU take the branch if rs1 is less than rs2, using signed and unsigned comparison respectively. BGE and BGEU take the branch if rs1 is greater than or equal to rs2, using signed and unsigned comparison respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: Software should be optimized such that the sequential code path is the most common path, with less-frequently taken code paths placed out of line.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: Software should also assume that backward branches will be predicted taken and forward branches as not taken, at least the first time they are encountered.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: Dynamic predictors should quickly learn any predictable branch behavior.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: Unlike some other architectures, the RISC-V jump (JAL with rd=`x0`) instruction should always be used for unconditional branches instead of a conditional branch instruction with an always-true condition. RISC-V jumps are also PC-relative and support a much wider offset range than branches, and will not pollute conditional-branch prediction tables.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: The conditional branch instructions will generate an instruction-address-misaligned exception if the target address is not aligned to a four-byte boundary and the branch condition evaluates to true.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: If the branch condition evaluates to false, the instruction-address-misaligned exception will not be raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Control Transfer Instructions > Conditional Branches

chunks.text: RV32I is a load-store architecture, where only load and store instructions access memory and arithmetic instructions only operate on CPU registers. RV32I provides a 32-bit address space that is byte-addressed.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Loads with a destination of `x0` must still raise any exceptions and cause any other side effects even though the load value is discarded.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Loads are encoded in the I-type format and stores are S-type.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The effective address is obtained by adding register rs1 to the sign-extended 12-bit offset.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The LW instruction loads a 32-bit value from memory into rd. LH loads a 16-bit value from memory, then sign-extends to 32-bits before storing in rd. LHU loads a 16-bit value from memory but then zero extends to 32-bits before storing in rd. LB and LBU are defined analogously for 8-bit values.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The SW, SH, and SB instructions store 32-bit, 16-bit, and 8-bit values from the low bits of register rs2 to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Regardless of EEI, loads and stores whose effective addresses are naturally aligned shall not raise an address-misaligned exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: An EEI may guarantee that misaligned loads and stores are fully supported, and so the software running inside the execution environment will never experience a contained or fatal address-misaligned trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: In this case, the misaligned loads and stores can be handled in hardware, or via an invisible trap into the execution environment implementation, or possibly a combination of hardware and invisible trap depending on address.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: An EEI may not guarantee misaligned loads and stores are handled invisibly.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: In this case, loads and stores that are not naturally aligned may either complete execution successfully or raise an exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The exception raised can be either an address-misaligned exception or an access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: When an EEI does not guarantee misaligned loads and stores are handled invisibly, the EEI must define if exceptions caused by address misalignment result in a contained trap (allowing software running inside the execution environment to handle the trap) or a fatal trap (terminating execution).
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Any combination of device input (I), device output (O), memory reads \(R), and memory writes (W) may be ordered with respect to any combination of the same.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: The EEI will define what I/O operations are possible, and in particular, which memory addresses when accessed by load and store instructions will be treated and ordered as device input and device output operations respectively rather than memory reads and writes.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: [float="center",align="center",cols="^1,^1,<3",options="header"] .Fence mode encoding | |fm field |Mnemonic suffix|Meaning |0000 |none |Normal Fence |1000 |.TSO |With `FENCE RW,RW`: exclude write-to-read ordering; otherwise: Reserved for future use. |other|other |Reserved for future use. |
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: The FENCE mode field fm defines the semantics of the FENCE instruction. A `FENCE` (with fm=`0000`) orders all memory operations in its predecessor set before all memory operations in its successor set.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: A `FENCE.TSO` instruction is encoded as a FENCE instruction with fm=`1000`, predecessor=`RW`, and successor=`RW`. `FENCE.TSO` orders all load operations in its predecessor set before all memory operations in its successor set, and all store operations in its predecessor set before all store operations in its successor set.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: The unused fields in the FENCE instructions--rs1 and rd--are reserved for finer-grain fences in future extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: For forward compatibility, base implementations shall ignore these fields, and standard software shall zero these fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: Base implementations shall treat all such reserved configurations as `FENCE` instructions (with fm=`0000`), and standard software shall use only non-reserved configurations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Memory Ordering Instructions

chunks.text: `SYSTEM` instructions are used to access system functionality that might require privileged access and are encoded using the I-type instruction format.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Environment Call and Breakpoints

chunks.text: These can be divided into two main classes: those that atomically read-modify-write control and status registers (CSRs), and all other potentially privileged instructions. CSR instructions are described in , and the base unprivileged instructions are described in the following section.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Environment Call and Breakpoints

chunks.text: These two instructions cause a precise requested trap to the supporting execution environment.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Environment Call and Breakpoints

chunks.text: Implementations are always allowed to ignore the encoded hints.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > HINT Instructions

chunks.text: Most RV32I HINTs are encoded as integer computational instructions with rd=`x0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > HINT Instructions

chunks.text: The other RV32I HINTs are encoded as FENCE instructions with a null predecessor or successor set and with fm=0.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > HINT Instructions

chunks.text: This chapter only outlines the differences between RV32E/RV64E and RV32I/RV64I, and so should be read after and .
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32E and RV64E Base Integer Instruction Sets, Version 2.0

chunks.text: This chapter presents only the differences with RV32I, so should be read in conjunction with the earlier chapter.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1

chunks.text: RV64I widens the integer registers and supported user address space to 64 bits (XLEN=64 in ).
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Register State

chunks.text: Additional instruction variants are provided to manipulate 32-bit values in RV64I, indicated by a 'W' suffix to the opcode.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions

chunks.text: ADDIW is an RV64I instruction that adds the sign-extended 12-bit immediate to register rs1 and produces the proper sign extension of a 32-bit result in rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: Shifts by a constant are encoded as a specialization of the I-type format using the same instruction opcode as RV32I.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The operand to be shifted is in rs1, and the shift amount is encoded in the lower 6 bits of the I-immediate field for RV64I.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The right-shift type is encoded in bit 30. SLLI is a logical left shift (zeros are shifted into the lower bits); SRLI is a logical right shift (zeros are shifted into the upper bits); and SRAI is an arithmetic right shift (the original sign bit is copied into the vacated upper bits).
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: LUI (load upper immediate) uses the same opcode as RV32I. LUI places the 32-bit U-immediate into register rd, filling in the lowest 12 bits with zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The 32-bit result is sign-extended to 64 bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: AUIPC (add upper immediate to `pc`) uses the same opcode as RV32I. AUIPC is used to build `pc`-relative addresses and uses the U-type format. AUIPC forms a 32-bit offset from the U-immediate, filling in the lowest 12 bits with zeros, sign-extends the result to 64 bits, adds it to the address of the AUIPC instruction, then places the result in register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: ADDW and SUBW are RV64I-only instructions that are defined analogously to ADD and SUB but operate on 32-bit values and produce signed 32-bit results.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: SLL, SRL, and SRA perform logical left, logical right, and arithmetic right shifts on the value in register rs1 by the shift amount held in register rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: In RV64I, only the low 6 bits of rs2 are considered for the shift amount.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: SLLW, SRLW, and SRAW are RV64I-only instructions that are analogously defined but operate on 32-bit values and sign-extend their 32-bit results to 64 bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: The shift amount is given by rs2[4:0].
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: RV64I extends the address space to 64 bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The LD instruction loads a 64-bit value from memory into register rd for RV64I.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The LW instruction loads a 32-bit value from memory and sign-extends this to 64 bits before storing it in register rd for RV64I.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The LWU instruction, on the other hand, zero-extends the 32-bit value from memory for RV64I. LH and LHU are defined analogously for 16-bit values, as are LB and LBU for 8-bit values.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The SD, SW, SH, and SB instructions store 64-bit, 32-bit, 16-bit, and 8-bit values from the low bits of register rs2 to memory respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV64I Base Integer Instruction Set, Version 2.1 > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Where possible, we have written this specification to be understandable by all, though we recognise that the motivations and references to algorithms or other specifications and standards may be unfamiliar to those who are not domain experts.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

chunks.text: We hope this aids people's understanding of which aspects of the specification are particularly relevant to them, and which they may (safely!) ignore or pass to a colleague.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

chunks.text: They should understand fairly obviously the motivations for the instructions we include, and be familiar with most of the algorithms and outside standards to which we refer.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

chunks.text: In particular, they should be aware of the literature around efficiently implementing AES and SM4 SBoxes in hardware.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Intended Audience

chunks.text: It is anticipated that the NIST Lightweight Cryptography contest and the NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography contest may be dealt with this way, depending on timescales.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

chunks.text: The standard will not try to anticipate new useful low-level operations which may be useful as building blocks for future cryptographic constructs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

chunks.text: Regarding side-channel countermeasures: Where relevant, proposed instructions must aim to remove the possibility of any timing side-channels.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Introduction > Policies

chunks.text: The entropy source extension defines the `seed` CSR at address `0x015`.

chunks.text: The `seed` CSR provides an interface to a NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] or BSI AIS-31 cite:[KiSc11] compliant physical Entropy Source (ES).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source

chunks.text: `seed` is an unprivileged CSR located at address `0x015`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The 32-bit contents of `seed` are as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR
Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Attempts to access the `seed` CSR using a read-only CSR-access instruction (`CSRRS`/`CSRRC` with rs1=`x0` or `CSRRSI`/`CSRRCI` with uimm=0) raise an illegal-instruction exception; any other CSR-access instruction may be used to access `seed`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The write value (in `rs1` or `uimm`) must be ignored by implementations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The purpose of the write is to signal polling and flushing.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Software normally uses the instruction `csrrw rd, seed, x0` to read the `seed` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The `seed` CSR is also access controlled by execution mode, and attempted read or write access will raise an illegal-instruction exception outside M mode unless access is explicitly granted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR
