chunks.text: The status bits `seed[31:30]` = `OPST` may be `ES16` (10), indicating successful polling, or one of three entropy polling failure statuses `BIST` (00), `WAIT` (01), or `DEAD` (11), discussed below.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Each returned `seed[15:0]` = `entropy` value represents unique randomness when `OPST`=`ES16` (`seed[31:30]` = `10`), even if its numerical value is the same as that of a previously polled `entropy` value.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The implementation requirements of `entropy` bits are defined in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: When `OPST` is not `ES16`, `entropy` must be set to 0.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: An implementation may safely set reserved and custom bits to zeros.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: For security reasons, the interface guarantees that secret `entropy` words are not made available multiple times.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Hence polling (reading) must also have the side effect of clearing (wipe-on-read) the `entropy` contents and changing the state to `WAIT` (unless there is `entropy` immediately available for `ES16`).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Other states (`BIST`, `WAIT`, and `DEAD`) may be unaffected by polling.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: `00` - `BIST` indicates that Built-In Self-Test "on-demand" (BIST) testing is being performed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: If `OPST` returns temporarily to `BIST` from any other state, this signals a non-fatal self-test alarm, which is non-actionable, apart from being logged.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Such a `BIST` alarm must be latched until polled at least once to enable software to record its occurrence.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: `01` - `WAIT` means that a sufficient amount of entropy is not yet available.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: This is not an error condition and may (in fact) be more frequent than ES16 since physical entropy sources often have low bandwidth.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: `11` - `DEAD` is an unrecoverable self-test error.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: This may indicate a hardware fault, a security issue, or (extremely rarely) a type-1 statistical false positive in the continuous testing procedures.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: In case of a fatal failure, an immediate lockdown may also be an appropriate response in dedicated security devices.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The main requirement is that 2-to-1 cryptographic post-processing in 256-bit input blocks will yield 128-bit "full entropy" output blocks.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: Entropy source users may make this conservative assumption but are not prohibited from using more than twice the number of seed bits relative to the desired resulting entropy.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: An implementation of the entropy source should meet at least one of the following requirements sets in order to be considered a secure and safe design:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: The source must also meet the NIST 800-90B min-entropy rate 192/256 = 0.75.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: It must have at least a 256-bit (Post-Quantum Category 5) internal security level.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: All implementations must signal initialization, test mode, and health alarms as required by respective standards.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: This may require the implementer to add non-standard (custom) test interfaces in a secure and safe manner, an example of which is described in
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements

chunks.text: All NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] required components and health test mechanisms must be implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

chunks.text: Instead, the implication is that every 256-bit sequence should have min-entropy of at least 128+64 = 192 bits, as discussed in SP 800-90C cite:[BaKeMc:25]; the likelihood of successfully "guessing" an individual 256-bit output sequence should not be higher than 2^-192^ even with (almost) unconstrained amount of entropy source data and computational power.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

chunks.text: Rather than attempting to define all the mathematical and architectural properties that the entropy source must satisfy, we define that the physical entropy source be strong and robust enough to pass the equivalent of NIST SP 800-90 evaluation and certification for full entropy when conditioned cryptographically in ratio 2:1 with 128-bit output blocks.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

chunks.text: Even though the requirement is defined in terms of 128-bit full entropy blocks, we recommend 256-bit security.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

chunks.text: This can be accomplished by using at least 512 `entropy` bits to initialize a DRBG that has 256-bit security.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > NIST SP 800-90B / FIPS 140-3 Requirements

chunks.text: For alternative Common Criteria certification (or self-certification), AIS 31 PTG.2 class cite:[KiSc11] (Sect. 4.3.) required hardware components and mechanisms must be implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

chunks.text: These two metrics should not be equated or confused with each other.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > BSI AIS-31 PTG.2 / Common Criteria Requirements

chunks.text: It is defined for the benefit of the RISC-V security ecosystem so that virtual systems may have a consistent level of security.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: Despite limited trust, implementers should try to guarantee that even such environments have sufficient entropy available for secure cryptographic operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: A virtual source traps access to the `seed` CSR, emulates it, or otherwise implements it, possibly without direct access to a physical entropy source.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: The output can be cryptographically secure pseudorandomness instead of real entropy, but must have at least 256-bit security, as defined below. A virtual source is intended especially for guest operating systems, sandboxes, emulators, and similar use cases.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: Any implementation of the `seed` CSR that limits the security strength shall not reduce it to less than 256 bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: If the security level is under 256 bits, then the interface must not be available.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: It should fail (`DEAD`) if the host DRBG or entropy source fails and there is insufficient seeding material for the host DRBG.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Entropy Source Requirements > Virtual Sources: Security Requirement

chunks.text: The Zkr extension adds the `SSEED` and `USEED` fields to the `mseccfg` CSR to control access to the `seed` CSR from U, S, or HS modes (see Privileged ISA specification).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

chunks.text: Systems should implement carefully considered access control policies from lower privilege modes to physical entropy sources.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

chunks.text: The system can trap attempted access to `seed` and feed a less privileged client virtual entropy source data () instead of invoking an SP 800-90B () or PTG.2 () physical entropy source.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

chunks.text: Implementations may implement `mseccfg` such that `[s,u]seed` is a read-only constant value `0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

chunks.text: Software may discover if access to the `seed` CSR can be enabled in U and S mode by writing a `1` to `[s,u]seed` and reading back the result.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source > Access Control to `seed`

chunks.text: This property is commonly called "constant-time" although should not be taken with that literal meaning.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt

chunks.text: Zkt does not define a set of instructions available in the core; it just restricts the behaviour of certain instructions if those are implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

chunks.text: The stated goal is that OpenSSL, BoringSSL (Android), the Linux Kernel, and similar trusted software will not have directly observable timing side channels when compiled and running on a Zkt-enabled RISC-V target.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

chunks.text: Vendors do not have to implement all of the list's instructions to be Zkt compliant; however, if they claim to have Zkt and implement any of the listed instructions, it must have data-independent latency.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

chunks.text: The guiding principle should be that no information about the data being operated on should be leaked based on the execution latency.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Scope and Goal

chunks.text: Timing attacks are much more powerful than was realised before the 2010s, which has led to a significant mitigation effort in current cryptographic code-bases. * Cryptography developers use static and dynamic security testing tools to trace the handling of secret information and detect occasions where it influences a branch or is used for a table lookup. * Architectural testing for Zkt can be pragmatic and semi-formal; security by design against basic timing attacks can usually be achieved via conscious implementation (of relevant iterative multi-cycle instructions or instructions composed of micro-ops) in way that avoids data-dependent latency. * Laboratory testing may utilize statistical timing attack leakage analysis techniques such as those described in ISO/IEC 17825 cite:[IS16]. * Binary executables should not contain secrets in the instruction encodings (Kerckhoffs's principle), so instruction timing may leak information about immediates, ordering of input registers, etc.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

chunks.text: There may be an exception to this in systems where a binary loader modifies the executable for purposes of relocation -- and it is desirable to keep the execution location (PC) secret.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

chunks.text: If a secret ends up in address calculation affecting a load or store, that is a violation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

chunks.text: If a secret affects a branch's condition, that is also a violation. A secret variable location or register becomes a non-secret via specific zeroization/sanitisation or by being declared ciphertext (or otherwise no-longer-secret information).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Background

chunks.text: Rather, every one of these instructions that the core does implement must adhere to the requirements of `Zkt`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Zkt listings

chunks.text: Additionally, `seed` CSR latency should be independent of `ES16` state output `entropy` bits, as that is a sensitive security parameter.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Data Independent Execution Latency Subset: Zkt > Zkt listings > RVK (Scalar Cryptography)

chunks.text: The 32-bit instructions were derived from work in cite:[MJS:LWAES:20] and contributed to the RISC-V cryptography extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > AES Instructions

chunks.text: The 64-bit instructions were developed collaboratively by task group members on our mailing list.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > AES Instructions

chunks.text: Algorithms making use of 32-bit rotations: SHA256, AES (Shift Rows), ChaCha20, SM3. * Algorithms making use of 64-bit rotations: SHA512, SHA3.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > Bitmanip Instructions for Cryptography > Rotations

chunks.text: The `xperm4` instruction operates on nibbles. `GPR[rs1]` contains a vector of `XLEN/4` 4-bit elements. `GPR[rs2]` contains a vector of `XLEN/4` 4-bit indexes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > Bitmanip Instructions for Cryptography > Crossbar Permutation Instructions

chunks.text: The `xperm8` instruction operates on bytes. `GPR[rs1]` contains a vector of `XLEN/8` 8-bit elements. `GPR[rs2]` contains a vector of `XLEN/8` 8-bit indexes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > Bitmanip Instructions for Cryptography > Crossbar Permutation Instructions

chunks.text: Lightweight block ciphers using 4-bit SBoxes include: PRESENT cite:[block:present], Rectangle cite:[block:rectangle], GIFT cite:[block:gift], Twine cite:[block:twine], Skinny, MANTIS cite:[block:skinny], Midori cite:[block:midori].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > Bitmanip Instructions for Cryptography > Crossbar Permutation Instructions

chunks.text: National ciphers using 8-bit SBoxes include: Camellia cite:[block:camellia] (Japan), Aria cite:[block:aria] (Korea), AES cite:[nist:fips:197] (USA, Belgium), SM4 cite:[gbt:sm4] (China) Kuznyechik (Russia).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Instruction Rationale > Bitmanip Instructions for Cryptography > Crossbar Permutation Instructions

chunks.text: Hence we also discuss non-ISA system features that may be needed for cryptographic standards compliance and security testing.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations

chunks.text: The scope of RISC-V ISA architectural tests are those behaviors that are independent of the physical entropy source details. A smoke test ES module may be helpful in design phase. * *Technical justification for entropy.* This may take the form of a stochastic model or a heuristic argument that explains why the noise source output is from a random, rather than pseudorandom (deterministic) process, and is not easily predictable or externally observable. A complete physical model is not necessary; research literature can be cited.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: For example, one can show that a good ring oscillator noise derives an amount of physical entropy from local, spontaneously occurring Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise cite:[Sa21], and is therefore not merely "random-looking". * *Entropy Source Design Review.* An entropy source is more than a noise source, and must have features such as health tests (), a conditioner (), and a security boundary with clearly defined interfaces.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: One may tabulate the SHALL statements of SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18], FIPS 140-3 Implementation Guidance cite:[NICC21], AIS-31 cite:[KiSc11] or other standards being used.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: Official and non-official checklist tables are available: https://github.com/usnistgov/90B-Shall-Statements * *Experimental Tests.* The raw noise source is subjected to entropy estimation as defined in NIST 800-90B, Section 3 cite:[TuBaKe:18].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: For SP 800-90B, NIST has made a min-entropy estimation package freely available: https://github.com/usnistgov/SP800-90BEntropyAssessment * **Resilience.** Above physical engineering steps should consider the operational environment of the device, which may be unexpected or hostile (actively attempting to exploit vulnerabilities in the design).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: Compared to older (FIPS 140-2) RNG and DRBG modules, an entropy source module may have a relatively small area (just a few thousand NAND2 gate equivalent). CMVP is introducing an "Entropy Source Validation Scope" which potentially allows 90B validations to be reused for different (FIPS 140-3) modules.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Checklists for Design and Self-Certification

chunks.text: These standards set many of the technical requirements for the RISC-V entropy source design, and we use their terminology if possible.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology

chunks.text: image::esdataflow.svg[align="center",scaledwidth=50%] The `seed` CSR provides an Entropy Source (ES) interface, not a stateful random number generator.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology

chunks.text: Since these are directly based on natural phenomena and are subject to environmental conditions (which may be adversarial), they require features that monitor the "health" and quality of those sources.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology > Entropy Source (ES)

chunks.text: Non-cryptographic conditioners and extractors such as von Neumann's "debiased coin tossing" cite:[Ne51] are easier to implement efficiently but may reduce entropy content (in individual bits removed) more than cryptographic hashes, which mix the input entropy very efficiently.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology > Conditioning: Cryptographic and Non-Cryptographic

chunks.text: Non-cryptographic PRNGs, such as LFSRs and the linear-congruential generators found in many programming libraries, may generate statistically satisfactory random numbers but must never be used for cryptographic keying.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology > Pseudorandom Number Generator (PRNG)

chunks.text: The DRBG should still be frequently refreshed (reseeded) for forward and backward security.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Standards and Terminology > Deterministic Random Bit Generator (DRBG)

chunks.text: An entropy source does not require a high-bandwidth interface; a single DRBG source initialization only requires 512 bits (256 bits of entropy), and DRBG output can be shared by any number of callers.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Once initiated, a DRBG requires new entropy only to mitigate the risk of state compromise.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: From a security perspective, it is essential that the side effect of flushing the secret entropy bits occurs upon reading.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Hence we mandate a write operation on this particular CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: A blocking instruction may have been easier to use, but most users should be querying a (D)RBG instead of an entropy source.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Without a polling-style mechanism, the entropy source could hang for thousands of cycles under some circumstances. A `wfi` or `pause` mechanism (at least potentially) allows energy-saving sleep on MCUs and context switching on higher-end CPUs.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The reason for the particular `OPST = seed[31:0]` two-bit mechanism is to provide redundancy.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The "fault" bit combinations `11` (`DEAD`) and `00` (`BIST`) are more likely for electrical reasons if feature discovery fails and the entropy source is actually not available.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: The 16-bit bandwidth was a compromise motivated by the desire to provide redundancy in the return value, some protection against potential Power/EM leakage (further alleviated by the 2:1 cryptographic conditioning discussed in ), and the desire to have all of the bits "in the same place" on both RV32 and RV64 architectures for programming convenience.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > The `seed` CSR

chunks.text: Hence NIST SP 800-90B cite:[TuBaKe:18] min-entropy assessment must guarantee at least 128 + 64 = 192 bits input entropy per 256-bit block (cite:[BaKeMc:25], Sections 4.1. and 4.3.2).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > NIST SP 800-90B

chunks.text: Only then a hashing of 16 * 16 = 256 bits from the entropy source will produce the desired 128 bits of full entropy.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > NIST SP 800-90B

chunks.text: If NIST SP 800-90B certification is chosen, the entropy source should implement at least the health tests defined in Section 4.4 of cite:[TuBaKe:18]: the repetition count test and adaptive proportion test, or show that the same flaws will be detected by vendor-defined tests.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > NIST SP 800-90B

chunks.text: For validation purposes, the PTG.2 requirements may be mapped to security controls T1-3 () and the interface as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > BSI AIS-31

chunks.text: P1 *[PTG.2.1]* Start-up tests map to T1 and reset-triggered (on-demand) `BIST` tests. * P2 *[PTG.2.2]* Continuous testing total failure maps to T2 and the `DEAD` state. * P3 *[PTG.2.3]* Online tests are continuous tests of T2 – entropy output is prevented in the `BIST` state. * P4 *[PTG.2.4]* Is related to the design of effective entropy source health tests, which we encourage. * P5 *[PTG.2.5]* Raw random sequence may be checked via the GetNoise interface (). * P6 *[PTG.2.6]* Test Procedure A cite:[KiSc11] (Sect 2.4.4.1) is a part of the evaluation process, and we suggest self-evaluation using these tests even if AIS-31 certification is not sought. * P7 *[PTG.2.7]* Average Shannon entropy of "internal random bits" exceeds 0.997.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > BSI AIS-31

chunks.text: The entropy source must be able to support current and future security standards and applications.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Virtual Sources

chunks.text: The 256-bit requirement maps to "Category 5" of NIST Post-Quantum Cryptography (4.A.5 "Security Strength Categories" in cite:[NI16]) and TOP SECRET schemes in Suite B and the newer U.S.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Virtual Sources

chunks.text: The ISA implementation and system design must try to ensure that the hardware-software interface minimizes avenues for adversarial information flow even if not explicitly forbidden in the specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: It is recommended if a single physical entropy source is shared between multiple different virtual machines or if the guest OS is untrusted. A virtual entropy source is significantly more resistant to depletion attacks and also lessens the risk from covert channels.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: The direct `mseccfg.[s,u]seed` option allows one to draw a security boundary around a component in relation to Sensitive Security Parameter (SSP) flows, even if that component is not in M mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: This is helpful when implementing trusted enclaves.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: All information flows and interaction mechanisms must be considered from an adversarial viewpoint: the fewer the better.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: If it does, additional countermeasures are necessary.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Specific Rationale and Considerations > Security Considerations for Direct Hardware Access

chunks.text: In almost all cases, a hardware entropy source must implement appropriate security controls to guarantee unpredictability, prevent leakage, detect attacks, and deny adversarial control over the entropy output or ts generation mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests

chunks.text: Hence the default action in case of a failure should be aimed at damage control: Limiting further output and preventing weak crypto keys from being generated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests

chunks.text: The implementation will simply return `BIST` during the initial start-up self-test period; in any case, the driver must wait for them to finish before starting cryptographic operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T1: On-demand testing

chunks.text: If an error is detected in continuous tests or environmental sensors, the entropy source will enter a no-output state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: We define that a non-critical alarm is signaled if the entropy source returns to `BIST` state from live (`WAIT` or `ES16`) states.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: Critical failures will result in `DEAD` state immediately. A hardware-based continuous testing mechanism must not make statistical information externally available, and it must be zeroized periodically or upon demand via reset, power-up, or similar signal.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: The design should avoid guiding such active attacks by revealing detailed status information.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: Upon detection of an attack, the default action should be aimed at damage control -- to prevent weak crypto keys from being generated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: There may also be requirements for signaling of non-fatal alarms; AIS 31 specifies "noise alarms" that can go off with non-negligible probability even if the device is functioning correctly; these can be signaled with `BIST`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: There rarely is anything that can or should be done about a non-fatal alarm condition in an operator-free, autonomous system.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T2: Continuous checks

chunks.text: Since the security of most cryptographic operations depends on the entropy source, a system-wide "default deny" security policy approach is appropriate for most entropy source failures. A hardware test failure should at least result in the `DEAD` state and possibly reset/halt.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T3: Fatal error states

chunks.text: It’s a show stopper: The entropy source (or its cryptographic client application) must not be allowed to run if its secure operation can’t be guaranteed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T3: Fatal error states

chunks.text: However, even such "innocent" failure modes may indicate a fault attack cite:[KaScVe13] and therefore should be addressed as a system integrity failure rather than as a diagnostic issue.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T3: Fatal error states

chunks.text: Security architects will understand to use permanent or hard-to-recover "security-fuse" lockdowns only if the threshold of a test is such that the probability of false-positive is negligible over the entire device lifetime.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > T3: Fatal error states

chunks.text: For the same reasons, only complete and fully processed random words shall be made available via `entropy` (ES16 status of `seed`).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > Information Flows

chunks.text: Importantly the test interface and the main interface should not be operational at the same time.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > Information Flows

chunks.text: [quote, NIST SP 800-90B, Noise Source Requirements] The noise source state shall be protected from adversarial knowledge or influence to the greatest extent possible.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > Information Flows

chunks.text: The methods used for this shall be documented, including a description of the (conceptual) security boundary's role in protecting the noise source from adversarial observation or influence.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Security Controls and Health Tests > Information Flows

chunks.text: When considering implementation options and trade-offs, one must look at the entire information flow.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies

chunks.text: This raw data also needs to be protected by the design. . *Continuous health tests* ensure that the noise source and its environment meet their operational parameters. . *Non-cryptographic conditioners* remove much of the bias and correlation in input noise. . *Cryptographic conditioners* produce full entropy output, completely indistinguishable from ideal random. . *DRBG* takes in `>=256` bits of seed entropy as keying material and uses a "one way" cryptographic process to rapidly generate bits on demand (without revealing the seed/state).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies

chunks.text: Steps 1-4 (possibly 5) are considered to be part of the Entropy Source (ES) and provided by the `seed` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies

chunks.text: The sampled bit sequence may be expected to be relatively uncorrelated (close to IID) if the sample rate is suitably low cite:[KiSc11].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Ring Oscillators

chunks.text: Their output is sometimes highly dependent on temperature, which must be taken into account in testing and modeling.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Ring Oscillators

chunks.text: Countermeasures are related to circuit design; environmental sensors, electrical filters, and usage of a differential oscillator may help.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Ring Oscillators

chunks.text: It may be possible to certify more exotic noise sources and designs, although their stochastic model needs to be equally well understood, and their CPU interfaces must be secure.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Other types of noise

chunks.text: The tests should be designed in a way that a specific number of samples guarantees a state flush (no hung states).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: Repetition count is reset every time the output sample value changes; if the count reaches a certain cutoff limit, a noise alarm (`BIST`) or failure (`DEAD`) is signaled.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: We see that the structure of the mandatory tests is such that, if well implemented, no information is carried beyond a limit of `W` samples.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: Especially if a non-cryptographic conditioner is used in hardware, it is possible that the AIS 31 cite:[KiSc11] online tests are implemented by driver software.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: For some security profiles, AIS 31 mandates that their tolerances are set in a way that the probability of an alarm is at least 10^-6^ yearly under "normal usage." Such requirements are problematic in modern applications since their probability is too high for critical systems.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: There rarely is anything that can or should be done about a non-fatal alarm condition in an operator-free, autonomous system.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: However, AIS 31 allows the DRBG component to keep running despite a failure in its Entropy Source, so we suggest re-entering a temporary `BIST` state () to signal a non-fatal statistical error if such (non-actionable) signaling is necessary.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: Drivers and applications can react to this appropriately (or simply log it), but it will not directly affect the availability of the TRNG. A permanent error condition should result in `DEAD` state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Continuous Health Tests

chunks.text: One may take a set of bits from a noise source and XOR them together to produce a less biased (and more independent) bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Non-cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: However, such an XOR may introduce "pseudorandomness" and make the output difficult to analyze. * The von Neumann extractor cite:[Ne51] looks at consecutive pairs of bits, rejects 00 and 11, and outputs 0 or 1 for 01 and 10, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Non-cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: If its assumptions hold, it also removes dependencies, creating an independent and identically distributed (IID) source. * Other linear and non-linear correctors such as those discussed by Dichtl and Lacharme cite:[La08].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Non-cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: If well designed, they can be evaluated in conjunction with a stochastic model of the noise source itself.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Non-cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: They may also be implemented on the hardware side if necessary.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: In any case, the `entropy` ES16 output must always be compressed 2:1 (or more) before being used as keying material or considered "full entropy."
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: On the other hand, simpler non-cryptographic conditioners may be too wasteful on input entropy if high-quality random output is required -- (ARM TrustZone TRBG cite:[AR17] outputs only 10Kbit/sec at 200 MHz.) Hence a resource-saving compromise is made between hardware and software generation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Cryptographic Conditioners

chunks.text: All random bits reaching end users and applications must come from a cryptographic DRBG.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > The Final Random: DRBGs

chunks.text: The RISC-V AES and SHA instruction set extensions should be used if available since they offer additional security features such as timing attack resistance.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > The Final Random: DRBGs

chunks.text: These are significantly easier to implement in software than in hardware.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > The Final Random: DRBGs

chunks.text: This security model means that many of the available QRNG devices do not use cryptographic conditioning and may fail cryptographic statistical requirements cite:[HuHe20].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Quantum vs. Classical Random

chunks.text: Many implementers may consider them to be entropy sources instead.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Quantum vs. Classical Random

chunks.text: Of course, one must avoid DRBGs that are based on problems that are easily solvable with quantum computers, such as factoring cite:[Sh94] in the case of the Blum-Blum-Shub generator cite:[BlBlSh86].
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Post-Quantum Cryptography

chunks.text: While even "64-bit" quantum security is extremely difficult to break, many applications specify a higher security requirement. NIST cite:[NI16] defines AES-128 to be "Category 1" equivalent post-quantum security, while AES-256 is "Category 5" (highest).
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Implementation Strategies > Post-Quantum Cryptography

chunks.text: Since this type of interface is both necessary for security testing and also constitutes a potential backdoor to the cryptographic key generation process, we define a safety behavior that compliant implementations can have for temporarily disabling the entropy source `seed` CSR interface during test.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: In order for shared RISC-V self-certification scripts (and drivers) to accommodate the test interface in a secure fashion, we suggest that it is implemented as a custom, M-mode only CSR, denoted here as `mnoise`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: We define the semantics for single bit for this interface, `mnoise[31]`, which is named `NOISETEST`, which will affect the behavior of `seed` if implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: When `NOISETEST = 1` in `mnoise`, the `seed` CSR must not return anything via `ES16`; it should be in `BIST` state unless the source is `DEAD`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: When `NOISETEST` is again disabled, the entropy source shall return from `BIST` via an appropriate zeroization and self-test mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: The behavior of other input and output bits is largely left to the vendor (as they depend on the technical details of the physical entropy source), as is the address of the custom `mnoise` CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: Other contents and behavior of the CSR only can be interpreted in the context of `mvendorid`, `marchid`, and `mimpid` CSR identifiers.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: Implementation of test interfaces that directly affect ES16 entropy output from the `seed` CSR interface is discouraged.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: Hence even a temporary lapse in `entropy` security may have serious security implications.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Scalar & Entropy Source Instructions, Version 1.0.1 > Entropy Source Rationale and Recommendations > Suggested GetNoise Test Interface

chunks.text: the result of *orc.b* on a chunk that does not contain any *NUL* bytes will be all-ones, and * after a bitwise-negation of the result of *orc.b*, the number of data bytes before the first *NUL* byte (if any) can be detected by *ctz*/*clz* (depending on the endianness of data).
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions Assembly Code Examples > strlen

chunks.text: Get bytes in big-endian order and compare. if BYTEORDER ORDERLITTLEENDIAN rev8 a2, a2 rev8 a3, a3 endif Synthesize (a2 >= a3) ? 1 : -1 in a branchless sequence. sltu a0, a2, a3 neg a0, a0 ori a0, a0, 1 ret
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions Assembly Code Examples > strlen > strcmp

chunks.text: If words don't match, fall back to simple loop. bne a2, a3, .Lsimpleloop
chunks.section: Preamble > Bit Manipulation Extensions Assembly Code Examples > strlen > strcmp

chunks.text: If XLEN=32 and the F extension is present, the ext:c[] extension additionally depends upon the extlink:zcf[] extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:c[] Extension for Compressed Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is present, the ext:c[] extension additionally depends upon the extlink:zcd[] extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:c[] Extension for Compressed Instructions

chunks.text: Conditional branches encode an offset in the immediate field of the instruction and are thus direct branches that are not susceptible to control-flow subversion.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI)

chunks.text: Unconditional indirect jumps using the `JALR` obtain their branch target by adding the sign extended 12-bit immediate encoded in the instruction to the `rs1` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI)

chunks.text: The RV32I/RV64I does not have a dedicated instruction for calling a procedure or returning from a procedure. A `JAL` or `JALR` may be used to perform a procedure call and `JALR` to return from a procedure.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI)

chunks.text: The `C.J` and `C.JAL` instructions encode an offset in the immediate field of the instruction and thus are not susceptible to control-flow subversion.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI)

chunks.text: The `LPAD` instruction must be placed at the program locations that are valid targets of indirect jumps or calls.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: When the Zicfilp is active, the hart tracks an expected landing pad (`ELP`) state that is updated by an indirectcall or indirectjump to require a landing pad instruction at the target of the branch.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: If the instruction at the target is not a landing pad, then a software-check exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: A landing pad may be optionally associated with a 20-bit label.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: When labeling of landing pads is used, indirect call or indirect jump site can specify the expected label of the landing pad and thereby constrain the set of landing pads that may be reached from each indirect call or indirect jump site in the program.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: By constraining gadgets to be preceded by a landing pad instruction that marks the start of indirect callable functions, the program can significantly reduce the available gadget space. A second form of label generation may generate a signature, such as a MAC, using the prototype of the function.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: Such programs may further use multiple labels per function, which means that if a function is called from two or more call sites, the functions can be labeled as being reachable from each of the call sites.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: However, if function Y was assigned two labels - one corresponding to call site A and the other to call site B, then Y can be invoked by both call sites, but X can only be invoked by call site A and Z can only be invoked by call site B.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: This would allow the function to be labeled with multiple labels, each corresponding to a specific call site. A portion of the label space may be dedicated to labeled landing pads that are only valid targets of an indirect jump (and not an indirect call).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: When Zicfilp is not active at a privilege level or when the extension is not implemented, the landing pad instruction executes as a no-op. A program that is built with `LPAD` instructions can thus continue to operate correctly, but without forward-edge control-flow integrity, on processors that do not support the Zicfilp extension or if the Zicfilp extension is not active.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: Compilers and linkers should provide an attribute flag to indicate if the program has been compiled with the Zicfilp extension and use that to determine if the Zicfilp extension should be activated.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: The dynamic loader should activate the use of Zicfilp extension for an application only if all executables (the application and the dependent dynamically linked libraries) used by that application use the Zicfilp extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: When Zicfilp extension is not active or not implemented, the hart does not require landing pad instructions at the targets of indirect calls/jumps, and the landing instructions revert to being no-ops.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: The Zicfilp extensions may be activated for use individually and independently for each privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: The Zicfilp extension depends on the Zicsr extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp)

chunks.text: To enforce that the target of an indirect call or indirect jump must be a valid landing pad instruction, the hart maintains an expected landing pad (`ELP`) state to determine if a landing pad instruction is required at the target of an indirect call or an indirect jump.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: The Zicfilp extension, when enabled, determines if an indirect call or an indirect jump must land on a landing pad, as specified in .
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: If `islpexpected` is 1, then the hart updates the `ELP` to `LPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: The landing pad may be labeled.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: The `LPAD` instruction is encoded with a 20-bit immediate value called the landing-pad-label (`LPL`) that is matched to the expected landing pad label.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: When `LPL` is encoded as zero, the `LPAD` instruction does not perform the label check and in programs built with this single label mode of operation the indirect call/jump sites do not need to establish an expected landing pad label value in `x7`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: When `ELP` is set to `LPEXPECTED`, if the next instruction in the instruction stream is not 4-byte aligned, or is not `LPAD`, or if the landing pad label encoded in `LPAD` is not zero and does not match the expected landing pad label in bits 31:12 of the `x7` register, then a software-check exception (cause=18) with `xtval` set to "landing pad fault (code=2)" is raised else the `ELP` is updated to `NOLPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement

chunks.text: When Zicfilp is enabled, `LPAD` is the only instruction allowed to execute when the `ELP` state is `LPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: If Zicfilp is not enabled then the instruction is a no-op.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: If Zicfilp is enabled, the `LPAD` instruction causes a software-check exception with `xtval` set to "landing pad fault (code=2)" if any of the following conditions are true:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: The `pc` is not 4-byte aligned and `ELP` is `LPEXPECTED`. * The `ELP` is `LPEXPECTED` and the `LPL` is not zero and the `LPL` does not match the expected landing pad label in bits 31:12 of the `x7` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: If a software-check exception is not caused then the `ELP` is updated to `NOLPEXPECTED`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: If PC not 4-byte aligned then software-check exception if pc[1:0] != 0 raise software-check exception //
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: If landing pad label not matched -> software-check exception else if (inst.LPL != x7[31:12] && inst.LPL != 0) raise software-check exception else ELP = NOLPEXPECTED else no-op endif ---- <<<
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Landing Pad Enforcement > Landing Pad Instruction

chunks.text: The Zicfiss extension introduces a shadow stack to enforce backward-edge control-flow integrity. A shadow stack is a second stack used to store a shadow copy of the return address in the link register if it needs to be spilled.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: The shadow stack is used to spill the link register, if required, by non-leaf functions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: When such a function returns (the epilogue), the function loads the link register from the regular stack and the shadow copy of the link register from the shadow stack.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: Then, the link register value from the regular stack and the shadow link register value from the shadow stack are compared. A mismatch of the two values is indicative of a subversion of the return address control variable and causes a software-check exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: The Zicfiss instructions, except `SSAMOSWAP.W/D`, are encoded using a subset of May-Be-Operation instructions defined by the Zimop and Zcmop extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: This subset of instructions revert to their Zimop/Zcmop defined behavior when the Zicfiss extension is not implemented or if the extension has not been activated. A program that is built with Zicfiss instructions can thus continue to operate correctly, but without backward-edge control-flow integrity, on processors that do not support the Zicfiss extension or if the Zicfiss extension is not active.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: The Zicfiss extension may be activated for use individually and independently for each privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: The dynamic loader should activate the use of Zicfiss extension for an application only if all executables (the application and the dependent dynamically-linked libraries) used by that application use the Zicfiss extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: The Zicfiss extension depends on the Zicsr, Zimop and Zaamo extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: Furthermore, if the Zcmop extension is implemented, the Zicfiss extension also provides the `C.SSPUSH` and `C.SSPOPCHK` instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss)

chunks.text: When a `MOP.RR.7` or `MOP.R.28` encoding is not used by the Zicfiss extension, the corresponding instruction adheres to its Zimop-defined behavior, unless redefined by another extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Zicfiss Instructions Summary

chunks.text: The `ssp` CSR is an unprivileged read-write (URW) CSR that reads and writes `XLEN` low order bits of the shadow stack pointer (`ssp`).
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`)

chunks.text: There is no high CSR defined as the `ssp` is always as wide as the `XLEN` of the current privilege mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`)

chunks.text: The bits 1:0 of `ssp` are read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`)

chunks.text: If the UXLEN or SXLEN may never be 32, then the bit 2 is also read-only zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Shadow Stack Pointer (`ssp`)

chunks.text: Zicfiss provides a 16-bit version of the `SSPUSH x1` instruction using the Zcmop defined `C.MOP.1` encoding.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Push to the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: Zicfiss provides a 16-bit version of the `SSPOPCHK x5` using the Zcmop defined `C.MOP.5` encoding.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: When returning from these non-leaf functions, such programs pop the link register from the regular stack and pop a shadow copy of the link register from the shadow stack.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: If the values do not match, it is indicative of a corruption of the return address variable on the regular stack.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: While any register may be used as link register, conventionally the `x1` or `x5` registers are used.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: Only x1 and x5 may be used as src raise software-check exception else ssp = ssp + (XLEN/8) increment ssp by XLEN/8. endif endif ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: If the value loaded from the address in `ssp` does not match the value in `rs1`, a software-check exception (cause=18) is raised with `xtval` set to "shadow stack fault (code=3)".
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: The software-check exception caused by `SSPOPCHK`/ `C.SSPOPCHK` is lower in priority than a load/store/AMO access-fault exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: The `ssp` is incremented by `SSPOPCHK` and `C.SSPOPCHK` only if the load from the shadow stack completes successfully and no software-check exception is raised.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Pop from the Shadow Stack

chunks.text: The `SSRDP` instruction is provided to move the contents of `ssp` to a destination register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Read `ssp` into a Register

chunks.text: Encoding rd as `x0` is not supported for `SSRDP`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Read `ssp` into a Register

chunks.text: The operation of the `SSRDP` instructions is as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Read `ssp` into a Register

chunks.text: For RV32, `SSAMOSWAP.W` atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address of a shadow stack location in `rs1`, puts the loaded value into register `rd`, and stores the 32-bit value held in `rs2` to the original address in `rs1`. `SSAMOSWAP.D` (RV64 only) is similar to `SSAMOSWAP.W` but operates on 64-bit data values.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Atomic Swap from a Shadow Stack Location

chunks.text: For RV64, `SSAMOSWAP.W` atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address of a shadow stack location in `rs1`, sign-extends the loaded value and puts it in `rd`, and stores the lower 32 bits of the value held in `rs2` to the original address in `rs1`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Atomic Swap from a Shadow Stack Location

chunks.text: The same exception options apply if the address is not naturally aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Atomic Swap from a Shadow Stack Location

chunks.text: An `SSAMOSWAP.W/D` operation has acquire semantics if `aq=1` and release semantics if `rl=1`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Control-flow Integrity (CFI) > Landing Pad (Zicfilp) > Shadow Stack (Zicfiss) > Atomic Swap from a Shadow Stack Location

chunks.text: A management instruction manipulates cached copies of data with respect to a set of agents that can access the data * A zero instruction zeros out a range of memory locations, potentially allocating cached copies of data in one or more caches * A prefetch instruction indicates to hardware that data at a given memory location may be accessed in the near future, potentially allocating cached copies of data in one or more caches
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Introduction

chunks.text: The execution behavior of the above instructions is also modified by CSR state added by this specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Introduction

chunks.text: Any number of caches may be interspersed between an agent and a memory location, and load and store operations from an agent may be satisfied by a cache instead of the memory location.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Memory and Caches

chunks.text: In the initial set of CMO extensions, the size of a cache block shall be uniform throughout the system.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Memory and Caches

chunks.text: Implementation techniques such as speculative execution or hardware prefetching may cause a given cache to allocate or deallocate a copy of a cache block at any time, provided the corresponding physical addresses are accessible according to the supported access type PMA and are cacheable according to the cacheability PMA.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Memory and Caches

chunks.text: Allocating a copy of a cache block results in a read transfer from another cache or from memory, while deallocating a copy of a cache block may result in a write transfer to another cache or to memory depending on whether the data in the copy were modified by a store operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Memory and Caches

chunks.text: A clean operation performs a write transfer to another cache or to memory if the data in the copy of the cache block have been modified by a store operation
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Cache-Block Operations

chunks.text: Unless specified otherwise, the store operations generated by a cache-block zero instruction have the same general properties and behaviors that other store instructions in the architecture have.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Cache-Block Operations

chunks.text: An implementation may or may not update the entire set of bytes atomically with a single store operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Background > Cache-Block Operations
