chunks.text: The coherent agents within such a set shall access a given memory location with the same physical address and the same physical memory attributes; however, if the coherence PMA for a given agent indicates a given memory location is not coherent, that agent shall not be a member of a set of coherent agents with any other agent for that memory location and shall be the sole member of a set of coherent agents consisting of itself.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: Caches introduce the possibility that multiple copies of a given cache block may be present in a system at the same time.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: Additionally, if a coherent agent in the set executes a CBO instruction that specifies the cache block, the resulting operation shall apply to any and all of the copies in the caches that can be accessed by the load and store operations from the coherent agents.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: A coherent cache is permitted to perform a write transfer to memory provided that a store operation has modified the data in the cache block since the most recent invalidate, clean, or flush operation on the cache block
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: A coherent cache is required to perform a write transfer to memory if a store operation has modified the data in the cache block since the most recent invalidate, clean, or flush operation on the cache block and if the next clean or flush operation requires a write transfer to memory
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: A non-coherent agent may initiate a cache-block operation that operates on the set of coherent caches accessed by a set of coherent agents.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches

chunks.text: Specifically, if a precedes b in program order, then a will precede b in the global memory order if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Preserved Program Order

chunks.text: An invalidate operation may change the set of values that can be returned by a load.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: If an invalidate operation i precedes a load r and operates on a byte x returned by r, and no store to x appears between i and r in program order or in the global memory order, then r returns any of the following values for x:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: If no clean or flush operations on x precede i in the global memory order, either the initial value of x or the value of any store to x that precedes i
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: If no store to x precedes a clean or flush operation on x in the global memory order and if the clean or flush operation on x precedes i in the global memory order, either the initial value of x or the value of any store to x that precedes i
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: If a store to x precedes a clean or flush operation on x in the global memory order and if the clean or flush operation on x precedes i in the global memory order, either the value of the latest store to x that precedes the latest clean or flush operation on x or the value of any store to x that both precedes i and succeeds the latest clean or flush operation on x that precedes i
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: The value of any store to x by a non-coherent agent regardless of the above conditions
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Memory Ordering > Load Values

chunks.text: Execution of certain CMO instructions may result in traps due to CSR state, described in the section, or due to the address translation and protection mechanisms.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps

chunks.text: The trapping behavior of CMO instructions is described in the following sections.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps

chunks.text: Cache-block management instructions and cache-block zero instructions may raise illegal-instruction exceptions or virtual-instruction exceptions depending on the current privilege mode and the state of the CMO control registers described in the section.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Illegal-Instruction and Virtual-Instruction Exceptions

chunks.text: Cache-block prefetch instructions raise neither illegal-instruction exceptions nor virtual-instruction exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Illegal-Instruction and Virtual-Instruction Exceptions

chunks.text: The PMP access control bits shall be the same for all physical addresses in the cache block, and if write permission is granted by the PMP access control bits, read permission shall also be granted
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: The PMAs shall be the same for all physical addresses in the cache block, and if write permission is granted by the supported access type PMAs, read permission shall also be granted
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: If the above constraints are not met, the behavior of a CBO instruction is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: Main memory regions are required to support accesses by cache-block zero instructions; however, I/O regions may specify whether accesses by cache-block zero instructions are supported.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: If neither a load instruction nor store instruction is permitted to access the physical addresses, but an instruction fetch is permitted to access the physical addresses, whether a cache-block management instruction is permitted to access the cache block is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: If access to the cache block is not permitted, a cache-block management instruction raises a store page-fault or store guest-page-fault exception if address translation does not permit any access or raises a store access-fault exception otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: During address translation, the instruction also checks the accessed bit and may either raise an exception or set the bit as required.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: A cache-block zero instruction is permitted to access the specified cache block whenever a store instruction is permitted to access the corresponding physical addresses and when the PMAs indicate that cache-block zero instructions are a supported access type.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: If access to the cache block is not permitted, a cache-block zero instruction raises a store page-fault or store guest-page-fault exception if address translation does not permit write access or raises a store access-fault exception otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: During address translation, the instruction also checks the accessed and dirty bits and may either raise an exception or set the bits as required.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: If access to the cache block is not permitted, a cache-block prefetch instruction does not raise any exceptions and shall not access any caches or memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: During address translation, the instruction does not check the accessed and dirty bits and neither raises an exception nor sets the bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Page-Fault, Guest-Page-Fault, and Access-Fault Exceptions

chunks.text: CMO instructions do not generate address-misaligned exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Address-Misaligned Exceptions

chunks.text: Unless otherwise defined by the debug architecture specification, the behavior of trigger modules with respect to CMO instructions is UNSPECIFIED.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Breakpoint Exceptions and Debug Mode Entry

chunks.text: For the purposes of writing the `mtinst` or `htinst` register on a trap, the following standard transformation is defined for cache-block management instructions and cache-block zero instructions:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Hypervisor Extension

chunks.text: The `operation` field corresponds to the 12 most significant bits of the trapping instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Traps > Hypervisor Extension

chunks.text: Other general cache characteristics may also be specified in the discovery mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Coherent Agents and Caches > Software Discovery

chunks.text: The x{csrname} registers control CBO instruction execution based on the current privilege mode and the state of the appropriate CSRs, as detailed below.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: A `CBO.INVAL` instruction executes or raises either an illegal-instruction exception or a virtual-instruction exception based on the state of the `x{csrname}.CBIE` fields:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: A `CBO.CLEAN` or `CBO.FLUSH` instruction executes or raises an illegal-instruction or virtual-instruction exception based on the state of the `x{csrname}.CBCFE` bits:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: Finally, a `CBO.ZERO` instruction executes or raises an illegal-instruction or virtual-instruction exception based on the state of the `x{csrname}.CBZE` bits:
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: The CBIE/CBCFE/CBZE fields in each `x{csrname}` register do not affect the read and write behavior of the same fields in the other `x{csrname}` registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: Each `x{csrname}` register is WARL; however, software should determine the legal values from the execution environment discovery mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > CSR controls for CMO instructions

chunks.text: An implementation may update the bytes in any order and with any granularity and atomicity, including individual bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Extensions > Cache-Block Zero Instructions

chunks.text: These instructions operate on the cache block whose effective address is the sum of the base address specified in rs1 and the sign-extended offset encoded in imm[11:0], where imm[4:0] shall equal `0b00000`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Extensions > Cache-Block Prefetch Instructions

chunks.text: The offset operand may be omitted; otherwise, any expression that computes the offset shall evaluate to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.clean

chunks.text: On faults, the faulting virtual address is considered to be the value in rs1, rather than the base address of the cache block.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.flush

chunks.text: The assembly offset operand may be omitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.flush

chunks.text: If it isn't then any expression that computes the offset shall evaluate to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.flush

chunks.text: On faults, the faulting virtual address is considered to be the value in rs1, rather than the base address of the cache block.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.inval

chunks.text: Depending on CSR programming, the instruction may perform a flush operation instead of an invalidate operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.inval

chunks.text: The assembly offset operand may be omitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.inval

chunks.text: If it isn't then any expression that computes the offset shall evaluate to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.inval

chunks.text: On faults, the faulting virtual address is considered to be the value in rs1, rather than the base address of the cache block.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.zero

chunks.text: An implementation may or may not update the entire set of bytes atomically.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.zero

chunks.text: The assembly offset operand may be omitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.zero

chunks.text: If it isn't then any expression that computes the offset shall evaluate to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > cbo.zero

chunks.text: A *prefetch.i* instruction indicates to hardware that the cache block whose effective address is the sum of the base address specified in rs1 and the sign-extended offset encoded in imm[11:0], where imm[4:0] equals `0b00000`, is likely to be accessed by an instruction fetch in the near future.
chunks.section: Preamble > Cache Management Operations (CMOs) > Pseudocode for instruction semantics > Instructions > prefetch.i

chunks.text: The D extension widens the 32 floating-point registers, `f0-f31`, to 64 bits (FLEN=64 in ).
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State

chunks.text: The `f` registers can now hold either 32-bit or 64-bit floating-point values as described below in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State

chunks.text: When multiple floating-point precisions are supported, then valid values of narrower n-bit types, n<FLEN, are represented in the lower n bits of an FLEN-bit NaN value, in a process termed NaN-boxing.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: The upper bits of a valid NaN-boxed value must be all 1s.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: Valid NaN-boxed n-bit values therefore appear as negative quiet NaNs (qNaNs) when viewed as any wider m-bit value, n < m {le} FLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: Any operation that writes a narrower result to an 'f' register must write all 1s to the uppermost FLEN-n bits to yield a legal NaN-boxedvalue.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: Floating-point n-bit transfer operations move external values held in the IEEE 754-2008 formats into and out of the `f` registers, and comprise floating-point loads and stores (FLn/FSn) and floating-point move instructions (FMV.n.X/FMV.X.n). A narrower n-bit transfer, n<FLEN, into the `f` registers will create a valid NaN-boxed value. A narrower n-bit transfer out of the floating-point registers will transfer the lower n bits of the register ignoring the upper FLEN-n bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: If so, the n least-significant bits of the input are used as the input value, otherwise the input value is treated as an n-bit canonical NaN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > NaN Boxing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: The FLD instruction loads a double-precision floating-point value from memory into floating-point register rd. FSD stores a double-precision value from the floating-point registers to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLD and FSD are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned and XLEN{ge}64.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLD and FSD do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: The double-precision floating-point computational instructions are defined analogously to their single-precision counterparts, but operate on double-precision operands and produce double-precision results.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OP-FP major opcode space. FCVT.W.D or FCVT.L.D converts a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point register rs1 to a signed 32-bit or 64-bit integer, respectively, in integer register rd. FCVT.D.W or FCVT.D.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into a double-precision floating-point number in floating-point register rd. FCVT.WU.D, FCVT.LU.D, FCVT.D.WU, and FCVT.D.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64, FCVT.W[U].D sign-extends the 32-bit result. FCVT.L[U].D and FCVT.D.L[U] are RV64-only instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The range of valid inputs for FCVT.int.D and the behavior for invalid inputs are the same as for FCVT.int.S.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: All floating-point to integer and integer to floating-point conversion instructions round according to the rm field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The double-precision to single-precision and single-precision to double-precision conversion instructions, FCVT.S.D and FCVT.D.S, are encoded in the OP-FP major opcode space and both the source and destination are floating-point registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The rs2 field encodes the datatype of the source, and the fmt field encodes the datatype of the destination. FCVT.S.D rounds according to the RM field; FCVT.D.S will never round.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point to floating-point sign-injection instructions, FSGNJ.D, FSGNJN.D, and FSGNJX.D are defined analogously to the single-precision sign-injection instruction. //FSGNJ.D, FSGNJN.D, and FSGNJX.D
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: For XLEN{ge}64 only, instructions are provided to move bit patterns between the floating-point and integer registers. FMV.X.D moves the double-precision value in floating-point register rs1 to a representation in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding in integer register rd. FMV.D.X moves the double-precision value encoded in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding from the integer register rs1 to the floating-point register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: FMV.X.D and FMV.D.X do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The double-precision floating-point classify instruction, FCLASS.D, is defined analogously to its single-precision counterpart, but operates on double-precision operands.
chunks.section: Preamble > "D" Extension for Double-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > D Register State > Double-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

chunks.text: The F extension depends on the "Zicsr" extension for control and status register access.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2

chunks.text: The F extension adds 32 floating-point registers, `f0-f31`, each 32 bits wide, and a floating-point control and status register `fcsr`, which contains the operating mode and exception status of the floating-point unit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: This additional state is shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: We use the term FLEN to describe the width of the floating-point registers in the RISC-V ISA, and FLEN=32 for the F single-precision floating-point extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: Most floating-point instructions operate on values in the floating-point register file.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: Floating-point load and store instructions transfer floating-point values between registers and memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: Instructions to transfer values to and from the integer register file are also provided.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State

chunks.text: The floating-point control and status register, `fcsr`, is a RISC-V control and status register (CSR).
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: It is a 32-bit read/write register that selects the dynamic rounding mode for floating-point arithmetic operations and holds the accrued exception flags, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The `fcsr` register can be read and written with the FRCSR and FSCSR instructions, which are assembler pseudoinstructions built on the underlying CSR access instructions. FRCSR reads `fcsr` by copying it into integer register rd. FSCSR swaps the value in `fcsr` by copying the original value into integer register rd, and then writing a new value obtained from integer register rs1 into `fcsr`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The fields within the `fcsr` can also be accessed individually through different CSR addresses, and separate assembler pseudoinstructions are defined for these accesses.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The FRRM instruction reads the Rounding Mode field `frm` (`fcsr` bits 7--5) and copies it into the least-significant three bits of integer register rd, with zero in all other bits. FSRM swaps the value in `frm` by copying the original value into integer register rd, and then writing a new value obtained from the three least-significant bits of integer register rs1 into `frm`. FRFLAGS and FSFLAGS are defined analogously for the Accrued Exception Flags field `fflags` (`fcsr` bits 4--0).
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Bits 31--8 of the `fcsr` are reserved for other standard extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: If these extensions are not present, implementations shall ignore writes to these bits and supply a zero value when read.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Standard software should preserve the contents of these bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Floating-point operations use either a static rounding mode encoded in the instruction, or a dynamic rounding mode held in `frm`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Rounding modes are encoded as shown in . A value of 111 in the instruction's rm field selects the dynamic rounding mode held in `frm`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The behavior of floating-point instructions that depend on rounding mode when executed with a reserved rounding mode is reserved, including both static reserved rounding modes (101-110) and dynamic reserved rounding modes (101-111).
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Some instructions, including widening conversions, have the rm field but are nevertheless mathematically unaffected by the rounding mode; software should set their rm field to RNE (000) but implementations must treat the rm field as usual (in particular, with regard to decoding legal vs. reserved encodings).
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The accrued exception flags indicate the exception conditions that have arisen on any floating-point arithmetic instruction since the field was last reset by software, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: The base RISC-V ISA does not support generating a trap on the setting of a floating-point exception flag.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Floating-Point Control and Status Register

chunks.text: Except when otherwise stated, if the result of a floating-point operation is NaN, it is the canonical NaN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > NaN Generation and Propagation

chunks.text: The canonical NaN has a positive sign and all significand bits clear except the MSB, a.k.a. the quiet bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > NaN Generation and Propagation

chunks.text: For single-precision floating-point, this corresponds to the pattern `0x7fc00000`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > NaN Generation and Propagation

chunks.text: Operations on subnormal numbers are handled in accordance with IEEE 754-2008.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Subnormal Arithmetic

chunks.text: In the parlance of IEEE 754-2008, tininess is detected after rounding.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Subnormal Arithmetic

chunks.text: The FLW instruction loads a single-precision floating-point value from memory into floating-point register rd. FSW stores a single-precision value from floating-point register rs2 to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLW and FSW are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLW and FSW do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: As described in , the execution environment defines whether misaligned floating-point loads and stores are handled invisibly or raise a contained or fatal trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point arithmetic instructions with one or two source operands use the R-type format with the OP-FP major opcode. FADD.S and FMUL.S perform single-precision floating-point addition and multiplication respectively, between rs1 and rs2. FSUB.S performs the single-precision floating-point subtraction of rs2 from rs1. FDIV.S performs the single-precision floating-point division of rs1 by rs2. FSQRT.S computes the square root of rs1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: The 2-bit floating-point format field fmt is encoded as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: It is set to S (00) for all instructions in the F extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: All floating-point operations that perform rounding can select the rounding mode using the rm field with the encoding shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point minimum-number and maximum-number instructions FMIN.S and FMAX.S write, respectively, the smaller or larger of rs1 and rs2 to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: For the purposes of these instructions only, the value −0.0 is considered to be less than the value +0.0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point fused multiply-add instructions require a new standard instruction format. R4-type instructions specify three source registers (rs1, rs2, and rs3) and a destination register (rd).
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: This format is only used by the floating-point fused multiply-add instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: FMADD.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2, adds the value in rs3, and writes the final result to rd. FMADD.S computes (rs1×rs2)+rs3.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: FMSUB.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2, subtracts the value in rs3, and writes the final result to rd. FMSUB.S computes (rs1×rs2)−rs3.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: FNMSUB.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2, negates the product, adds the value in rs3, and writes the final result to rd. FNMSUB.S computes −(rs1×rs2)+rs3.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: FNMADD.S multiplies the values in rs1 and rs2, negates the product, subtracts the value in rs3, and writes the final result to rd. FNMADD.S computes −(rs1×rs2)−rs3.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: The fused multiply-add instructions must set the invalid operation exception flag when the multiplicands are {inf} and zero, even when the addend is a quiet NaN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Computational Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point-to-integer and integer-to-floating-point conversion instructions are encoded in the OP-FP major opcode space. FCVT.W.S or FCVT.L.S converts a floating-point number in floating-point register rs1 to a signed 32-bit or 64-bit integer, respectively, in integer register rd. FCVT.S.W or FCVT.S.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, in integer register rs1 into a floating-point number in floating-point register rd. FCVT.WU.S, FCVT.LU.S, FCVT.S.WU, and FCVT.S.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: For XLEN>32, FCVT.W[U].S sign-extends the 32-bit result to the destination register width. FCVT.L[U].S and FCVT.S.L[U] are RV64-only instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: If the rounded result is not representable in the destination format, it is clipped to the nearest value and the invalid flag is set. gives the range of valid inputs for FCVT.int.S and the behavior for invalid inputs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: All floating-point to integer and integer to floating-point conversion instructions round according to the rm field. A floating-point register can be initialized to floating-point positive zero using FCVT.S.W rd, `x0`, which will never set any exception flags.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: |Maximum valid input (after rounding) |2^31^−1 |2^32^−1 |2^63^−1 |2^64^−1
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: |Output for out-of-range negative input |−2^31^ |0 |−2^63^ |0
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions
Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: |Output for out-of-range positive input |2^31^−1 |2^32^−1 |2^63^−1 |2^64^−1
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions
Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: All floating-point conversion instructions set the Inexact exception flag if the rounded result differs from the operand value and the Invalid exception flag is not set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: Floating-point to floating-point sign-injection instructions, FSGNJ.S, FSGNJN.S, and FSGNJX.S, produce a result that takes all bits except the sign bit from rs1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: For FSGNJ, the result's sign bit is rs2's sign bit; for FSGNJN, the result's sign bit is the opposite of rs2's sign bit; and for FSGNJX, the sign bit is the XOR of the sign bits of rs1 and rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: Sign-injection instructions do not set floating-point exception flags, nor do they canonicalize NaNs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: Instructions are provided to move bit patterns between the floating-point and integer registers. FMV.X.W moves the single-precision value in floating-point register rs1 represented in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding to the lower 32 bits of integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64, the higher 32 bits of the destination register are filled with copies of the floating-point number's sign bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: FMV.W.X moves the single-precision value encoded in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding from the lower 32 bits of integer register rs1 to the floating-point register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The bits are not modified in the transfer, and in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: FLT.S and FLE.S perform what IEEE 754-2008 refers to as signaling comparisons: that is, they set the invalid operation exception flag if either input is NaN. FEQ.S performs a quiet comparison: it only sets the invalid operation exception flag if either input is a signaling NaN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Compare Instructions

chunks.text: The FCLASS.S instruction examines the value in floating-point register rs1 and writes to integer register rd a 10-bit mask that indicates the class of the floating-point number.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

chunks.text: The format of the mask is described in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

chunks.text: The corresponding bit in rd will be set if the property is true and clear otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

chunks.text: All other bits in rd are cleared.
chunks.section: Preamble > "F" Extension for Single-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > F Register State > Single-Precision Floating-Point Classify Instruction

chunks.text: MUL performs an XLEN-bit×XLEN-bit multiplication of `rs1` by `rs2` and places the lower XLEN bits in the destination register. MULH, MULHU, and MULHSU perform the same multiplication but return the upper XLEN bits of the full 2×XLEN-bit product, for signed×signed, unsigned×unsigned, and `rs1`×unsigned `rs2` multiplication.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations

chunks.text: If both the high and low bits of the same product are required, then the recommended code sequence is: `MULH[[S]U] rdh, rs1, rs2; MUL rdl, rs1, rs2` (source register specifiers must be in same order and `rdh` cannot be the same as `rs1` or `rs2`).
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations

chunks.text: MULW is an RV64 instruction that multiplies the lower 32 bits of the source registers, placing the sign extension of the lower 32 bits of the result into the destination register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations

chunks.text: DIV and DIVU perform an XLEN bits by XLEN bits signed and unsigned integer division of `rs1` by `rs2`, rounding towards zero. REM and REMU provide the remainder of the corresponding division operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: If both the quotient and remainder are required from the same division, the recommended code sequence is: `DIV[U] rdq, rs1, rs2; REM[U] rdr,` `rs1, rs2` (`rdq` cannot be the same as `rs1` or `rs2`).
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: DIVW and DIVUW are RV64 instructions that divide the lower 32 bits of `rs1` by the lower 32 bits of `rs2`, treating them as signed and unsigned integers, placing the 32-bit quotient in `rd`, sign-extended to 64 bits. REMW and REMUW are RV64 instructions that provide the corresponding signed and unsigned remainder operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: Both REMW and REMUW always sign-extend the 32-bit result to 64 bits, including on a divide by zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: The quotient of division by zero has all bits set, and the remainder of division by zero equals the dividend.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: Signed division overflow occurs only when the most-negative integer is divided by −1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "M" Extension for Integer Multiplication and Division, Version 2.0 > Multiplication Operations > Division Operations

chunks.text: This appendix should be treated as commentary; all normative material is provided in and in the rest of the main body of the ISA specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material

chunks.text: To facilitate the porting of code from other architectures, some hardware implementations may choose to implement the Ztso extension, which provides stricter RVTSO ordering semantics by default.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

chunks.text: Each implementation must therefore choose whether to prioritize compatibility with RVTSO code (e.g., to facilitate porting from x86) or whether to instead prioritize compatibility with other RISC-V cores implementing RVWMO.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

chunks.text: However, these fences must remain present in the code if compatibility with non-Ztso implementations is desired.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO?

chunks.text: As a convention for this figure and for all figures that follow in this chapter, we assume that `s0-s2` are pre-set to the same value in all harts and that `s0` holds the address labeled `x`, `s1` holds `y`, and `s2` holds `z`, where `x`, `y`, and `z` are disjoint memory locations aligned to 8 byte boundaries.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Litmus Tests

chunks.text: However, in this example, the final value of `a0` in Hart 0 will never be 1 or 3; intuitively, the value 1 will no longer be visible at the time the load executes, and the value 3 will not yet be visible by the time the load executes.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Litmus Tests

chunks.text: Preserved program order represents the subset of program order that must be respected within the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: Conceptually, events from the same hart that are ordered by preserved program order must appear in that order from the perspective of other harts and/or observers.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: Events from the same hart that are not ordered by preserved program order, on the other hand, may appear reordered from the perspective of other harts and/or observers.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: Informally, the global memory order represents the order in which loads and stores perform.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: In this sense, the global memory order also represents the contribution of the coherence protocol and/or the rest of the memory system to interleave the (possibly reordered) memory accesses being issued by each hart into a single total order agreed upon by all harts.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: The order in which loads perform does not always directly correspond to the relative age of the values those two loads return.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: This discrepancy captures (among other things) the reordering effects of buffering placed between the core and memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: To account for this, at the time each load performs, the value it returns is determined by the load value axiom, not just strictly by determining the most recent store to the same address in the global memory order, as described below.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Preserved Program Order and Global Memory Order

chunks.text: Preserved program order is not required to respect the ordering of a store followed by a load to an overlapping address.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: Informally, the load may perform (return a value) by forwarding from the store while the store is still in the store buffer, and hence before the store itself performs (writes back to globally visible memory).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: When running this program on an implementation with store buffers, it is possible to arrive at the final outcome `a0=1, a1=0, a2=1, a3=0` as follows:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: Therefore, the memory model must be able to account for this behavior.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: To put it another way, suppose the definition of preserved program order did include the following hypothetical rule: memory access a precedes memory access b in preserved program order (and hence also in the global memory order) if a precedes b in program order and a and b are accesses to the same memory location, a is a write, and b is a read.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: Otherwise, if (e) preceded (d), then (d) would be required to return the value 1. (This is a perfectly legal execution; it's just not the one in question) * (e) precedes (f): by rule X * (f) precedes (h): by rule * (h) precedes (a): by the load value axiom, as above.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: The global memory order must be a total order and cannot be cyclic, because a cycle would imply that every event in the cycle happens before itself, which is impossible.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: Nevertheless, even if (b) precedes (a) and/or (f) precedes (e) in the global memory order, the only sensible possibility in this example is for (b) to return the value written by (a), and likewise for (f) and (e).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Load value axiom

chunks.text: If r and w are paired load and store operations generated by aligned LR and SC instructions in a hart h, s is a store to byte x, and r returns a value written by s, then s must precede w in the global memory order, and there can be no store from a hart other than h to byte x following s and preceding w in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

chunks.text: Unlike architectures such as TSO, RISC-V atomics under RVWMO do not impose any ordering requirements by default.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

chunks.text: These conceptually behave differently, in the following way. LR/SC behave as if the old value is brought up to the core, modified, and written back to memory, all while a reservation is held on that memory location. AMOs on the other hand conceptually behave as if they are performed directly in memory. AMOs are therefore inherently atomic, while LR/SC pairs are atomic in the slightly different sense that the memory location in question will not be modified by another hart during the time the original hart holds the reservation.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

chunks.text: Likewise, scenarios in which stores from the same hart between an LR/SC pair actually overlap the memory location(s) referenced by the LR or SC are expected to be rare compared to scenarios where the intervening store may simply fall onto the same cache line.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Atomicity axiom

chunks.text: [IMPORTANT] : No memory operation may be preceded in the global memory order by an infinite sequence of other memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

chunks.text: Implementations with non-coherent caches may have to provide some other mechanism to ensure the eventual visibility of all stores (or successors thereof) to all harts.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Progress axiom

chunks.text: Same-address orderings where the latter is a store are straightforward: a load or store can never be reordered with a later store to an overlapping memory location.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: From a microarchitecture perspective, generally speaking, it is difficult or impossible to undo a speculatively reordered store if the speculation turns out to be invalid, so such behavior is simply disallowed by the model.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Same-address orderings from a store to a later load, on the other hand, do not need to be enforced.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: As discussed in , this reflects the observable behavior of implementations that forward values from buffered stores to later loads.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Same-address load-load ordering requirements are far more subtle.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: The basic requirement is that a younger load must not return a value that is older than a value returned by an older load in the same hart to the same address.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: This is often known as "CoRR" (Coherence for Read-Read pairs), or as part of a broader "coherence" or "sequential consistency per location" requirement.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Some architectures in the past have relaxed same-address load-load ordering, but in hindsight this is generally considered to complicate the programming model too much, and so RVWMO requires CoRR ordering to be enforced.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] .Litmus test MP+fence.w.w+fre-rfi-addr (outcome permitted) | | ! 2+!Hart 0 2+^!Hart 1 !!li t1, 1 !!li t2, 2 >!(a) !sw t1,0(s0) >!(d) !lw a0,0(s1) >!(b) !fence w, w >!(e) !sw t2,0(s1) >!(c) !sw t1,0(s1) >!(f) !lw a1,0(s1) ! ! >!(g) !xor t3,a1,a1 ! ! >!(h) !add s0,s0,t3 ! ! >!(i) !lw a2,0(s0) 4+^!Outcome: `a0=1`, `a1=2`, `a2=0` ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmusmpfencewwfrirfiaddr.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmusmpfencewwfrirfiaddr.png[] ! | Consider the litmus test of , which is one particular instance of the more general "fri-rfi" pattern.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: From a microarchitectural perspective, outcome `a0=1`, `a1=2`, `a2=0` is legal (as are various other less subtle outcomes).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Intuitively, the following would produce the outcome in question:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: (d) stalls (for whatever reason; perhaps it's stalled waiting for some other preceding instruction) * (e) executes and enters the store buffer (but does not yet drain to memory) * (f) executes and forwards from (e) in the store buffer * (g), (h), and (i) execute * (a) executes and drains to memory, (b) executes, and (c) executes and drains to memory * (d) unstalls and executes * (e) drains from the store buffer to memory
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: This corresponds to a global memory order of (f), (i), (a), (c), (d), (e).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Therefore, this execution is legal and does not violate the CoRR requirements.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Likewise, if two back-to-back loads return the values written by the same store, then they may also appear out-of-order in the global memory order without violating CoRR.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Consider the litmus test of .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: The outcome `a0=1`, `a1=v`, `a2=v`, `a3=0` (where v is some value written by another hart) can be observed by allowing (g) and (h) to be reordered.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: The global memory order corresponding to this execution would be (h),(k),(a),(c),(d),(g).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Executions of the test in in which `a1` does not equal `a2` do in fact require that (g) appears before (h) in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: Therefore, forbids this CoRR violation from occurring.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: As such, strikes a careful balance between enforcing CoRR in all cases while simultaneously being weak enough to permit "RSW" and "fri-rfi" patterns that commonly appear in real microarchitectures.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: There is one more overlapping-address rule: simply states that a value cannot be returned from an AMO or SC to a subsequent load until the AMO or SC has (in the case of the SC, successfully) performed globally.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: This follows somewhat naturally from the conceptual view that both AMOs and SC instructions are meant to be performed atomically in memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: However, notably, states that hardware may not even non-speculatively forward the value being stored by an AMOSWAP to a subsequent load, even though for AMOSWAP that store value is not actually semantically dependent on the previous value in memory, as is the case for the other AMOs.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: The same holds true even when forwarding from SC store values that are not semantically dependent on the value returned by the paired LR.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: The three PPO rules above also apply when the memory accesses in question only overlap partially.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: When misaligned memory accesses are being used, the overlapping-address PPO rules apply to each of the component memory accesses independently.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Overlapping-Address Orderings (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 1-3>>)

chunks.text: [IMPORTANT] Rule : There is a FENCE instruction that orders a before b
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: By default, the FENCE instruction ensures that all memory accesses from instructions preceding the fence in program order (the "predecessor set") appear earlier in the global memory order than memory accesses from instructions appearing after the fence in program order (the "successor set").
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: However, fences can optionally further restrict the predecessor set and/or the successor set to a smaller set of memory accesses in order to provide some speedup.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: The FENCE encoding currently has nine non-trivial combinations of the four bits PR, PW, SR, and SW, plus one extra encoding FENCE.TSO which facilitates mapping of "acquire+release" or RVTSO semantics.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: The remaining seven combinations have empty predecessor and/or successor sets and hence are no-ops.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: FENCE instructions using other combinations of PR, PW, SR, and SW are not normally used in the Linux or C++ memory models but are otherwise well defined.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: Fences in RISC-V are not cumulative, as they are in some non-multi-copy-atomic memory models.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Fences (<<overlapping-ordering, Rule 4>>)

chunks.text: [IMPORTANT] : a has an acquire annotation
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: An acquire operation, as would be used at the start of a critical section, requires all memory operations following the acquire in program order to also follow the acquire in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Acquire ordering can be enforced in one of two ways: with an acquire annotation, which enforces ordering with respect to just the synchronization variable itself, or with a FENCE R,RW, which enforces ordering with respect to all previous loads.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: However, assuming `a0`, `a1`, and `a2` point to different memory locations, the loads and stores in the critical section may or may not appear after the "Arbitrary unrelated load" at the beginning of the example in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Now, consider the alternative in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: In this case, even though the AMOSWAP does not enforce ordering with an aq bit, the fence nevertheless enforces that the acquire AMOSWAP appears earlier in the global memory order than all loads and stores in the critical section.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Release orderings work exactly the same as acquire orderings, just in the opposite direction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Release semantics require all loads and stores preceding the release operation in program order to also precede the release operation in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Just as for acquire semantics, release semantics can be enforced using release annotations or with a FENCE RW,W operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: Using the same examples, the ordering between the loads and stores in the critical section and the "Arbitrary unrelated store" at the end of the code snippet is enforced only by the FENCE RW,W in , not by the rl in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: With RCpc annotations alone, store-release-to-load-acquire ordering is not enforced.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: This facilitates the porting of code written under the TSO and/or RCpc memory models.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: To enforce store-release-to-load-acquire ordering, the code must use store-release-RCsc and load-acquire-RCsc operations so that PPO rule 7 applies. RCpc alone is sufficient for many use cases in C/C\++ but is insufficient for many other use cases in C/C++, Java, and Linux, to name just a few examples; see for details.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: PPO rule 8 indicates that an SC must appear after its paired LR in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: This will follow naturally from the common use of LR/SC to perform an atomic read-modify-write operation due to the inherent data dependency.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: However, PPO rule 8 also applies even when the value being stored does not syntactically depend on the value returned by the paired LR.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Explicit Synchronization (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 5-8>>)

chunks.text: [IMPORTANT] : b has a syntactic address dependency on a
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: : b is a store, and b has a syntactic control dependency on a
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Dependencies from a load to a later memory operation in the same hart are respected by the RVWMO memory model.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: The Alpha memory model was notable for choosing not to enforce the ordering of such dependencies, but most modern hardware and software memory models consider allowing dependent instructions to be reordered too confusing and counterintuitive.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Furthermore, modern code sometimes intentionally uses such dependencies as a particularly lightweight ordering enforcement mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Instructions are said to carry dependencies from their source register(s) to their destination register(s) whenever the value written into each destination register is a function of the source register(s).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: For most instructions, this means that the destination register(s) carry a dependency from all source register(s).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: In the case of memory instructions, the value written into the destination register ultimately comes from the memory system rather than from the source register(s) directly, and so this breaks the chain of dependencies carried from the source register(s).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: In the case of unconditional jumps, the value written into the destination register comes from the current `pc` (which is never considered a source register by the memory model), and so likewise, JALR (the only jump with a source register) does not carry a dependency from rs1 to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Like other modern memory models, the RVWMO memory model uses syntactic rather than semantic dependencies.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: In other words, this definition depends on the identities of the registers being accessed by different instructions, not the actual contents of those registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)
