chunks.text: This means that an address, control, or data dependency must be enforced even if the calculation could seemingly be `optimized away`.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: This choice ensures that RVWMO remains compatible with code that uses these false syntactic dependencies as a lightweight ordering mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: The benefit of using dependencies as a lightweight synchronization mechanism is that the ordering enforcement requirement is limited only to the specific two instructions in question.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Other non-dependent instructions may be freely reordered by aggressive implementations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: One alternative would be to use a load-acquire, but this would enforce ordering for the first load with respect to all subsequent instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Control dependencies behave differently from address and data dependencies in the sense that a control dependency always extends to all instructions following the original target in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Consider the instruction at `next` will always execute, but the memory operation generated by that last instruction nevertheless still has a control dependency from the memory operation generated by the first instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Even though both branch outcomes have the same target, there is still a control dependency from the memory operation generated by the first instruction in this snippet to the memory operation generated by the last instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: However, since control, address, and data dependencies are defined over memory operations, and since an unsuccessful SC does not generate a memory operation, no order is enforced between unsuccessful SC and its dependent instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Moreover, since SC is defined to carry dependencies from its source registers to rd only when the SC is successful, an unsuccessful SC has no effect on the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: In addition, the choice to respect dependencies originating at store-conditional instructions ensures that certain out-of-thin-air-like behaviors will be prevented.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Suppose a hypothetical implementation could occasionally make some early guarantee that a store-conditional operation will succeed.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: In this case, (c) could return 0 to `a2` early (before actually executing), allowing the sequence (d), (e), (f), (a), and then (b) to execute, and then (c) might execute (successfully) only at that point.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: For example: a syntactic dependency between two `F` instructions via one of the `accumulating CSRs` in does not imply that the two `F` instructions must be executed in order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: Such a dependency would only serve to ultimately set up later a dependency from both `F` instructions to a later CSR instruction accessing the CSR flag in question.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Syntactic Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 9-11>>)

chunks.text: [IMPORTANT] : b is a load, and there exists some store m between a and b in program order such that m has an address or data dependency on a, and b returns a value written by m
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: : b is a store, and there exists some instruction m between a and b in program order such that m has an address dependency on a
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: PPO rules and reflect behaviors of almost all real processor pipeline implementations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Rule states that a load cannot forward from a store until the address and data for that store are known.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Consider (f) cannot be executed until the data for (e) has been resolved, because (f) must return the value written by (e) (or by something even later in the global memory order), and the old value must not be clobbered by the write-back of (e) before (d) has had a chance to perform.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Therefore, (f) will never perform before (d) has performed.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: [float="center",align="center",cols=".^1a,.^1a",frame="none",grid="none",options="noheader"] | | ! 2+!Hart 0 2+!Hart 1 2+!li t1, 1 2+^!li t1, 1 !(a) !sw t1,0(s0) !(d) !lw a0, 0(s1) !(b) !fence w, w !(e) !sw a0, 0(s2) !(c) !sw t1,0(s1) !(f) !sw t1, 0(s2) ! ! !(g) !lw a1, 0(s2) ! ! ! !xor a2,a1,a1 ! ! ! !add s0,s0,a2 ! ! !(h) !lw a3,0(s0) 4+!Outcome: `a0=1`, `a3=0` ! | ! //a! graphviz::images/graphviz/litmusdatacoirfi.txt[] a! image::graphviz/litmusdatacoirfi.png[] ! |
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Rule makes a similar observation to the previous rule: a store cannot be performed at memory until all previous loads that might access the same address have themselves been performed.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Such a load must appear to execute before the store, but it cannot do so if the store were to overwrite the value in memory before the load had a chance to read the old value.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Likewise, a store generally cannot be performed until it is known that preceding instructions will not cause an exception due to failed address resolution, and in this sense, rule 13 can be seen as somewhat of a special case of rule .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: Therefore, (f) cannot be sent to memory before (d) has executed and confirmed whether the addresses do indeed overlap.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Explaining the RVWMO Rules > Pipeline Dependencies (<<overlapping-ordering, Rules 12-13>>)

chunks.text: The RISC-V Privileged ISA defines Physical Memory Attributes (PMAs) which specify, among other things, whether portions of the address space are coherent and/or cacheable.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: See the RISC-V Privileged ISA Specification for the complete details.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: Here, we simply discuss how the various details in each PMA relate to the memory model:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: Non-cacheable regions may have more restrictive behavior than cacheable regions, but the set of allowed behaviors does not change regardless.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: However, some platform-specific and/or device-specific cacheability settings may differ. * Coherence PMAs: The memory consistency model for memory regions marked as non-coherent in PMAs is currently platform-specific and/or device-specific: the load-value axiom, the atomicity axiom, and the progress axiom all may be violated with non-coherent memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: This distinction does not affect the memory model.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > Coherence and Cacheability

chunks.text: Nevertheless, the following preserved program order rules still generally apply for accesses to I/O memory: memory access a precedes memory access b in global memory order if a precedes b in program order and one or more of the following holds:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: To enforce ordering between I/O operations and main memory operations, code must use a FENCE with PI, PO, SI, and/or SO, plus PR, PW, SR, and/or SW.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: When a fence is in fact used, implementations must assume that the device may attempt to access memory immediately after receiving the MMIO signal, and subsequent memory accesses from that device to memory must observe the effects of all accesses ordered prior to that MMIO operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: In other words, in , suppose `0(a0)` is in main memory and `0(a1)` is the address of a device register in I/O memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: If the device accesses `0(a0)` upon receiving the MMIO write, then that load must conceptually appear after the first store to `0(a0)` according to the rules of the RVWMO memory model.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Other implementations may find ways to be more aggressive, while others still may not need to do anything different at all for I/O and main memory accesses.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Nevertheless, the RVWMO memory model does not distinguish between these options; it simply provides an implementation-agnostic mechanism to specify the orderings that must be enforced.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Many architectures include separate notions of "ordering" and "completion" fences, especially as it relates to I/O (as opposed to regular main memory).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Ordering fences simply ensure that memory operations stay in order, while completion fences ensure that predecessor accesses have all completed before any successors are made visible. RISC-V does not explicitly distinguish between ordering and completion fences.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Instead, this distinction is simply inferred from different uses of the FENCE bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: For implementations that conform to the RISC-V Unix Platform Specification, I/O devices and DMA operations are required to access memory coherently and via strongly ordered I/O channels.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Therefore, accesses to regular main memory regions that are concurrently accessed by external devices can also use the standard synchronization mechanisms.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Implementations that do not conform to the Unix Platform Specification and/or in which devices do not access memory coherently will need to use mechanisms (which are currently platform-specific or device-specific) to enforce coherency.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: I/O regions in the address space should be considered non-cacheable regions in the PMAs for those regions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Such regions can be considered coherent by the PMA if they are not cached by any agent.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: The ordering guarantees in this section may not apply beyond a platform-specific boundary between the RISC-V cores and the device.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Ordering must be enforced in such situations according to the platform-specific rules of those external devices and buses.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Beyond Main Memory > I/O Ordering

chunks.text: Normal x86 loads and stores are all inherently acquire-RCpc and release-RCpc operations: TSO enforces all load-load, load-store, and store-store ordering by default.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Therefore, under RVWMO, all TSO loads must be mapped onto a load followed by FENCE R,RW, and all TSO stores must be mapped onto FENCE RW,W followed by a store. TSO atomic read-modify-writes and x86 instructions using the LOCK prefix are fully ordered and can be implemented either via an AMO with both aq and rl set, or via an LR with aq set, the arithmetic operation in question, an SC with both aq and rl set, and a conditional branch checking the success condition.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: However, since RVWMO PPO Rule forbids forwarding of values from AMOs to subsequent loads, the use of AMOSWAP for stores may negatively affect performance. A TSO load can be mapped using LR with aq set: all such LR instructions will be unpaired, but that fact in and of itself does not preclude the use of LR for loads.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: However, again, this mapping may also negatively affect performance if it puts more pressure on the reservation mechanism than was originally intended.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Since RISC-V does not currently have plain load and store opcodes with aq or rl annotations, ARM load-acquire and store-release operations should be mapped using fences instead.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Furthermore, in order to enforce store-release-to-load-acquire ordering, there must be a FENCE RW,RW between the store-release and load-acquire; enforces this by always placing the fence in front of each acquire operation. ARM load-exclusive and store-exclusive instructions can likewise map onto their RISC-V LR and SC equivalents, but instead of placing a FENCE RW,RW in front of an LR with aq set, we simply also set rl instead. ARM ISB maps on RISC-V to FENCE.I followed by FENCE R,R similarly to how ISYNC maps for Power.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: With regards to , other constructs (such as spinlocks) should follow accordingly.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Platforms or devices with non-coherent DMA may need additional synchronization (such as cache flush or invalidate mechanisms); currently any such extra synchronization will be device-specific.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Platforms with non-coherent DMA may also require a mechanism by which cache lines can be flushed and/or invalidated.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: The Linux mappings for release operations may seem stronger than necessary, but these mappings are needed to cover some cases in which Linux requires stronger orderings than the more intuitive mappings would provide.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: If load and store opcodes with aq and rl modifiers are introduced, then the mappings in will suffice.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Even more importantly, a sequentially consistent store, followed by a sequentially consistent load can be reordered unless the mapping of stores is strengthened by either adding a second fence or mapping the store to `amoswap.rl` instead.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: 2+!^*^ must be `lr.{w|d}.aqrl` in order to interoperate with code mapped per |
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Any AMO can be emulated by an LR/SC pair, but care must be taken to ensure that any PPO orderings that originate from the LR are also made to originate from the SC, and that any PPO orderings that terminate at the SC are also made to terminate at the LR.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: For example, the LR must also be made to respect any data dependencies that the AMO has, given that load operations do not otherwise have any notion of a data dependency.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: Likewise, the effect a FENCE R,R elsewhere in the same hart must also be made to apply to the SC, which would not otherwise respect that fence.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Code Porting and Mapping Guidelines

chunks.text: That said, to help people understand the actual implementations of the memory model, in this section we provide some guidelines on how architects and programmers should interpret the models' rules.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Both RVWMO and RVTSO are multi-copy atomic (or other-multi-copy-atomic): any store value that is visible to a hart other than the one that originally issued it must also be conceptually visible to all other harts in the system.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: In other words, harts may forward from their own previous stores before those stores have become globally visible to all harts, but no early inter-hart forwarding is permitted.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Multi-copy atomicity may be enforced in a number of ways.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: It might hold inherently due to the physical design of the caches and store buffers, it may be enforced via a single-writer/multiple-reader cache coherence protocol, or it might hold due to some other mechanism.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Nor may a cache coherence protocol forward a value from one hart to another until the coherence protocol has invalidated all older copies from other caches.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Of course, microarchitectures may (and high-performance implementations likely will) violate these rules under the covers through speculation or other optimizations, as long as any non-compliant behaviors are not exposed to the programmer.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: programmers will use PPO rules and regularly and actively. * expert programmers will use PPO rules to speed up critical paths of important data structures. * even expert programmers will rarely if ever use PPO rules and directly.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: PPO rules and reflect well-understood rules that should pose few surprises to architects. * PPO rule reflects a natural and common hardware optimization, but one that is very subtle and hence is worth double checking carefully. * PPO rule may not be immediately obvious to architects, but it is a standard memory model requirement * The load value axiom, the atomicity axiom, and PPO rules reflect rules that most hardware implementations will enforce naturally, unless they contain extreme optimizations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Of course, implementations should make sure to double check these rules nevertheless.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Hardware must also ensure that syntactic dependencies are not `optimized away`.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Ignore all fences that do not have both PW and SR (unless the fence also orders I/O) * Ignore all PPO rules except for rules through , since the rest are redundant with other PPO rules under RVTSO assumptions
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: Microarchitectures that attempt to implement silent stores must take care to ensure that the memory model is still obeyed, particularly in cases such as RSW which tend to be incompatible with silent stores. * Writes may be merged (i.e., two consecutive writes to the same address may be merged) or subsumed (i.e., the earlier of two back-to-back writes to the same address may be elided) as long as the resulting behavior does not otherwise violate the memory model semantics.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: As written, if the load (d) reads value 1, then (a) must precede (f) in the global memory order:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: In other words the final value of the memory location whose address is in `s0` must be 2 (the value written by the store (f)) and cannot be 3 (the value written by the store (a)).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Implementation Guidelines

chunks.text: To address this, we may choose to add something like the following new PPO rule: Memory operation a precedes memory operation b in preserved program order (and hence also in the global memory order) if a precedes b in program order, a and b both access regular main memory (rather than I/O regions), a is a load, b is a store, there is a load m between a and b, there is a byte x that both a and m read, there is no store between a and m that writes to x, and m precedes b in PPO.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

chunks.text: In other words, in herd syntax, we may choose to add `(po-loc & rsw);ppo;[W]` to PPO.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Explanatory Material > Why RVWMO? > Known Issues > Mixed-size RSW

chunks.text: This appendix should be treated as commentary; all normative material is provided in and in the rest of the main body of the ISA specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1

chunks.text: We present a formal specification of the RVWMO memory model in Alloy (http://alloy.mit.edu).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: This model is available online at https://github.com/daniellustig/riscv-memory-model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: sig Hart { // hardware thread start : one Event } sig Address {} abstract sig Event { po: lone Event // program order }
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: abstract sig MemoryEvent extends Event { address: one Address, acquireRCpc: lone MemoryEvent, acquireRCsc: lone MemoryEvent, releaseRCpc: lone MemoryEvent, releaseRCsc: lone MemoryEvent, addrdep: set MemoryEvent, ctrldep: set Event, datadep: set MemoryEvent, gmo: set MemoryEvent, // global memory order rf: set MemoryEvent } sig LoadNormal extends MemoryEvent {} // l{b|h|w|d} sig LoadReserve extends MemoryEvent { // lr pair: lone StoreConditional } sig StoreNormal extends MemoryEvent {} // s{b|h|w|d} // all StoreConditionals in the model are assumed to be successful sig StoreConditional extends MemoryEvent {} // sc sig AMO extends MemoryEvent {} // amo sig NOP extends Event {}
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: fun Load : Event { LoadNormal + LoadReserve + AMO } fun Store : Event { StoreNormal + StoreConditional + AMO }
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: sig Fence extends Event { pr: lone Fence, // opcode bit pw: lone Fence, // opcode bit sr: lone Fence, // opcode bit sw: lone Fence // opcode bit } sig FenceTSO extends Fence {}
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: pred restricttocurrentencodings { no (LoadNormal + StoreNormal) & (Acquire + Release) }
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy

chunks.text: The tool [.sans-serif]herd takes a memory model and a litmus test as input and simulates the execution of the test on top of the memory model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: Memory models are written in the domain specific language Cat.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: This section provides two Cat memory model of RVWMO.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: The first model, , follows the global memory order, , definition of RVWMO, as much as is possible for a Cat model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: The second model, , is an equivalent, more efficient, partial order based RVWMO model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: The simulator `herd` is part of the `diy` tool suite — see http://diy.inria.fr for software and documentation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Same address, no W to the same address in-between *) let po-loc-no-w = po-loc \ (po-loc?;[W];po-loc) (* Read same write *) let rsw = rf^-1;rf (* Acquire, or stronger *) let AQ = Acq|AcqRel (* Release or stronger *) and RL = RelAcqRel (* All RCsc *) let RCsc = Acq|Rel|AcqRel (* Amo events are both R and W, relation rmw relates paired lr/sc *) let AMO = R & W let StCond = range(rmw)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Overlapping-Address Orderings *) let r1 = [M];po-loc;[W] and r2 = ([R];po-loc-no-w;[R]) \ rsw and r3 = [AMO|StCond];rfi;[R] (* Explicit Synchronization *) and r4 = fence and r5 = [AQ];po;[M] and r6 = [M];po;[RL] and r7 = [RCsc];po;[RCsc] and r8 = rmw (* Syntactic Dependencies *) and r9 = [M];addr;[M] and r10 = [M];data;[W] and r11 = [M];ctrl;[W] (* Pipeline Dependencies *) and r12 = [R];(addr|data);[W];rfi;[R] and r13 = [R];addr;[M];po;[W]
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Notice that herd has defined its own rf relation *)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd
Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: let gmo0 = (* precursor: ie build gmo as an total order that include gmo0 *) loc & (W\FW) * FW | Final write after any write to the same location ppo | ppo compatible rfe includes herd external rf (optimization)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Walk over all linear extensions of gmo0 *) with gmo from linearizations(M\IW,gmo0)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Add initial writes upfront -- convenient for computing rfGMO *) let gmo = gmo | loc & IW * (M\IW)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Compute rf according to the load value axiom, aka rfGMO *) let WR = loc & ([W];(gmo|po);[R]) let rfGMO = WR \ (loc&([W];gmo);WR)
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Check equality of herd rf and of rfGMO *) empty (rf\rfGMO)|(rfGMO\rf) as RfCons
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Compute coherence relation *) include "cos-opt.cat"
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Sc per location *) acyclic co|rf|fr|po-loc as Coherence
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: (* Main model axiom *) acyclic co|rfe|fr|ppo as Model
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Herd

chunks.text: This is an alternative presentation of the RVWMO memory model in operational style.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: It aims to admit exactly the same extensional behavior as the axiomatic presentation: for any given program, admitting an execution if and only if the axiomatic presentation allows it.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The axiomatic presentation is defined as a predicate on complete candidate executions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: In contrast, this operational presentation has an abstract microarchitectural flavor: it is expressed as a state machine, with states that are an abstract representation of hardware machine states, and with explicit out-of-order and speculative execution (but abstracting from more implementation-specific microarchitectural details such as register renaming, store buffers, cache hierarchies, cache protocols, etc.).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: It can also construct executions incrementally, making it possible to interactively and randomly explore the behavior of larger examples, while the axiomatic model requires complete candidate executions over which the axioms can be checked.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The operational presentation covers mixed-size execution, with potentially overlapping memory accesses of different power-of-two byte sizes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: In `rmem`, the ISA semantics is expressed explicitly in Sail (see https://github.com/rems-project/sail for the Sail language, and https://github.com/rems-project/sail-riscv for the RISC-V ISA model), and the concurrency semantics is expressed in Lem (see https://github.com/rems-project/lem for the Lem language).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: `rmem` has a command-line interface and a web-interface.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The web-interface runs entirely on the client side, and is provided online together with a library of litmus tests: http://www.cl.cam.ac.uk/.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The command-line interface is faster than the web-interface, specially in exhaustive mode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Terminology: In contrast to the axiomatic presentation, here every memory operation is either a load or a store.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Hence, AMOs give rise to two distinct memory operations, a load and a store.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: When used in conjunction with `instruction`, the terms `load` and `store` refer to instructions that give rise to such memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The term `acquire` refers to an instruction (or its memory operation) with the acquire-RCpc or acquire-RCsc annotation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The term `release` refers to an instruction (or its memory operation) with the release-RCpc or release-RCsc annotation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Model states: A model state consists of a shared memory and a tuple of hart states.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The shared memory state records all the memory store operations that have propagated so far, in the order they propagated (this can be made more efficient, but for simplicity of the presentation we keep it this way).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Each hart state consists principally of a tree of instruction instances, some of which have been finished, and some of which have not.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Conditional branch and indirect jump instructions may have multiple successors in the instruction tree.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Each instruction instance in the instruction tree has a state that includes an execution state of the intra-instruction semantics (the ISA pseudocode for this instruction).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: One can think of the execution state of an instruction as a representation of the pseudocode control state, pseudocode call stack, and local variable values.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: An instruction instance state also includes information about the instance's memory and register footprints, its register reads and writes, its memory operations, whether it is finished, etc.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The model defines, for any model state, the set of allowed transitions, each of which is a single atomic step to a new abstract machine state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Each transition arises from a single instruction instance; it will change the state of that instance, and it may depend on or change the rest of its hart state and the shared memory state, but it does not depend on other hart states, and it will not change them.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The transitions are introduced below and defined in , with a precondition and a construction of the post-transition model state for each.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : This transition represents a fetch and decode of a new instruction instance, as a program order successor of a previously fetched instruction instance (or the initial fetch address).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The model assumes the instruction memory is fixed; it does not describe the behavior of self-modifying code.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: In particular, the transition does not generate memory load operations, and the shared memory is not involved in the transition.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: Instead, the model depends on an external oracle that provides an opcode when given a memory location.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : This is a read of a register value from the most recent program-order-predecessor instruction instance that writes to that register.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : This covers pseudocode internal computation: arithmetic, function calls, etc.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : At this point the instruction pseudocode is done, the instruction cannot be restarted, memory accesses cannot be discarded, and all memory effects have taken place.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: For conditional branch and indirect jump instructions, any program order successors that were fetched from an address that is not the one that was written to the pc register are discarded, together with the sub-tree of instruction instances below them.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [circle] * : At this point the memory footprint of the load instruction is provisionally known (it could change if earlier instructions are restarted) and its individual memory load operations can start being satisfied.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [disc] * : This partially or entirely satisfies a single memory load operation by forwarding, from program-order-previous memory store operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : This entirely satisfies the outstanding slices of a single memory load operation, from memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [circle] * : At this point all the memory load operations of the instruction have been entirely satisfied and the instruction pseudocode can continue executing. A load instruction can be subject to being restarted until the transition.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [circle] * : At this point the memory footprint of the store is provisionally known.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : At this point the memory store operations have their values and program-order-successor memory load operations can be satisfied by forwarding from them.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : At this point the store operations are guaranteed to happen (the instruction can no longer be restarted or discarded), and they can start being propagated to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [disc] * : This propagates a single memory store operation to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [circle] * : At this point all the memory store operations of the instruction have been propagated to memory, and the instruction pseudocode can continue executing.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: : This is an atomic execution of the transitions and , it is enabled only if the stores from which the `lr` read from have not been overwritten.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: [disc] * : This is an atomic execution of all the transitions needed to satisfy the load operation, do the required arithmetic, and propagate the store operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The transitions labeled {circ} can always be taken eagerly, as soon as their precondition is satisfied, without excluding other behavior; the {bullet} cannot.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: This concludes the informal description of the operational model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The following sections describe the formal operational model.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model

chunks.text: The intra-instruction semantics for each instruction instance is expressed as a state machine, essentially running the instruction pseudocode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Given a pseudocode execution state, it computes the next state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Most states identify a pending memory or register operation, requested by the pseudocode, which the memory model has to do.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: [cols="<,<",grid="none"] | |Loadmem(kind, address, size, loadcontinuation) |- memory load operation
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: |Earlyscfail(rescontinuation) |- allow `sc` to fail early
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: |Storeea(kind, address, size, nextstate) |- memory store effective address
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: for load/store, kind identifies whether it is `lr/sc`, acquire-RCpc/release-RCpc, acquire-RCsc/release-RCsc, acquire-release-RCsc; * for fence, kind identifies whether it is a normal or TSO, and (for normal fences) the predecessor and successor ordering bits; * regname identifies a register and a slice thereof (start and end bit indices); and the continuations describe how the instruction instance will continue for each value that might be provided by the surrounding memory model (the loadcontinuation and readcontinuation take the value loaded from memory and read from the previous register write, the storecontinuation takes false for an `sc` that failed and true in all other cases, and rescontinuation takes false if the `sc` fails and true otherwise).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Notice that writing to memory is split into two steps, Storeea and Storememv: the first one makes the memory footprint of the store provisionally known, and the second one adds the value to be stored.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: We ensure these are paired in the pseudocode (Storeea followed by Storememv), but there may be other steps between them.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: The pseudocode of each instruction performs at most one store or one load, except for AMOs that perform exactly one load and one store.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Those memory accesses are then split apart into the architecturally atomic units by the hart semantics (see and below).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Informally, each bit of a register read should be satisfied from a register write by the most recent (in program order) instruction instance that can write that bit (or from the hart’s initial register state if there is no such write).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: We ensure in the pseudocode that each instruction does at most one register write to each register bit, and also that it does not try to read a register value it just wrote.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Data-flow dependencies (address and data) in the model emerge from the fact that each register read has to wait for the appropriate register write to be executed (as described above).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Intra-instruction Pseudocode Execution

chunks.text: Each instruction instance i has a state comprising:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: [cols="<,<",grid="none"] | |Plain(isastate) |- ready to make a pseudocode transition
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: |Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation) |- requesting memory store operation(s) | * regreads, the register reads the instance has performed, including, for each one, the register write slices it read from; * regwrites, the register writes the instance has performed; * memloads, a set of memory load operations, and for each one the as-yet-unsatisfied slices (the byte indices that have not been satisfied yet), and, for the satisfied slices, the store slices (each consisting of a memory store operation and subset of its byte indices) that satisfied it. * memstores, a set of memory store operations, and for each one a flag that indicates whether it has been propagated (passed to the shared memory) or not. * information recording whether the instance is committed, finished, etc.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: Each memory load operation includes a memory footprint (address and size).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: Each memory store operations includes a memory footprint, and, when available, a value.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: Similarly, it is said to have a fully determined memory footprint if the load (and `sc`) instructions feeding its memory operation address register are finished.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: Formally, we first define the notion of fully determined register write: a register write w from regwrites of instruction instance i is said to be fully determined if one of the following conditions hold:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: Now, an instruction instance i is said to have fully determined data if for every register read r from regreads, the register writes that r reads from are fully determined.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: An instruction instance i is said to have a fully determined memory footprint if for every register read r from regreads that feeds into i’s memory operation address, the register writes that r reads from are fully determined.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Instruction Instance State

chunks.text: The model state of a single hart comprises:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Hart State

chunks.text: hartid, a unique identifier of the hart; * initialregisterstate, the initial register value for each register; * initialfetchaddress, the initial instruction fetch address; * instructiontree, a tree of the instruction instances that have been fetched (and not discarded), in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Hart State

chunks.text: The model state of the shared memory comprises a list of memory store operations, in the order they propagated to the shared memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

chunks.text: When a store operation is propagated to the shared memory it is simply added to the end of the list.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

chunks.text: When a load operation is satisfied from memory, for each byte of the load operation, the most recent corresponding store slice is returned.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Shared Memory State

chunks.text: The description starts with a condition over the current system state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

chunks.text: The transition can be taken in the current state only if the condition is satisfied.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

chunks.text: The condition is followed by an action that is applied to that state when the transition is taken, in order to generate the new system state.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions

chunks.text: A possible program-order-successor of instruction instance i can be fetched from address loc if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: Action: construct a freshly initialized instruction instance i' for the instruction in the program memory at loc, with state Plain(isastate), computed from the instruction pseudocode, including the static information available from the pseudocode such as its instructionkind, srcregs, and dstregs, and add i' to the hart’s instructiontree as a successor of i.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: For most instructions these addresses are easily obtained from the instruction pseudocode.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: In principle the mathematical model should allow speculation to arbitrary addresses here.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: The exhaustive search in the `rmem` tool handles this by running the exhaustive search multiple times with a growing set of possible next fetch addresses for each indirect jump.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: The initial search uses empty sets, hence there is no fetch after indirect jump instruction until the pseudocode of the instruction writes to pc, and then we use that value for fetching the next instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: Before starting the next iteration of exhaustive search, we collect for each indirect jump (grouped by code location) the set of values it wrote to pc in all the executions in the previous search iteration, and use that as possible next fetch addresses of the instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Fetch instruction

chunks.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Loadmem(kind, address, size, loadcontinuation)) can always initiate the corresponding memory load operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Initiate memory load operations

chunks.text: For a non-AMO load instruction instance i in state Pendingmemloads(loadcontinuation), and a memory load operation mlo in i.memloads that has unsatisfied slices, the memory load operation can be partially or entirely satisfied by forwarding from unpropagated memory store operations by store instruction instances that are program-order-before i if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

chunks.text: Let msoss be the set of all unpropagated memory store operation slices from non-`sc` store instruction instances that are program-order-before i and have already calculated the value to be stored, that overlap with the unsatisfied slices of mlo, and which are not superseded by intervening store operations or store operations that are read from by an intervening load.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

chunks.text: The last condition requires, for each memory store operation slice msos in msoss from instruction i':
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

chunks.text: that there is no store instruction program-order-between i and i' with a memory store operation overlapping msos; and * that there is no load instruction program-order-between i and i' that was satisfied from an overlapping memory store operation slice from a different hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

chunks.text: program-order-successors of j that have data-flow dependency on a register write of j; * program-order-successors of j that have a memory load operation that reads from a memory store operation of j (by forwarding); * if j is a load-acquire, all the program-order-successors of j; * if j is a load, for every `fence`, f, with `.sr` and `.pr` set, and `.pw` not set, that is a program-order-successor of j, all the load instructions that are program-order-successors of f; * if j is a load, for every `fence.tso`, f, that is a program-order-successor of j, all the load instructions that are program-order-successors of f; and * (recursively) all the restart-dependents of all the instruction instances above.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores

chunks.text: For an instruction instance i of a non-AMO load instruction or an AMO instruction in the context of the transition, any memory load operation mlo in i.memloads that has unsatisfied slices, can be satisfied from memory if all the conditions of <satbyforwarding, Satisfy memory load operation by forwarding from unpropagated stores>> are satisfied.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation from memory

chunks.text: Action: let msoss be the memory store operation slices from memory covering the unsatisfied slices of mlo, and apply the action of .
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy memory load operation from memory

chunks.text: Action: update the state of i to Plain(loadcontinuation(memvalue)), where memvalue is assembled from all the memory store operation slices that satisfied i.memloads.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Complete load operations

chunks.text: An `sc` instruction instance i in state Plain(Earlyscfail(rescontinuation)) can always be made to fail.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Early `sc` fail

chunks.text: Action: update the state of i to Plain(rescontinuation(false)).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Early `sc` fail

chunks.text: An `sc` instruction instance i in state Plain(Earlyscfail(rescontinuation)) can continue its (potentially successful) execution if i is paired with an `lr`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Paired `sc`

chunks.text: Action: update the state of i to Plain(rescontinuation(true)).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Paired `sc`

chunks.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Storeea(kind, address, size, nextstate)) can always announce its pending memory store operation footprint.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Initiate memory store operation footprints

chunks.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Storememv(memvalue, storecontinuation)) can always instantiate the values of the memory store operations i.memstores.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Instantiate memory store operation values

chunks.text: An uncommitted instruction instance i of a non-`sc` store instruction or an `sc` instruction in the context of the transition, in state Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation), can be committed (not to be confused with propagated) if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Commit store instruction

chunks.text: Action: record that i is committed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Commit store instruction

chunks.text: For a committed instruction instance i in state Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation), and an unpropagated memory store operation mso in i.memstores, mso can be propagated if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Propagate store operation

chunks.text: An uncommitted `sc` instruction instance i, from hart h, in state Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation), with a paired `lr` i' that has been satisfied by some store slices msoss, can be committed and propagated at the same time if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Commit and propagate store operation of an `sc`

chunks.text: An `sc` instruction instance i in state Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation), that has not propagated its memory store operation, can always be made to fail.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Late `sc` fail

chunks.text: A store instruction instance i in state Pendingmemstores(storecontinuation), for which all the memory store operations in i.memstores have been propagated, can always be completed (not to be confused with finished).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Complete store operations

chunks.text: Action: update the state of i to Plain(storecontinuation(true)).
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Complete store operations

chunks.text: An AMO instruction instance i in state Pendingmemloads(loadcontinuation) can perform its memory access if it is possible to perform the following sequence of transitions with no intervening transitions:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy, commit and propagate operations of an AMO

chunks.text: and in addition, the condition of , with the exception of not requiring i to be in state Plain(Done), holds after those transitions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy, commit and propagate operations of an AMO

chunks.text: Action: perform the above sequence of transitions (this does not include ), one after the other, with no intervening transitions.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Satisfy, commit and propagate operations of an AMO

chunks.text: A fence instruction instance i in state Plain(Fence(kind, nextstate)) can be committed if:
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Commit fence

chunks.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Readreg(regname, readcont)) can do a register read of regname if every instruction instance that it needs to read from has already performed the expected regname register write.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Register read

chunks.text: Let readsources include, for each bit of regname, the write to that bit by the most recent (in program order) instruction instance that can write to that bit, if any.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Register read

chunks.text: If there is no such instruction, the source is the initial register value from initialregisterstate.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Register read

chunks.text: Let regvalue be the value assembled from readsources.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Register read

chunks.text: where deps is a pair of the set of all readsources from i.regreads, and a flag that is true iff i is a load instruction instance that has already been entirely satisfied.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Register write

chunks.text: An instruction instance i in state Plain(Internal(nextstate)) can always do that pseudocode-internal step.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Transitions > Pseudocode internal step

chunks.text: The model covers user-level RV64I and RV64A.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Limitations

chunks.text: In particular, it does not support the misaligned atomicity granule PMA or the total store ordering extension "Ztso".
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Limitations

chunks.text: This will involve, mostly, writing Sail code for the instructions, with minimal, if any, changes to the concurrency model. * The model covers only normal memory accesses (it does not handle I/O accesses). * The model does not cover TLB-related effects. * The model assumes the instruction memory is fixed.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Limitations

chunks.text: In particular, the transition does not generate memory load operations, and the shared memory is not involved in the transition.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Limitations

chunks.text: Instead, the model depends on an external oracle that provides an opcode when given a memory location. * The model does not cover exceptions, traps and interrupts.
chunks.section: Preamble > Formal Memory Model Specifications, Version 0.1 > Formal Axiomatic Specification in Alloy > An Operational Memory Model > Limitations

chunks.text: However, it remains the only standard instruction-fetch coherence mechanism. * Removed prohibitions on using RV32E with other extensions. * Removed platform-specific mandates that certain encodings produce illegal-instruction exceptions in RV32E and RV64I chapters. * Counter/timer instructions are now not considered part of the mandatory base ISA, and so CSR instructions were moved into separate chapter and marked as version 2.0, with the unprivileged counters moved into another separate chapter.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: The counters are not ready for ratification as there are outstanding issues, including counter inaccuracies. * A CSR-access ordering model has been added. * Explicitly defined the 16-bit half-precision floating-point format for floating-point instructions in the 2-bit fmt field. * Defined the signed-zero behavior of FMIN.fmt and FMAX.fmt, and changed their behavior on signaling-NaN inputs to conform to the `minimumNumber` and `maximumNumber` operations in the proposed IEEE 754-201x specification. * The memory consistency model, RVWMO, has been defined. * The "Zam" extension, which permits misaligned AMOs and specifies their semantics, has been defined. * The "Ztso" extension, which enforces a stricter memory consistency model than RVWMO, has been defined. * Improvements to the description and commentary. * Defined the term `IALIGN` as shorthand to describe the instruction-address alignment constraint. * Removed text of `P` extension chapter as now superseded by active task group documents. * Removed text of `V` extension chapter as now superseded by separate vector extension draft document.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: Numerous additions and improvements to the commentary sections. * Separate version numbers for each chapter. * Modification to long instruction encodings >64 bits to avoid moving the rd specifier in very long instruction formats. * CSR instructions are now described in the base integer format where the counter registers are introduced, as opposed to only being introduced later in the floating-point section (and the companion privileged architecture manual). * The SCALL and SBREAK instructions have been renamed to `ECALL` and `EBREAK`, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface
