chunks.text: The ISA has been divided into an integer base with several standard extensions. * The instruction formats have been rearranged to make immediate encoding more efficient. * The base ISA has been defined to have a little-endian memory system, with big-endian or bi-endian as non-standard variants. * Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional (`LR/SC`) instructions have been added in the atomic instruction extension. * `AMOs` and `LR/SC` can support the release consistency model. * The `FENCE` instruction provides finer-grain memory and I/O orderings. * An `AMO` for fetch-and-`XOR` (`AMOXOR`) has been added, and the encoding for `AMOSWAP` has been changed to make room. * The `AUIPC` instruction, which adds a 20-bit upper immediate to the `PC`, replaces the `RDNPC` instruction, which only read the current `PC` value.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: This change simplifies datapath design. * `FCLASS.S` and `FCLASS.D` floating-point classify instructions have been added. * A simpler NaN generation and propagation scheme has been adopted. * For `RV32I`, the system performance counters have been extended to 64-bits wide, with separate read access to the upper and lower 32 bits. * Canonical `NOP` and `MV` encodings have been defined. * Standard instruction-length encodings have been defined for 48-bit, 64-bit, and >64-bit instructions. * Description of a 128-bit address space variant, `RV128`, has been added. * Major opcodes in the 32-bit base instruction format have been allocated for user-defined custom extensions. * A typographical error that suggested that stores source their data from rd has been corrected to refer to rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > Preface

chunks.text: New 128-bit variants of LOAD-FP and STORE-FP instructions are added, encoded with a new value for the funct3 width field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Q" Extension for Quad-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > Quad-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLQ and FSQ are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned and XLEN=128.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Q" Extension for Quad-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > Quad-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: A new supported format is added to the format field of most instructions, as shown in
chunks.section: Preamble > "Q" Extension for Quad-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > Quad-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Quad-Precision Computational Instructions

chunks.text: These instructions are defined analogously to the double-precision-to-integer and integer-to-double-precision conversion instructions. FCVT.W.Q or FCVT.L.Q converts a quad-precision floating-point number to a signed 32-bit or 64-bit integer, respectively. FCVT.Q.W or FCVT.Q.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, into a quad-precision floating-point number. FCVT.WU.Q, FCVT.LU.Q, FCVT.Q.WU, and FCVT.Q.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. FCVT.L[U].Q and FCVT.Q.L[U] are RV64-only instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Q" Extension for Quad-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > Quad-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Quad-Precision Convert and Move Instructions

chunks.text: FMV.X.Q and FMV.Q.X instructions are not provided in RV32 or RV64, so quad-precision bit patterns must be moved to the integer registers via memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Q" Extension for Quad-Precision Floating-Point, Version 2.2 > Quad-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Quad-Precision Convert and Move Instructions

chunks.text: The PAUSE instruction hints to a hart that it should temporarily reduce its rate of execution.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zihintpause" Extension for Pause Hint

chunks.text: Much of the debate surrounding this extension centered on whether a facility similar to x86's MONITOR/MWAIT should instead be provided.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zihintpause" Extension for Pause Hint

chunks.text: Two conditional-zero instructions are included: one that writes zero if the comparand is zero, and one that does so if the comparand is nonzero.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations

chunks.text: While compare-and-swap for XLEN wide data may be accomplished using LR/SC, the CAS atomic instructions scale better to highly parallel systems than LR/SC.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions

chunks.text: Many lock-free algorithms, such as a lock-free queue, require manipulation of pointer variables. A simple CAS operation may not be sufficient to guard against what is commonly referred to as the ABA problem in such algorithms that manipulate pointer variables.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions

chunks.text: Emulation of narrower AMOs through wider AMO* instructions on non-idempotent IO memory regions may result in unintended side effects.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zabha" Extension for Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operations, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When FP16 (officially called binary16) was first introduced by IEEE 754-2008, it was just an interchange format.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: However, they also found that intermediate values were much better when accumulated into a higher precision.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: There was the added benefit that smaller multiplication blocks could be created for the FP16's smaller number of significant bits.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: Also, more complicated dot product instructions started to show up including those that packed two FP16 numbers in a 32-bit register, multiplied these by another pair of FP16 numbers in another register, added these two products to an FP32 accumulate value in a 3rd register and returned an FP32 result.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: Experts working in machine learning at Google who continued to work with FP32 values noted that the least significant 16 bits of their mantissas were not always needed for good results, even in training.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: They proposed a truncated version of FP32, which was the 16 most significant bits of the FP32 encoding.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: Not only did they find that the number of significant bits in BF16 tended to be sufficient for their work (despite being fewer than in FP16), but it was very easy for them to reuse their existing data; FP32 numbers could be readily rounded to BF16 with a minimal amount of work.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: Furthermore, the even smaller number of the BF16 significant bits enabled even smaller multiplication blocks to be built.
chunks.section: Preamble > Historical Rationale for Extensions > "Zfbfmin" Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Operations

chunks.text: For this purpose, we define a combination of a base ISA (RV32I or RV64I) plus selected standard extensions (IMAFD, Zicsr, Zifencei) as a "general-purpose" ISA, and we use the abbreviation G for the IMAFDZicsrZifencei combination of instruction-set extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: Opcodes marked as reserved should be avoided for custom instruction-set extensions as they might be used by future standard extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: Major opcodes marked as custom-0 through custom-3 will be avoided by future standard extensions and are recommended for use by custom instruction-set extensions within the base 32-bit instruction format.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings
Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: lists the CSRs that have currently been allocated CSR addresses.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: The timers, counters, and floating-point CSRs are the only CSRs defined in this specification.
chunks.section: Preamble > RV32/64G Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: Under RVWMO, code running on a single hart appears to execute in order from the perspective of other memory instructions in the same hart, but memory instructions from another hart may observe the memory instructions from the first hart being executed in a different order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0

chunks.text: Therefore, multithreaded code may require explicit synchronization to guarantee ordering between memory instructions from different harts.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0

chunks.text: Each aligned memory instruction that accesses XLEN or fewer bits gives rise to exactly one memory operation, unless specified otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: A misaligned load or store instruction may be decomposed into a set of component memory operations of any granularity. A floating-point load or store of more than XLEN bits may also be decomposed into a set of component memory operations of any granularity.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: However, if misaligned atomics are supported via the misaligned atomicity granule PMA, then AMOs within an atomicity granule are not decomposed, nor are loads and stores defined in the base ISAs, nor are loads and stores of no more than XLEN bits defined in the F, D, and Q extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: An LR instruction and an SC instruction are said to be paired if the LR precedes the SC in program order and if there are no other LR or SC instructions in between; the corresponding memory operations are said to be paired as well (except in case of a failed SC, where no store operation is generated).
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: The complete list of conditions determining whether an SC must succeed, may succeed, or must fail is defined in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: Load and store operations may also carry one or more ordering annotations from the following set: "acquire-RCpc", "acquire-RCsc", "release-RCpc", and "release-RCsc".
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives

chunks.text: In the context of defining dependencies, a register refers either to an entire general-purpose register, some portion of a CSR, or an entire CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: The granularity at which dependencies are tracked through CSRs is specific to each CSR and is defined in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: In the opcode of i, rs1, rs2, or rs3 is set to r * i is a CSR instruction, and in the opcode of i, csr is set to r, unless i is CSRRW or CSRRWI and rd is set to `x0` * r is a CSR and an implicit source register for i, as defined in * r is a CSR that aliases with another source register for i
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: In the opcode of i, rd is set to r * i is a CSR instruction, and in the opcode of i, csr is set to r, unless i is CSRRS or CSRRC and rs1 is set to `x0` or i is CSRRSI or CSRRCI and uimm[4:0] is set to zero. * r is a CSR and an implicit destination register for i, as defined in * r is a CSR that aliases with another destination register for i
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: Instruction j has a syntactic dependency on instruction i via destination register s of i and source register r of j if either of the following hold:
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: b has a syntactic address dependency on a if r is an address source register for j and j has a syntactic dependency on i via source register r
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: b has a syntactic data dependency on a if b is a store operation, r is a data source register for j, and j has a syntactic dependency on i via source register r
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: b has a syntactic control dependency on a if there is an instruction m program-ordered between i and j such that m is a branch or indirect jump and m has a syntactic dependency on i.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Syntactic Dependencies

chunks.text: The subset of program order that must be respected by the global memory order is known as preserved program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Preserved Program Order

chunks.text: An execution of a RISC-V program obeys the RVWMO memory consistency model only if there exists a global memory order conforming to preserved program order and satisfying the load value axiom, the atomicity axiom, and the progress axiom.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Memory Model Axioms

chunks.text: If r and w are paired load and store operations generated by aligned LR and SC instructions in a hart h, s is a store to byte x, and r returns a value written by s, then s must precede w in the global memory order, and there can be no store from a hart other than h to byte x following s and preceding w in the global memory order.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Memory Model Axioms > Atomicity Axiom

chunks.text: No memory operation may be preceded in the global memory order by an infinite sequence of other memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Memory Model Primitives > Memory Model Axioms > Progress Axiom

chunks.text: This section provides a concrete listing of the source and destination registers for each instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Source and Destination Register Listings

chunks.text: These listings are used in the definition of syntactic dependencies in .
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Source and Destination Register Listings

chunks.text: The term "accumulating CSR" is used to describe a CSR that is both a source and a destination register, but which carries a dependency only from itself to itself.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Source and Destination Register Listings

chunks.text: Instructions carry a dependency from each source register in the "Source Registers" column to each destination register in the "Destination Registers" column, from each source register in the "Source Registers" column to each CSR in the "Accumulating CSRs" column, and from each CSR in the "Accumulating CSRs" column to itself, except where annotated otherwise.
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Source and Destination Register Listings

chunks.text: † The instruction does not carry a dependency from any source register to any destination register
chunks.section: Preamble > RVWMO Memory Consistency Model, Version 2.0 > Definition of the RVWMO Memory Model > Source and Destination Register Listings

chunks.text: ‡ The instruction carries dependencies from source register(s) to destination register(s) as specified

chunks.text: ---- Code using one width for predicate and different width for masked compute. int8t a[]; int32t b[], c[]; for (i=0; i<n; i++) { b[i] = (a[i] < 5) ? c[i] : 1; } Mixed-width code that keeps SEW/LMUL=8 loop: vsetvli a4, a0, e8, m1, ta, ma Byte vector for predicate calc vle8.v v1, (a1) Load a[i] add a1, a1, a4 Bump pointer. vmslt.vi v0, v1, 5 a[i] < 5?
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Example with mixed-width mask and compute.

chunks.text: vsetvli x0, a0, e32, m4, ta, mu Vector of 32-bit values. sub a0, a0, a4 Decrement count vmv.v.i v4, 1 Splat immediate to destination vle32.v v4, (a3), v0.t Load requested elements of C, others undisturbed sll t1, a4, 2 add a3, a3, t1 Bump pointer. vse32.v v4, (a2) Store b[i]. add a2, a2, t1 Bump pointer. bnez a0, loop Any more? ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Example with mixed-width mask and compute.

chunks.text: loop: vsetvli t0, a0, e8, m1, ta, ma Use 8b elements. vle8.v v0, (a1) Get x[i] sub a0, a0, t0 Decrement element count add a1, a1, t0 x[i] Bump pointer vmslt.vi v0, v0, 5 Set mask in v0 vsetvli x0, x0, e16, m2, ta, mu Use 16b elements. slli t0, t0, 1 Multiply by 2 bytes vle16.v v2, (a2), v0.t z[i] = a[i] case vmnot.m v0, v0 Invert v0 add a2, a2, t0 a[i] bump pointer vle16.v v2, (a3), v0.t z[i] = b[i] case add a3, a3, t0 b[i] bump pointer vse16.v v2, (a4) Store z add a4, a4, t0 z[i] bump pointer bnez a0, loop ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Conditional example

chunks.text: ---- v1 = v1 / v2 to almost 23 bits of precision.
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Division approximation example

chunks.text: ---- v1 = sqrt(v1) to more than 23 bits of precision.
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Square root approximation example

chunks.text: fmv.w.x ft0, x0 Mask off zero inputs vmfne.vf v0, v1, ft0 to avoid DZ exception vfrsqrt7.v v2, v1, v0.t Estimate r ~= 1/sqrt(v1) vmfne.vf v0, v2, ft0, v0.t Mask off +inf to avoid NV li t0, 0x3f800000 fli.s ft0, 0.5 vmv.v.x v5, t0 Splat 1.0 vfmul.vv v3, v1, v2, v0.t t = v1 r vfmul.vf v4, v2, ft0, v0.t 0.5 r vfmsub.vv v3, v2, v5, v0.t t r - 1 vfnmsac.vv v2, v3, v4, v0.t r - (0.5 r) (t r - 1) Better estimate of 1/sqrt(v1) vfmul.vv v1, v1, v2, v0.t t = v1 r vfmsub.vv v2, v1, v5, v0.t t r - 1 vfmul.vf v3, v1, ft0, v0.t 0.5 t vfnmsac.vv v1, v2, v3, v0.t t - (0.5 t) (t r - 1) ~ sqrt(v1) to about 23.3 bits ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Square root approximation example

chunks.text: The compiler realizes there are two datatypes within the loop: an 8-bit 'char' and a 64-bit 'long *'.
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Fractional LMUL example

chunks.text: Without fractional LMUL, the compiler would be forced to use LMUL=1 for the 8-bit computation and LMUL=8 for the 64-bit computation(s), to have equal number of elements on all computations within the same loop iteration.
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Fractional LMUL example

chunks.text: Given the large number of 64-bit variables and temporaries required in this loop, the compiler ends up generating a lot of spill code.
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Fractional LMUL example

chunks.text: ---- .LBB04: %vector.body =>This Inner Loop Header: Depth=1 add s9, a2, s6 vsetvli s1, zero, e8,m1,ta,mu vle8.v v25, (s9) add s1, a3, s6 vle8.v v26, (s1) vadd.vv v25, v26, v25 add s1, a1, s6 vse8.v v25, (s1) add s9, a5, s10 vsetvli s1, zero, e64,m8,ta,mu vle64.v v8, (s9) add s1, a6, s10 vle64.v v16, (s1) add s1, a7, s10 vle64.v v24, (s1) add s1, s3, s10 vle64.v v0, (s1) sd a0, -112(s0) ld a0, -128(s0) vs8r.v v0, (a0) Spill LMUL=8 add s9, t6, s10 add s11, t5, s10 add ra, t2, s10 add s1, t3, s10 vle64.v v0, (s9) ld s9, -136(s0) vs8r.v v0, (s9) Spill LMUL=8 vle64.v v0, (s11) ld s9, -144(s0) vs8r.v v0, (s9) Spill LMUL=8 vle64.v v0, (ra) ld s9, -160(s0) vs8r.v v0, (s9) Spill LMUL=8 vle64.v v0, (s1) ld s1, -152(s0) vs8r.v v0, (s1) Spill LMUL=8 vadd.vv v16, v16, v8 ld s1, -128(s0) vl8r.v v8, (s1) Reload LMUL=8 vadd.vv v8, v8, v24 ld s1, -136(s0) vl8r.v v24, (s1) Reload LMUL=8 ld s1, -144(s0) vl8r.v v0, (s1) Reload LMUL=8 vadd.vv v24, v0, v24 ld s1, -128(s0) vs8r.v v24, (s1) Spill LMUL=8 ld s1, -152(s0) vl8r.v v0, (s1) Reload LMUL=8 ld s1, -160(s0) vl8r.v v24, (s1) Reload LMUL=8 vadd.vv v0, v0, v24 add s1, a4, s10 vse64.v v16, (s1) add s1, s2, s10 vse64.v v8, (s1) vadd.vv v8, v8, v16 add s1, t4, s10 ld s9, -128(s0) vl8r.v v16, (s9) Reload LMUL=8 vse64.v v16, (s1) add s9, t0, s10 vadd.vv v8, v8, v16 vle64.v v16, (s9) add s1, t1, s10 vse64.v v0, (s1) vadd.vv v8, v8, v0 vsll.vi v16, v16, 1 vadd.vv v8, v8, v16 vse64.v v8, (s9) add s6, s6, s7 add s10, s10, s8 bne s6, s4, .LBB04 ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Fractional LMUL example

chunks.text: ---- .LBB04: %vector.body =>This Inner Loop Header: Depth=1 add s9, a2, s6 vsetvli s1, zero, e8,mf2,ta,mu // LMUL=1/2 ! vle8.v v25, (s9) add s1, a3, s6 vle8.v v26, (s1) vadd.vv v25, v26, v25 add s1, a1, s6 vse8.v v25, (s1) add s9, a5, s10 vsetvli s1, zero, e64,m4,ta,mu // LMUL=4 vle64.v v28, (s9) add s1, a6, s10 vle64.v v8, (s1) vadd.vv v28, v8, v28 add s1, a7, s10 vle64.v v8, (s1) add s1, s3, s10 vle64.v v12, (s1) add s1, t6, s10 vle64.v v16, (s1) add s1, t5, s10 vle64.v v20, (s1) add s1, a4, s10 vse64.v v28, (s1) vadd.vv v8, v12, v8 vadd.vv v12, v20, v16 add s1, t2, s10 vle64.v v16, (s1) add s1, t3, s10 vle64.v v20, (s1) add s1, s2, s10 vse64.v v8, (s1) add s9, t4, s10 vadd.vv v16, v20, v16 add s11, t0, s10 vle64.v v20, (s11) vse64.v v12, (s9) add s1, t1, s10 vse64.v v16, (s1) vsll.vi v20, v20, 1 vadd.vv v28, v8, v28 vadd.vv v28, v28, v12 vadd.vv v28, v28, v16 vadd.vv v28, v28, v20 vse64.v v28, (s11) add s6, s6, s7 add s10, s10, s8 bne s6, s4, .LBB04 ----
chunks.section: Preamble > Vector Assembly Code Examples > Vector-vector add example > Fractional LMUL example

chunks.text: No ordering constraint is implied to accesses to the other domain, and a FENCE instruction should be used to order across both domains.
chunks.section: Preamble > Atomic Instructions > Specifying Ordering of Atomic Instructions

chunks.text: If both bits are clear, no additional ordering constraints are imposed on the atomic memory operation.
chunks.section: Preamble > Atomic Instructions > Specifying Ordering of Atomic Instructions

chunks.text: If both the aq and rl bits are set, the atomic memory operation is sequentially consistent and cannot be observed to happen before any earlier memory operations or after any later memory operations in the same RISC-V hart and to the same address domain.
chunks.section: Preamble > Atomic Instructions > Specifying Ordering of Atomic Instructions

chunks.text: The atomic memory operation (AMO) instructions perform read-modify-write operations for multiprocessor synchronization and are encoded with an R-type instruction format.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: For RV64, 32-bit AMOs always sign-extend the value placed in rd, and ignore the upper 32 bits of the original value of rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: If the address is not naturally aligned, an address-misaligned exception or an access-fault exception will be generated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: The access-fault exception can be generated for a memory access that would otherwise be able to complete except for the misalignment, if the misaligned access should not be emulated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: If present, the misaligned atomicity granule PMA specifies the size of a misaligned atomicity granule, a power-of-two number of bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: The operations supported are swap, integer add, bitwise AND, bitwise OR, bitwise XOR, and signed and unsigned integer maximum and minimum.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: If the aq bit is set, then no later memory operations in this RISC-V hart can be observed to take place before the AMO.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: Conversely, if the rl bit is set, then other RISC-V harts will not observe the AMO before memory accesses preceding the AMO in this RISC-V hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zaamo" Extension for Atomic Memory Operations

chunks.text: Emulation of narrower AMOs through wider AMO* instructions on non-idempotent IO memory regions may result in unintended side effects.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zabha" Extension for Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operations, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If Zacas extension is also implemented, Zabha further provides the `AMOCAS.[B|H]` instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zabha" Extension for Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operations, Version 1.0 > Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operation Instructions

chunks.text: Similar to the AMOs specified in the A extension, the Zabha extension mandates that the address contained in the `rs1` register must be naturally aligned to the size of the operand.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zabha" Extension for Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operations, Version 1.0 > Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operation Instructions

chunks.text: The same exception options as specified in the A extension are applicable in cases where the address is not naturally aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zabha" Extension for Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operations, Version 1.0 > Byte and Halfword Atomic Memory Operation Instructions

chunks.text: This extension defines CAS instructions to operate on 32-bit, 64-bit, and 128-bit (RV64 only) data values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0

chunks.text: For RV32, `AMOCAS.W` atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address in `rs1`, compares the loaded value to the 32-bit value held in `rd`, and if the comparison is bitwise equal, then stores the 32-bit value held in `rs2` to the original address in `rs1`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: [listing] ---- temp = mem[X(rs1)] if ( temp X(rd) ) mem[X(rs1)] = X(rs2) X(rd) = temp ----
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: `AMOCAS.D` is similar to `AMOCAS.W` but operates on 64-bit data values.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: When the first register of a source register pair is `x0`, then both halves of the pair read as zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: When the first register of a destination register pair is `x0`, then the entire register result is discarded and neither destination register is written.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: [listing] temp0 = mem[X(rs1)+0] temp1 = mem[X(rs1)+4] comp0 = (rd x0) ? 0 : X(rd) comp1 = (rd x0) ? 0 : X(rd+1) swap0 = (rs2 x0) ? 0 : X(rs2) swap1 = (rs2 x0) ? 0 : X(rs2+1) if ( temp0 comp0 ) && ( temp1 comp1 ) mem[X(rs1)+0] = swap0 mem[X(rs1)+4] = swap1 endif if ( rd != x0 ) X(rd) = temp0 X(rd+1) = temp1 endif
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64, `AMOCAS.W` atomically loads a 32-bit data value from address in `rs1`, compares the loaded value to the lower 32 bits of the value held in `rd`, and if the comparison is bitwise equal, then stores the lower 32 bits of the value held in `rs2` to the original address in `rs1`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: The 32-bit value loaded from memory is sign-extended and is placed into register `rd`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: [listing] temp[31:0] = mem[X(rs1)] if ( temp[31:0] X(rd)[31:0] ) mem[X(rs1)] = X(rs2)[31:0] X(rd) = SignExtend(temp[31:0])
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64, `AMOCAS.D` atomically loads 64-bits of a data value from address in `rs1`, compares the loaded value to a 64-bit value held in `rd`, and if the comparison is bitwise equal, then stores the 64-bit value held in `rs2` to the original address in `rs1`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: The operation performed by `AMOCAS.D` for RV64 is as follows: [listing] temp = mem[X(rs1)] if ( temp X(rd) ) mem[X(rs1)] = X(rs2) X(rd) = temp
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: When the first register of a source register pair is `x0`, then both halves of the pair read as zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: When the first register of a destination register pair is `x0`, then the entire register result is discarded and neither destination register is written.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: [listing] temp0 = mem[X(rs1)+0] temp1 = mem[X(rs1)+8] comp0 = (rd x0) ? 0 : X(rd) comp1 = (rd x0) ? 0 : X(rd+1) swap0 = (rs2 x0) ? 0 : X(rs2) swap1 = (rs2 x0) ? 0 : X(rs2+1) if ( temp0 comp0 ) && ( temp1 comp1 ) mem[X(rs1)+0] = swap0 mem[X(rs1)+8] = swap1 endif if ( rd != x0 ) X(rd) = temp0 X(rd+1) = temp1 endif
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: And the same exception options apply if the address is not naturally aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: The memory operation performed by an `AMOCAS.W/D/Q`, when successful, has acquire semantics if `aq` bit is 1 and has release semantics if `rl` bit is 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: The memory operation performed by an `AMOCAS.W/D/Q`, when not successful, has acquire semantics if `aq` bit is 1 but does not have release semantics, regardless of `rl`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: A FENCE instruction may be used to order the memory read access and, if produced, the memory write access by an `AMOCAS.W/D/Q` instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zacas" Extension for Atomic Compare-and-Swap (CAS) Instructions, Version 1.0.0 > Word/Doubleword/Quadword CAS (AMOCAS.W/D/Q) Instructions

chunks.text: The Zalasr instructions always sign-extend the value placed in rd and ignore the upper bits of the value of rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions

chunks.text: If the address is not naturally aligned, an address-misaligned exception or an access-fault exception will be generated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions

chunks.text: The access-fault exception can be generated for a memory access that would otherwise be able to complete except for the misalignment, if the misaligned access should not be emulated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions

chunks.text: This load must have the ordering annotation aq and may have ordering annotation rl encoded in the instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions > Load Acquire

chunks.text: The instruction always has an "acquire-RCsc" annotation, and if the bit rl is set the instruction has a "release-RCsc" annotation. + The versions without the aq bit set are RESERVED. LD.{AQ, AQRL} is RV64-only.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions > Load Acquire

chunks.text: This store must have ordering annotation rl and may have ordering annotation aq encoded in the instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions > Store Release

chunks.text: The instruction always has an "release-RCsc" annotation, and if the bit aq is set the instruction has a "acquire-RCsc" annotation. + The versions without the rl bit set are RESERVED. SD.{RL, AQRL} is RV64-only.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalasr" Atomic Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Acquire and Store-Release Instructions > Store Release

chunks.text: Complex atomic memory operations on a single memory word or doubleword are performed with the load-reserved (LR) and store-conditional (SC) instructions. LR.W loads a word from the address in rs1, places the sign-extended value in rd, and registers a reservation set—a set of bytes that subsumes the bytes in the addressed word. SC.W conditionally writes a word in rs2 to the address in rs1: the SC.W succeeds only if the reservation is still valid and the reservation set contains the bytes being written.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If the SC.W succeeds, the instruction writes the word in rs2 to memory, and it writes zero to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If the SC.W fails, the instruction does not write to memory, and it writes a nonzero value to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: No SC.W instruction shall retire unless it passes memory permission checks, but it is UNSPECIFIED whether any side effects of implicit address translation and protection memory accesses (such as setting a page-table entry D bit) occur on a failed SC.W.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: For the purposes of memory protection, a failed SC.W may be treated like a store.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: Portable software should only assume the failure code will be non-zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If the address is not naturally aligned, an address-misaligned exception or an access-fault exception will be generated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The access-fault exception can be generated for a memory access that would otherwise be able to complete except for the misalignment, if the misaligned access should not be emulated.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: An SC may succeed only if no store from another hart to the reservation set can be observed to have occurred between the LR and the SC, and if there is no other SC between the LR and itself in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: An SC may succeed only if no write from a device other than a hart to the bytes accessed by the LR instruction can be observed to have occurred between the LR and SC.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The SC must fail if the address is not within the reservation set of the most recent LR in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The SC must fail if a store to the reservation set from another hart can be observed to occur between the LR and SC.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The SC must fail if a write from some other device to the bytes accessed by the LR can be observed to occur between the LR and SC. (
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If such a device writes the reservation set but does not write the bytes accessed by the LR, the SC may or may not fail.) An SC must fail if there is another SC (to any address) between the LR and the SC in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: Software should not set the rl bit on an LR instruction unless the aq bit is also set, nor should software set the aq bit on an SC instruction unless the rl bit is also set. LR.rl and SC.aq instructions are not guaranteed to provide any stronger ordering than those with both bits clear, but may result in lower performance.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The loop comprises only an LR/SC sequence and code to retry the sequence in the case of failure, and must comprise at most 16 instructions placed sequentially in memory. * An LR/SC sequence begins with an LR instruction and ends with an SC instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions > Eventual Success of Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: Compressed forms of the aforementioned ''I'' instructions in the C (hence Zca) and Zcb extensions are also permitted. * The code to retry a failing LR/SC sequence can contain backwards jumps and/or branches to repeat the LR/SC sequence, but otherwise has the same constraint as the code between the LR and SC. * The LR and SC addresses must lie within a memory region with the LR/SC eventuality property.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions > Eventual Success of Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: The execution environment is responsible for communicating which regions have this property. * The SC must be to the same effective address and of the same data size as the latest LR executed by the same hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions > Eventual Success of Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If a hart H enters a constrained LR/SC loop, the execution environment must guarantee that one of the following events eventually occurs:
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions > Eventual Success of Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: H or some other hart executes a successful SC to the reservation set of the LR instruction in H's constrained LR/SC loops. * Some other hart executes an unconditional store or AMO instruction to the reservation set of the LR instruction in H's constrained LR/SC loop, or some other device in the system writes to that reservation set. * H executes a branch or jump that exits the constrained LR/SC loop. * H traps.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zalrsc" Extension for Load-Reserved/Store-Conditional Instructions > Eventual Success of Store-Conditional Instructions

chunks.text: If the Zama16b extension is implemented, then the in main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs is 16 bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > Zama16b Extension for 16-byte Misaligned Atomicity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Misaligned loads, stores, and AMOs to main memory regions that do not cross a naturally aligned 16-byte boundary are atomic.
chunks.section: Preamble > Zama16b Extension for 16-byte Misaligned Atomicity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Za128rs extension requires that the reservation sets used by the instructions in the Zalrsc extension be contiguous, naturally aligned, and at most 128 bytes in size.
chunks.section: Preamble > Za128rs Extension for Reservation-Set Size, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Za64rs extension requires that the reservation sets used by the instructions in the Zalrsc extension be contiguous, naturally aligned, and at most 64 bytes in size.
chunks.section: Preamble > Za128rs Extension for Reservation-Set Size, Version 1.0 > Za64rs Extension for Reservation-Set Size, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The producer may be code executing on a RISC-V hart, an accelerator device, an external I/O agent.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01

chunks.text: Then a subsequent `WRS.NTO` instruction would cause the hart to temporarily stall execution in a low-power state until a store occurs to the reservation set or an interrupt is observed.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01

chunks.text: Sometimes the program waiting on a memory update may also need to carry out a task at a future time or otherwise place an upper bound on the wait.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01

chunks.text: To support such use cases a second instruction `WRS.STO` (WRS-with-short-timeout) is provided that works like `WRS.NTO` but bounds the stall duration to an implementation-define short timeout such that the stall is terminated on the timeout if no other conditions have occurred to terminate the stall.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01

chunks.text: The program using this instruction may then determine if its deadline has been reached.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01

chunks.text: The `WRS.NTO` and `WRS.STO` instructions cause the hart to temporarily stall execution in a low-power state as long as the reservation set is valid and no pending interrupts, even if disabled, are observed.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: Hart execution may be stalled while the following conditions are all satisfied: [loweralpha] .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: If `WRS.STO`, a "short" duration since start of stall has not elapsed .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: No pending interrupt is observed (see the rules below)
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: `WRS.NTO` and `WRS.STO` instructions follow the rules of the `WFI` instruction for resuming execution on a pending interrupt.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: When the `TW` (Timeout Wait) bit in `mstatus` is set and `WRS.NTO` is executed in any privilege mode other than M mode, and it does not complete within an implementation-specific bounded time limit, the `WRS.NTO` instruction will cause an illegal-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: When executing in VS or VU mode, if the `VTW` bit is set in `hstatus`, the `TW` bit in `mstatus` is clear, and the `WRS.NTO` does not complete within an implementation-specific bounded time limit, the `WRS.NTO` instruction will cause a virtual-instruction exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zawrs" Extension for Wait-on-Reservation-Set instructions, Version 1.01 > Wait-on-Reservation-Set Instructions

chunks.text: This chapter describes the RISC-V compressed instruction-set extensions, which reduce static and dynamic code size by adding short 16-bit instruction encodings for common operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > Compressed Instructions

chunks.text: The extlink:c[] extension combines ext:zca[], ext:zcf[] if XLEN=32 and the F extension is present, and ext:zcd[] if the D extension is present.
chunks.section: Preamble > Compressed Instructions

chunks.text: The compressed extensions use a simple compression scheme that offers shorter 16-bit versions of common 32-bit RISC-V instructions when:
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension

chunks.text: With the addition of the ext:zca[] extension, no instructions can raise instruction-address-misaligned exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension

chunks.text: If the C extension is implemented, the appropriate compressed floating-point load and store instructions must be provided whenever the relevant standard floating-point extension (F and/or D) is also implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension

chunks.text: ext:zca[] was designed under the constraint that each ext:zca[] instruction expands into a single 32-bit instruction in the base ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension

chunks.text: When the full 5-bit destination register specifier is present, it is in the same place as in the 32-bit RISC-V encoding.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats

chunks.text: Where immediates are sign-extended, the sign extension is always from bit 12.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats

chunks.text: Immediate fields have been scrambled, as in the base specification, to reduce the number of immediate multiplexers required.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats

chunks.text: For many ext:zca[] instructions, zero-valued immediates are disallowed and `x0` is not a valid 5-bit register specifier.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats

chunks.text: To increase the reach of 16-bit instructions, data-transfer instructions use zero-extended immediates that are scaled by the size of the data in bytes: ×4 for words, ×8 for doublewords, and ×16 for quadwords.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: C.LWSP loads a 32-bit value from memory into register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.LDSP is an XLEN=64-only instruction that loads a 64-bit value from memory into register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes its effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.SWSP stores a 32-bit value in register rs2 to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.SDSP is an XLEN=64-only instruction that stores a 64-bit value in register rs2 to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: These instructions use the CL format.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.LW loads a 32-bit value from memory into register `rd′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.LD is an XLEN=64-only instruction that loads a 64-bit value from memory into register `rd′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: These instructions use the CS format.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.SW stores a 32-bit value in register `rs2′` to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.SD is an XLEN=64-only instruction that stores a 64-bit value in register `rs2′` to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Load and Store Instructions > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: As with base RVI instructions, the offsets of all ext:zca[] control transfer instructions are in multiples of 2 bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Control Transfer Instructions

chunks.text: The offset is sign-extended and added to the `pc` to form the branch target address.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Control Transfer Instructions

chunks.text: It can therefore target a {pm}256 B range. C.BEQZ takes the branch if the value in register rs1′ is zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Control Transfer Instructions

chunks.text: C.BNEZ is defined analogously, but it takes the branch if rs1′ contains a nonzero value.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Control Transfer Instructions

chunks.text: C.LI loads the sign-extended 6-bit immediate, imm, into register rd. C.LI expands into `addi rd, x0, imm`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Constant-Generation Instructions

chunks.text: C.LUI loads the non-zero 6-bit immediate field into bits 17–12 of the destination register, clears the bottom 12 bits, and sign-extends bit 17 into all higher bits of the destination. C.LUI expands into `lui rd, imm`. C.LUI is valid only when rd!=`x2`, and when the immediate is not equal to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Constant-Generation Instructions

chunks.text: These integer register-immediate operations are encoded in the CI format and perform operations on an integer register and a 6-bit immediate.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.ADDI adds the non-zero sign-extended 6-bit immediate to the value in register rd then writes the result to rd. C.ADDI expands into `addi rd, rd, imm`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The code points with rd!=0 and imm=0 are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The code points with rd=`x0` encode the C.NOP instruction, of which the code points with imm!=0 are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.ADDIW is an XLEN=64-only instruction that performs the same computation but produces a 32-bit result, then sign-extends result to 64 bits. C.ADDIW expands into `addiw rd, rd, imm`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The immediate can be zero for C.ADDIW, where this corresponds to `sext.w rd`. C.ADDIW is valid only when rd!=`x0`; the code points with rd=`x0` are reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.ADDI16SP (add immediate to stack pointer) shares the opcode with C.LUI, but has a destination field of `x2`. C.ADDI16SP adds the non-zero sign-extended 6-bit immediate to the value in the stack pointer (`sp=x2`), where the immediate is scaled to represent multiples of 16 in the range [-512, 496]. C.ADDI16SP is used to adjust the stack pointer in procedure prologues and epilogues.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: It expands into `addi x2, x2, nzimm[9:4]`. C.ADDI16SP is valid only when nzimm!=0; the code point with nzimm=0 is reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.SLLI is a CI-format instruction that performs a logical left shift of the value in register rd then writes the result to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The shift amount is encoded in the shamt field. C.SLLI expands into `slli rd, rd, shamt[5:0]`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The C.SLLI code points with shamt=0 or with rd=`x0` are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: For XLEN=32, shamt[5] must be zero; the code points with shamt[5]=1 are designated for custom extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.SRLI is a CB-format instruction that performs a logical right shift of the value in register rd′ then writes the result to rd′.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The shift amount is encoded in the shamt field. C.SRLI expands into `srli rd′, rd′, shamt`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: The C.SRLI code points with shamt=0 are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: For XLEN=32, shamt[5] must be zero; the code points with shamt[5]=1 are designated for custom extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Immediate Operations

chunks.text: C.MV copies the value in register rs2 into register rd. C.MV expands into `add rd, x0, rs2`. C.MV is valid only when rs2!=`x0`; the code points with rs2=`x0` correspond to the C.JR instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: The code points with rs2!=`x0` and rd=`x0` are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: C.ADD adds the values in registers rd and rs2 and writes the result to register rd. C.ADD expands into `add rd, rd, rs2`. C.ADD is only valid when rs2!=`x0`; the code points with rs2=`x0` correspond to the C.JALR and C.EBREAK instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: The code points with rs2!=`x0` and rd=`x0` are HINTs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: These instructions use the CA format.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.AND` computes the bitwise `AND` of the values in registers rd′ and rs2′, then writes the result to register rd′. `C.AND` expands into `and rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.OR` computes the bitwise `OR` of the values in registers rd′ and rs2′, then writes the result to register rd′. `C.OR` expands into `or rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.XOR` computes the bitwise `XOR` of the values in registers rd′ and rs2′, then writes the result to register rd′. `C.XOR` expands into `xor rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.SUB` subtracts the value in register rs2′ from the value in register rd′, then writes the result to register rd′. `C.SUB` expands into `sub rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.ADDW` is an XLEN=64-only instruction that adds the values in registers rd′ and rs2′, then sign-extends the lower 32 bits of the sum before writing the result to register rd′. `C.ADDW` expands into `addw rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: `C.SUBW` is an XLEN=64-only instruction that subtracts the value in register rs2′ from the value in register rd′, then sign-extends the lower 32 bits of the difference before writing the result to register rd′. `C.SUBW` expands into `subw rd′, rd′, rs2′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Integer Register-Register Operations

chunks.text: A 16-bit instruction with all bits zero is permanently reserved as an illegal instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > Integer Computational Instructions > Defined Illegal Instruction

chunks.text: The last quadrant, which has the two least-significant bits set, corresponds to instructions wider than 16 bits, including those in the base ISAs.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > ext:zca[] Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: Several instructions are only valid for certain operands; when invalid, they are marked either RES to indicate that the opcode is reserved for future standard extensions;
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zca[] Integer Compressed Instructions Extension > Compressed Instruction Formats > ext:zca[] Instruction Set Listings

chunks.text: The ext:zcb[] extension adds several compressed instructions which, like those in the extlink:zca[] extension, expand into a single 32-bit instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions

chunks.text: The resulting byte is zero extended to XLEN bits and is written to rd'.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu

chunks.text: The resulting halfword is zero extended to XLEN bits and is written to rd'.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.lhu

chunks.text: The resulting halfword is sign extended to XLEN bits and is written to rd'.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.lh

chunks.text: This instruction stores the least significant byte of rs2' to the memory address formed by adding rs1' to the zero extended immediate uimm.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.sb

chunks.text: This instruction stores the least significant halfword of rs2' to the memory address formed by adding rs1' to the zero extended immediate uimm.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.sh

chunks.text: It zero-extends the least-significant byte of the operand to XLEN bits by inserting zeros into all of the bits more significant than 7.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.zext.b

chunks.text: Zbb is also required. // //32-bit equivalent: // // from Zbb
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.sext.b

chunks.text: It zero-extends the least-significant halfword of the operand to XLEN bits by inserting zeros into all of the bits more significant than 15.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.zext.h

chunks.text: Zbb is also required. // //32-bit equivalent: // // from Zbb
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.zext.h

chunks.text: Zbb is also required. // //32-bit equivalent: // // from Zbb
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.sext.h

chunks.text: It zero-extends the least-significant word of the operand to XLEN bits by inserting zeros into all of the bits more significant than 31.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.zext.w

chunks.text: M or Zmmul must be configured. // //32-bit equivalent: // //
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcb[] Extension for Additional Compressed Instructions > c.lbu > c.mul

chunks.text: It computes its effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: These instructions encode their data source or destination as described in .

chunks.text: C.FLD is an RV32DC/RV64DC-only instruction that loads a double-precision floating-point value from memory into floating-point register `rd′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: C.FSD is an RV32DC/RV64DC-only instruction that stores a double-precision floating-point value in floating-point register `rs2′` to memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 8, to the base address in register `rs1′`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcd[] Double-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: If XLEN=32 and the F extension is present, the ext:zce[] extension additionally depends upon the extlink:zcf[] extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zce[] Extension for Enhanced Instruction Compression

chunks.text: It computes its effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the stack pointer, `x2`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcf[] Single-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: It computes an effective address by adding the zero-extended offset, scaled by 4, to the stack pointer, `x2`.

chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcf[] Single-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcf[] Single-Precision Floating-Point Compressed Instructions Extension > Stack-Pointer-Based Loads and Stores > Register-Based Loads and Stores

chunks.text: For the purposes of RVWMO and exception handling, C.LD[SP] and C.SD[SP] instructions are considered to be misaligned loads and stores, with one additional constraint: a C.LD[SP] or C.SD[SP] instruction whose effective address is a multiple of 4 gives rise to two 4-byte memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zclsd[] Extension for Compressed Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of `x0` as operand > Exception Handling
