chunks.text: This section defines the ext:zcmop[] extension, which defines eight 16-bit MOP instructions named C.MOP.n, where n is an odd integer between 1 and 15, inclusive. C.MOP.n is encoded in the reserved encoding space corresponding to C.LUI xn, 0, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmop[] Compressed May-Be-Operations Extension

chunks.text: As well as reducing the code-size PUSH and POPRET eliminate the branches from calling the millicode save/restore routines and so may also perform better.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Example usage

chunks.text: Additionally the spimm field in the encoding allows the stack pointer to be adjusted in additional increments of 16-bytes.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Example usage > Stack pointer adjustment handling

chunks.text: There is no support for the \{ra, s0-s10} register list without also adding s11.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Example usage > Register list handling

chunks.text: Therefore the \{ra, s0-s11} register list must be used in this case.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Example usage > Register list handling

chunks.text: Correct execution requires that sp refers to idempotent memory (also see ), because the core must be able to handle traps detected during the sequence.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > PUSH/POP Fault handling

chunks.text: The entire PUSH/POP sequence is re-executed after returning from the trap handler, and multiple traps are possible during the sequence.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > PUSH/POP Fault handling

chunks.text: If a trap occurs during the sequence then xEPC is updated with the PC of the instruction, xTVAL (if not read-only-zero) updated with the bad address if it was an access fault and xCAUSE updated with the type of trap.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > PUSH/POP Fault handling

chunks.text: A sequence of stores writing the bytes required by the pseudocode ** The bytes may be written in any order. ** The bytes may be grouped into larger accesses. ** Any of the bytes may be written multiple times. * A stack pointer adjustment
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the PUSH sequence

chunks.text: The stack pointer adjustment must only be committed only when it is certain that the entire PUSH instruction will commit.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the PUSH sequence

chunks.text: Stores may also return imprecise faults from the bus.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the PUSH sequence

chunks.text: [source,asm] ---- any bytes from sp-1 to sp-28 may be written multiple times before the instruction completes therefore these updates may be visible in the interrupt/exception handler below the stack pointer sw s5, -4(sp) sw s4, -8(sp) sw s3,-12(sp) sw s2,-16(sp) sw s1,-20(sp) sw s0,-24(sp) sw ra,-28(sp)
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the PUSH sequence

chunks.text: this must only execute once, and will only execute after all stores completed without any precise faults, therefore this update is only visible in the interrupt/exception handler if cm.push has completed addi sp, sp, -64 ----
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the PUSH sequence

chunks.text: A sequence of loads reading the bytes required by the pseudocode. ** The bytes may be loaded in any order. ** The bytes may be grouped into larger accesses. ** Any of the bytes may be loaded multiple times. * A stack pointer adjustment * An optional `li a0, 0` * An optional `ret`
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: If a trap occurs during the sequence, then any loads which were executed before the trap may update architectural state.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: The loads will be re-executed once the trap handler completes, so the values will be overwritten.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: Therefore it is permitted for an implementation to update some of the destination registers before taking a fault.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: The optional `li a0, 0`, stack pointer adjustment and optional `ret` must only be committed only when it is certain that the entire POP/POPRET instruction will commit.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: For POPRET once the stack pointer adjustment has been committed the `ret` must execute.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: [source,asm] ---- any or all of these load instructions may execute multiple times therefore these updates may be visible in the interrupt/exception handler lw s3, 28(sp) lw s2, 24(sp) lw s1, 20(sp) lw s0, 16(sp) lw ra, 12(sp)
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: these must only execute once, will only execute after all loads complete successfully all instructions must execute atomically therefore these updates are not visible in the interrupt/exception handler li a0, 0 addi sp, sp, 32 ret ----
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Software view of execution > Software view of the POP/POPRET sequence

chunks.text: An implementation may have a requirement to issue a PUSH/POP instruction to non-idempotent memory.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Non-idempotent memory handling

chunks.text: If the core implementation does not support PUSH/POP to non-idempotent memories, the core may use an idempotency PMA to detect it and take a load (POP/POPRET) or store (PUSH) access-fault exception in order to avoid unpredictable results.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Non-idempotent memory handling

chunks.text: Software should only use these instructions on non-idempotent memory regions when software can tolerate the required memory accesses being issued repeatedly in the case that they cause exceptions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > Non-idempotent memory handling

chunks.text: switch (rlist){ case 4: \{reglist="ra"; xreglist="x1";} case 5: \{reglist="ra, s0"; xreglist="x1, x8";} case 6: \{reglist="ra, s0-s1"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9";} default: reserved(); } stackadj = stackadjbase + spimm * 16; ----
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: [source,sail] ---- RV32I, RV64: switch (rlist){ case 4: \{reglist="ra"; xreglist="x1";} case 5: \{reglist="ra, s0"; xreglist="x1, x8";} case 6: \{reglist="ra, s0-s1"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9";} case 7: \{reglist="ra, s0-s2"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18";} case 8: \{reglist="ra, s0-s3"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x19";} case 9: \{reglist="ra, s0-s4"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x20";} case 10: \{reglist="ra, s0-s5"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x21";} case 11: \{reglist="ra, s0-s6"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x22";} case 12: \{reglist="ra, s0-s7"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x23";} case 13: \{reglist="ra, s0-s8"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x24";} case 14: \{reglist="ra, s0-s9"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x25";} //note - to include s10, s11 must also be included case 15: \{reglist="ra, s0-s11"; xreglist="x1, x8-x9, x18-x27";} default: reserved(); } stackadj = stackadjbase + spimm * 16; ----
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: This instruction pushes (stores) the registers in reglist to the memory below the stack pointer, and then creates the stack frame by decrementing the stack pointer by stackadj, including any additional stack space requested by the value of spimm.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: stackadjbase is the minimum number of bytes, in multiples of 16-byte address increments, required to cover the registers in the list.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: spimm is the number of additional 16-byte address increments allocated for the stack frame.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: The first section of pseudocode may be executed multiple times before the instruction successfully completes.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.push

chunks.text: addr=sp-bytes; for(i in 27,26,25,24,23,22,21,20,19,18,9,8,1) { //if register i is in xreglist if (xreglist[i]) { switch(bytes) { 4: asm("sw x[i], 0(addr)"); 8: asm("sd x[i], 0(addr)"); } addr-=bytes; } } ----

chunks.text: The SAIL hasn't been written yet. if (RV32E && (r1sc>1 || r2sc>1)) { reserved(); } xreg1 = {r1sc[2:1]>0,r1sc[2:1]0,r1sc[2:0]}; xreg2 = {r2sc[2:1]>0,r2sc[2:1]0,r2sc[2:0]}; X[10] = X[xreg1]; X[11] = X[xreg2]; ----
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmp[] Extension for Compressed Prologues and Epilogues > PUSH/POP functional overview > cm.mva01s

chunks.text: The ext:zcmt[] extension adds table-jump instructions, which improve code density when procedures have many call sites.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps

chunks.text: It also adds the csr:jvt[] CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps

chunks.text: The csr:jvt[] CSR requires a state enable if Smstateen is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps

chunks.text: The ext:zcmt[] extension depends on the extlink:zca[] and extlink:zicsr[] extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps

chunks.text: The table must be a minimum of 64-byte aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview

chunks.text: If a return address stack is implemented, then as cm.jalt is equivalent to jal ra, it pushes to the stack.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview

chunks.text: If the same function is called with and without linking then it must have two entries in the table.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt

chunks.text: Multiple contexts may have different jump vector tables. JVT may be switched between them without an instruction barrier if the tables have not been updated in memory since the last fence.i.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > Table Jump Fault handling

chunks.text: If an exception occurs on either instruction fetch, xEPC is set to the PC of the table jump instruction, xCAUSE is set as expected for the type of fault and xTVAL (if not set to zero) contains the fetch address which caused the fault.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > Table Jump Fault handling

chunks.text: <<< [csrs-jvt,reftext="jvt CSR, table jump base vector and control register"]
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > Table Jump Fault handling

chunks.text: Table jump base vector and control register
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: The jvt register is an XLEN-bit *WARL* read/write register that holds the jump table configuration, consisting of the jump table base address (BASE) and the jump table mode (MODE).
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: If ext:zcmt[] is implemented then jvt must also be implemented, but can contain a read-only value.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: If jvt is writable, the set of values the register may hold can vary by implementation.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: The value in the BASE field must always be aligned on a 64-byte boundary.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: When computing jump-table accesses, the lower six bits of base are filled with zeroes to obtain an XLEN-bit jump-table base address jvt.base that is always aligned on a 64-byte boundary.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: jvt.base is a virtual address, whenever virtual memory is enabled.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: The memory pointed to by jvt.base is treated as instruction memory for the purpose of executing table jump instructions, implying execute access permission.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: [JVT-config-table] .jvt.mode definition [width="60%",options=header] | | jvt.mode | Comment | 000000 | Jump table mode | others | *reserved for future standard use* |
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: jvt.mode is a *WARL* field, so can only be programmed to modes which are implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: Therefore the discovery mechanism is to attempt to program different modes and read back the values to see which are available.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: Jump table mode must be implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: jvt CSR adds architectural state to the system software context (such as an OS process), therefore must be saved/restored on context switches. <<< [insns-cmjt,reftext="Jump via table"]
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zcmt[] Extension for Compressed Table Jumps > Table Jump Overview > jvt CSR

chunks.text: The FLI.S instruction loads one of 32 single-precision floating-point constants, encoded in the rs1 field, into floating-point register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The correspondence of rs1 field values and single-precision floating-point values is shown in . FLI.S is encoded like FMV.W.X, but with rs2=1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is implemented, FLI.D performs the analogous operation, but loads a double-precision value into floating-point register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: If the Q extension is implemented, FLI.Q performs the analogous operation, but loads a quad-precision value into floating-point register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: If the Zfh or Zvfh extension is implemented, FLI.H performs the analogous operation, but loads a half-precision floating-point value into register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: The FLI.fmt instructions never set any floating-point exception flags.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is implemented, FMINM.D and FMAXM.D instructions are analogously defined to operate on double-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Minimum and Maximum Instructions

chunks.text: If the Zfh extension is implemented, FMINM.H and FMAXM.H instructions are analogously defined to operate on half-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Minimum and Maximum Instructions

chunks.text: If the Q extension is implemented, FMINM.Q and FMAXM.Q instructions are analogously defined to operate on quad-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Minimum and Maximum Instructions

chunks.text: These instructions are encoded like their FMIN and FMAX counterparts, but with instruction bit 13 set to 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Minimum and Maximum Instructions

chunks.text: The FROUND.S instruction rounds the single-precision floating-point number in floating-point register rs1 to an integer, according to the rounding mode specified in the instruction's rm field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: Signaling NaN inputs cause the invalid operation exception flag to be set; no other exception flags are set. FROUND.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2=4.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: The FROUNDNX.S instruction is defined similarly, but it also sets the inexact exception flag if the input differs from the rounded result and is not NaN. FROUNDNX.S is encoded like FCVT.S.D, but with rs2=5.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is implemented, FROUND.D and FROUNDNX.D instructions are analogously defined to operate on double-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: They are encoded like FCVT.D.S, but with rs2=4 and 5, respectively,
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: If the Zfh extension is implemented, FROUND.H and FROUNDNX.H instructions are analogously defined to operate on half-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: They are encoded like FCVT.H.S, but with rs2=4 and 5, respectively,
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: If the Q extension is implemented, FROUND.Q and FROUNDNX.Q instructions are analogously defined to operate on quad-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: They are encoded like FCVT.Q.S, but with rs2=4 and 5, respectively,
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Round-to-Integer Instructions

chunks.text: Bits 31:0 are taken from the rounded, unbounded two's complement result, then sign-extended to XLEN bits and written to integer register rd. ±{inf} and NaN are converted to zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Modular Convert-to-Integer Instruction

chunks.text: Floating-point exception flags are raised the same as they would be for FCVT.W.D with the same input operand.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Modular Convert-to-Integer Instruction

chunks.text: This instruction is only provided if the D extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Modular Convert-to-Integer Instruction

chunks.text: It is encoded like FCVT.W.D, but with the rs2 field set to 8 and the rm field set to 1 (RTZ).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Modular Convert-to-Integer Instruction

chunks.text: For RV32 only, if the D extension is implemented, the FMVH.X.D instruction moves bits 63:32 of floating-point register rs1 into integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: It is encoded in the OP-FP major opcode with funct3=0, rs2=1, and funct7=1110001.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: For RV32 only, if the D extension is implemented, the FMVP.D.X instruction moves a double-precision number from a pair of integer registers into a floating-point register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64 only, if the Q extension is implemented, the FMVH.X.Q instruction moves bits 127:64 of floating-point register rs1 into integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: It is encoded in the OP-FP major opcode with funct3=0, rs2=1, and funct7=1110011.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: For RV64 only, if the Q extension is implemented, the FMVP.Q.X instruction moves a double-precision number from a pair of integer registers into a floating-point register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Move Instructions

chunks.text: The FLEQ.S and FLTQ.S instructions are defined like the FLE.S and FLT.S instructions, except that quiet NaN inputs do not cause the invalid operation exception flag to be set.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Comparison Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is implemented, FLEQ.D and FLTQ.D instructions are analogously defined to operate on double-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Comparison Instructions

chunks.text: If the Zfh extension is implemented, FLEQ.H and FLTQ.H instructions are analogously defined to operate on half-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Comparison Instructions

chunks.text: If the Q extension is implemented, FLEQ.Q and FLTQ.Q instructions are analogously defined to operate on quad-precision numbers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Comparison Instructions

chunks.text: These instructions are encoded like their FLE and FLT counterparts, but with instruction bit 14 set to 1.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfa" Extension for Additional Floating-Point Instructions, Version 1.0 > Load-Immediate Instructions > Comparison Instructions

chunks.text: BF16 computational instructions defined in this chapter support all IEEE 754-2008 features, including all rounding modes, subnormal inputs and outputs, overflow and underflow, and default exception handling.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfbfmin` Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Conversions > BF16 Number Format

chunks.text: BF16 values are NaN-boxed when held in `f` registers, as described in .
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfbfmin` Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Conversions > BF16 Number Format

chunks.text: Round according to the RM field.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfbfmin` Extension for Scalar BFloat16 Conversions > BF16 Number Format > fcvt.bf16.s

chunks.text: The NaN-boxing scheme described in is extended to allow a half-precision value to be NaN-boxed inside a single-precision value (which may be recursively NaN-boxed inside a double- or quad-precision value when the D or Q extension is present).
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point

chunks.text: New 16-bit variants of LOAD-FP and STORE-FP instructions are added, encoded with a new value for the funct3 width field.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLH and FSH are only guaranteed to execute atomically if the effective address is naturally aligned.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: FLH and FSH do not modify the bits being transferred; in particular, the payloads of non-canonical NaNs are preserved. FLH NaN-boxes the result written to rd, whereas FSH ignores all but the lower 16 bits in rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions

chunks.text: A new supported format is added to the format field of most instructions, as shown in .
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Computational Instructions

chunks.text: These instructions are defined analogously to the single-precision-to-integer and integer-to-single-precision conversion instructions. FCVT.W.H or FCVT.L.H converts a half-precision floating-point number to a signed 32-bit or 64-bit integer, respectively. FCVT.H.W or FCVT.H.L converts a 32-bit or 64-bit signed integer, respectively, into a half-precision floating-point number. FCVT.WU.H, FCVT.LU.H, FCVT.H.WU, and FCVT.H.LU variants convert to or from unsigned integer values. FCVT.L[U].H and FCVT.H.L[U] are RV64-only instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: If the D extension is present, FCVT.D.H or FCVT.H.D converts a half-precision floating-point number to a double-precision floating-point number, or vice-versa, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: If the Q extension is present, FCVT.Q.H or FCVT.H.Q converts a half-precision floating-point number to a quad-precision floating-point number, or vice-versa, respectively.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: Instructions are provided to move bit patterns between the floating-point and integer registers. FMV.X.H moves the half-precision value in floating-point register rs1 to a representation in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding in integer register rd, filling the upper XLEN-16 bits with copies of the floating-point number's sign bit.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: FMV.H.X moves the half-precision value encoded in the IEEE 754-2008 encoding from the lower 16 bits of integer register rs1 to the floating-point register rd, NaN-boxing the result.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zfh` Extension for Half-Precision Floating-Point > Half-Precision Load and Store Instructions > Half-Precision Conversion and Move Instructions

chunks.text: This section describes the Zfhmin standard extension, which provides minimal support for 16-bit half-precision binary floating-point instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zfhmin[] Extension for Minimal Half-Precision Floating-Point

chunks.text: If the D extension is present, the FCVT.D.H and FCVT.H.D instructions are also included.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zfhmin[] Extension for Minimal Half-Precision Floating-Point

chunks.text: If the Q extension is present, the FCVT.Q.H and FCVT.H.Q instructions are additionally included.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zfhmin[] Extension for Minimal Half-Precision Floating-Point

chunks.text: This chapter defines the "Zfinx" extension (pronounced "z-f-in-x") that provides instructions similar to those in the standard floating-point F extension for single-precision floating-point instructions but which operate on the `x` registers instead of the `f` registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0

chunks.text: This chapter also defines the "Zdinx", "Zhinx", and "Zhinxmin" extensions that provide similar instructions for other floating-point precisions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Zfinx extension adds all of the instructions that the F extension adds, except for the transfer instructions FLW, FSW, FMV.W.X, FMV.X.W, C.FLW[SP], and C.FSW[SP].
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Zfinx variants of these F-extension instructions have the same semantics, except that whenever such an instruction would have accessed an `f` register, it instead accesses the `x` register with the same number.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The Zfinx extension depends on the "Zicsr" extension for control and status register access.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Floating-point operands of width w < XLEN bits occupy bits w-1:0 of an `x` register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: Floating-point operations on w-bit operands ignore operand bits XLEN-1: w.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: Floating-point operations that produce w < XLEN-bit results fill bits XLEN-1: w with copies of bit w-1 (the sign bit).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values

chunks.text: The Zdinx extension provides analogous double-precision floating-point instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zdinx

chunks.text: The Zdinx extension depends upon the Zfinx extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zdinx

chunks.text: The Zdinx extension adds all of the instructions that the D extension adds, except for the transfer instructions FLD, FSD, FMV.D.X, FMV.X.D, C.FLD[SP], and C.FSD[SP].
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zdinx

chunks.text: The Zdinx variants of these D-extension instructions have the same semantics, except that whenever such an instruction would have accessed an `f` register, it instead accesses the `x` register with the same number.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zdinx

chunks.text: Double-precision operands in RV32Zdinx are held in aligned `x`-register pairs, i.e., register numbers must be even.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Processing of Wider Values

chunks.text: Use of misaligned (odd-numbered) registers for double-width floating-point operands is reserved.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Processing of Wider Values

chunks.text: The Zhinx extension provides analogous half-precision floating-point instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinx

chunks.text: The Zhinx extension depends upon the Zfinx extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinx

chunks.text: The Zhinx extension adds all of the instructions that the Zfh extension adds, except for the transfer instructions FLH, FSH, FMV.H.X, and FMV.X.H.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinx

chunks.text: The Zhinx variants of these Zfh-extension instructions have the same semantics, except that whenever such an instruction would have accessed an `f` register, it instead accesses the `x` register with the same number.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinx

chunks.text: The Zhinxmin extension provides minimal support for 16-bit half-precision floating-point instructions that operate on the `x` registers.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinxmin

chunks.text: The Zhinxmin extension depends upon the Zfinx extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinxmin

chunks.text: The Zhinxmin extension includes the following instructions from the Zhinx extension: FCVT.S.H and FCVT.H.S.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinxmin

chunks.text: If the Zdinx extension is present, the FCVT.D.H and FCVT.H.D instructions are also included.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Zhinxmin

chunks.text: As described in , the `mstatus` field FS is hardwired to 0 if the Zfinx extension is implemented, and FS no longer affects the trapping behavior of floating-point instructions or `fcsr` accesses.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Privileged Architecture Implications

chunks.text: The `misa` bits F, D, and Q are hardwired to 0 when the Zfinx extension is implemented.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zfinx", "Zdinx", "Zhinx", "Zhinxmin" Extensions for Floating-Point in Integer Registers, Version 1.0 > Processing of Narrower Values > Privileged Architecture Implications

chunks.text: Most of these extensions are accordingly named with the prefix "Zi", with the exception of the integer multiplication and division extensions, which are named "M" or prefixed with "Zm".
chunks.section: Preamble > Scalar Integer Extensions

chunks.text: If the Zic64b extension is implemented, then cache blocks must be 64 bytes in size, naturally aligned in the address space.
chunks.section: Preamble > Zic64b Extension for 64-byte Cache Blocks, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Ziccamoa extension is implemented, then main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support all atomics in the Zaamo extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccamoa Extension for Main Memory Atomics, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Ziccamoc extension is implemented, then main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must provide ``AMOCASQ``-level PMA support.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccamoc Extension for Main Memory Compare-and-Swap, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Ziccif extension is implemented, main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support instruction fetch, and any instruction fetches of naturally aligned power-of-2 sizes of at most `min(ILEN,XLEN)` bits are atomic.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccif Extension for Instruction-Fetch Atomicity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: An implementation with the Ziccif extension fetches instructions in a manner equivalent to the following state machine.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccif Extension for Instruction-Fetch Atomicity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the `T` bytes fetched begin with a complete instruction of length `L` {le} `T`, then execute that instruction, discard the remaining `T` - `L` bytes fetched, and go back to step 1, using the updated `pc`.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccif Extension for Instruction-Fetch Atomicity, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Zicclsm extension is implemented, then misaligned loads and stores to main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must be supported.
chunks.section: Preamble > Zicclsm Extension for Main Memory Misaligned Accesses, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Standard software distributions should assume their existence only for correctness, not for performance.
chunks.section: Preamble > Zicclsm Extension for Main Memory Misaligned Accesses, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the Ziccrse extension is implemented, then main memory regions with both the cacheability and coherence PMAs must support the RsrvEventual PMA.
chunks.section: Preamble > Ziccrse Extension for Main Memory Reservability, Version 1.0

chunks.text: RISC-V ISAs provide a set of up to thirty-two 64-bit performance counters and timers that are accessible via unprivileged XLEN-bit read-only CSR registers `0xC00`–`0xC1F` (when XLEN=32, the upper 32 bits are accessed via CSR registers `0xC80`–`0xC9F`).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The Zicntr extension depends on the Zicsr extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: For base ISAs with XLEN{ge}64, CSR instructions can access the full 64-bit CSRs directly.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: In particular, the RDCYCLE, RDTIME, and RDINSTRET pseudoinstructions read the full 64 bits of the `cycle`, `time`, and `instret` counters.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: For base ISAs with XLEN=32, the Zicntr extension enables the three 64-bit read-only counters to be accessed in 32-bit pieces.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The RDCYCLE, RDTIME, and RDINSTRET pseudoinstructions provide the lower 32 bits, and the RDCYCLEH, RDTIMEH, and RDINSTRETH pseudoinstructions provide the upper 32 bits of the respective counters.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The RDCYCLE pseudoinstruction reads the low XLEN bits of the `cycle` CSR which holds a count of the number of clock cycles executed by the processor core on which the hart is running from an arbitrary start time in the past. RDCYCLEH is only present when XLEN=32 and reads bits 63-32 of the same cycle counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The execution environment should provide a means to determine the current rate (cycles/second) at which the cycle counter is incrementing.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The RDTIME pseudoinstruction reads the low XLEN bits of the "time" CSR, which counts wall-clock real time that has passed from an arbitrary start time in the past. RDTIMEH is only present when XLEN=32 and reads bits 63-32 of the same real-time counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The execution environment should provide a means of determining the period of a counter tick (seconds/tick).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The period should be constant within a small error bound.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The real-time clocks of all harts must be synchronized to within one tick of the real-time clock.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The RDINSTRET pseudoinstruction reads the low XLEN bits of the `instret` CSR, which counts the number of instructions retired by this hart from some arbitrary start point in the past. RDINSTRETH is only present when XLEN=32 and reads bits 63-32 of the same instruction counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: The following code sequence will read a valid 64-bit cycle counter value into `x3:x2`, even if the counter overflows its lower half between reading its upper and lower halves.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: [source,asm.] .Sample code for reading the 64-bit cycle counter when XLEN=32. again: rdcycleh x3 rdcycle x2 rdcycleh x4 bne x3, x4, again
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicntr" Extension for Base Counters and Timers

chunks.text: [insns-czero-eqz,reftext="Conditional zero, if condition is equal to zero"]
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order)

chunks.text: If rs2 contains the value zero, this instruction writes the value zero to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > czero.eqz

chunks.text: Furthermore, if the Zkt extension is implemented, this instruction's timing is independent of the data values in rs1 and rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > czero.eqz

chunks.text: If rs2 contains a nonzero value, this instruction writes the value zero to rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > czero.eqz > czero.nez

chunks.text: Furthermore, if the Zkt extension is implemented, this instruction's timing is independent of the data values in rs1 and rs2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > czero.eqz > czero.nez

chunks.text: |*Conditional add, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? (rs1 + rs2) : rs1` |czero.nez rd, rs2, rc add rd, rs1, rd .8+.^|2 insns
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional add, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? (rs1 + rs2) : rs1` |czero.eqz rd, rs2, rc add rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional subtract, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? (rs1 - rs2) : rs1` |czero.nez rd, rs2, rc sub rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional subtract, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? (rs1 - rs2) : rs1` |czero.eqz rd, rs2, rc sub rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-or, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? (rs1 \| rs2) : rs1` |czero.nez rd, rs2, rc or rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-or, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? (rs1 \| rs2) : rs1` |czero.eqz rd, rs2, rc or rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-xor, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? (rs1 ^ rs2) : rs1` |czero.nez rd, rs2, rc xor rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-xor, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? (rs1 ^ rs2) : rs1` |czero.eqz rd, rs2, rc xor rd, rs1, rd
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-and, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? (rs1 & rs2) : rs1` |and rd, rs1, rs2 czero.eqz rtmp, rs1, rc or rd, rd, rtmp .4+.^|3 insns + (requires 1 temporary)
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional bitwise-and, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? (rs1 & rs2) : rs1` |and rd, rs1, rs2 czero.nez rtmp, rs1, rc or rd, rd, rtmp
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional select, if zero* + `rd = (rc 0) ? rs1 : rs2` |czero.nez rd, rs1, rc czero.eqz rtmp, rs2, rc add rd, rd, rtmp
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: |*Conditional select, if non-zero* + `rd = (rc != 0) ? rs1 : rs2` |czero.eqz rd, rs1, rc czero.nez rtmp, rs2, rc add rd, rd, rtmp
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicond" Extension for Integer Conditional Operations, Version 1.0.0 > Instructions (in alphabetical order) > Usage examples > Instruction sequences

chunks.text: RISC-V defines a separate address space of 4096 Control and Status registers associated with each hart.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0

chunks.text: This chapter defines the full set of CSR instructions that operate on these CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0

chunks.text: All CSR instructions atomically read-modify-write a single CSR, whose CSR specifier is encoded in the 12-bit csr field of the instruction held in bits 31-20.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The immediate forms use a 5-bit zero-extended immediate encoded in the rs1 field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRW (Atomic Read/Write CSR) instruction atomically swaps values in the CSRs and integer registers. CSRRW reads the old value of the CSR, zero-extends the value to XLEN bits, then writes it to integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The initial value in rs1 is written to the CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: If rd=`x0`, then the instruction shall not read the CSR and shall not cause any of the side effects that might occur on a CSR read.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRS (Atomic Read and Set Bits in CSR) instruction reads the value of the CSR, zero-extends the value to XLEN bits, and writes it to integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The initial value in integer register rs1 is treated as a bit mask that specifies bit positions to be set in the CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Any bit that is high in rs1 will cause the corresponding bit to be set in the CSR, if that CSR bit is writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRC (Atomic Read and Clear Bits in CSR) instruction reads the value of the CSR, zero-extends the value to XLEN bits, and writes it to integer register rd.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The initial value in integer register rs1 is treated as a bit mask that specifies bit positions to be cleared in the CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Any bit that is high in rs1 will cause the corresponding bit to be cleared in the CSR, if that CSR bit is writable.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: For both CSRRS and CSRRC, if rs1=`x0`, then the instruction will not write to the CSR at all, and so shall not cause any of the side effects that might otherwise occur on a CSR write, nor raise illegal-instruction exceptions on accesses to read-only CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Both CSRRS and CSRRC always read the addressed CSR and cause any read side effects regardless of rs1 and rd fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: A CSRRW with rs1=`x0` will attempt to write zero to the destination CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRWI, CSRRSI, and CSRRCI variants are similar to CSRRW, CSRRS, and CSRRC respectively, except they update the CSR using an XLEN-bit value obtained by zero-extending a 5-bit unsigned immediate (uimm[4:0]) field encoded in the rs1 field instead of a value from an integer register.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: For CSRRSI and CSRRCI, if the uimm[4:0] field is zero, then these instructions will not write to the CSR, and shall not cause any of the side effects that might otherwise occur on a CSR write, nor raise illegal-instruction exceptions on accesses to read-only CSRs.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: For CSRRWI, if rd=`x0`, then the instruction shall not read the CSR and shall not cause any of the side effects that might occur on a CSR read.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Both CSRRSI and CSRRCI will always read the CSR and cause any read side effects regardless of rd and rs1 fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: |Instruction |rd is `x0` |uimm=0 |Reads CSR |Writes CSR
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: summarizes the behavior of the CSR instructions with respect to whether they read and/or write the CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: In addition to side effects that occur as a consequence of reading or writing a CSR, individual fields within a CSR might have side effects when written.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRW[I] instructions action side effects for all such fields within the written CSR.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The CSRRS[I] and CSRRC[I] instructions only action side effects for fields for which the rs1 or uimm argument has at least one bit set corresponding to that field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: For any event or consequence that occurs due to a CSR having a particular value, if a write to the CSR gives it that value, the resulting event or consequence is said to be an indirect effect of the write.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Indirect effects of a CSR write are not considered by the RISC-V ISA to be side effects of that write.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Some CSRs, such as the instructions-retired counter, `instret`, may be modified as side effects of instruction execution.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: In these cases, if a CSR access instruction reads a CSR, it reads the value prior to the execution of the instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: If a CSR access instruction writes such a CSR, the explicit write is done instead of the update from the side effect.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: In particular, a value written to `instret` by one instruction will be the value read by the following instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The assembler pseudoinstruction to read a CSR, CSRR rd, csr, is encoded as CSRRS rd, csr, x0.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: The assembler pseudoinstruction to write a CSR, CSRW csr, rs1, is encoded as CSRRW x0, csr, rs1, while CSRWI csr, uimm, is encoded as CSRRWI x0, csr, uimm.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Further assembler pseudoinstructions are defined to set and clear bits in the CSR when the old value is not required: CSRS/CSRC csr, rs1; CSRSI/CSRCI csr, uimm.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions

chunks.text: Each RISC-V hart normally observes its own CSR accesses, including its implicit CSR accesses, as performed in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: Furthermore, an explicit CSR read returns the CSR state before the execution of the instruction, while an explicit CSR write suppresses and overrides any implicit writes or modifications to the same CSR by the same instruction.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: Likewise, any side effects from an explicit CSR access are normally observed to occur synchronously in program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: Unless specified otherwise, the full consequences of any such side effects are observable by the very next instruction, and no consequences may be observed out-of-order by preceding instructions. (Note the distinction made earlier between side effects and indirect effects of CSR writes.)
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: For the RVWMO memory consistency model (), CSR accesses are weakly ordered by default, so other harts or devices may observe CSR accesses in an order different from program order.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: In addition, CSR accesses are not ordered with respect to explicit memory accesses, unless a CSR access modifies the execution behavior of the instruction that performs the explicit memory access or unless a CSR access and an explicit memory access are ordered by either the syntactic dependencies defined by the memory model or the ordering requirements defined in .
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: To enforce ordering in all other cases, software should execute a FENCE instruction between the relevant accesses.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: For the purposes of the FENCE instruction, CSR read accesses are classified as device input (I), and CSR write accesses are classified as device output (O).
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: The hardware platform may define that accesses to certain CSRs are strongly ordered, Accesses to strongly ordered CSRs have stronger ordering constraints with respect to accesses to both weakly ordered CSRs and accesses to memory-mapped I/O regions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zicsr" Extension for Control and Status Register (CSR) Instructions, Version 2.0 > CSR Instructions > CSR Access Ordering

chunks.text: The unused fields in the FENCE.I instruction, funct12, rs1, and rd, are reserved for finer-grain fences in future extensions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zifencei" Extension for Instruction-Fetch Fence, Version 2.0

chunks.text: For forward compatibility, base implementations shall ignore these fields, and standard software shall zero these fields.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zifencei" Extension for Instruction-Fetch Fence, Version 2.0

chunks.text: The NTL.P1 instruction indicates that the target instruction does not exhibit temporal locality within the capacity of the innermost level of private cache in the memory hierarchy. NTL.P1 is encoded as ADD x0, x0, x2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The NTL.PALL instruction indicates that the target instruction does not exhibit temporal locality within the capacity of any level of private cache in the memory hierarchy. NTL.PALL is encoded as ADD x0, x0, x3.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The NTL.S1 instruction indicates that the target instruction does not exhibit temporal locality within the capacity of the innermost level of shared cache in the memory hierarchy. NTL.S1 is encoded as ADD x0, x0, x4.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The NTL.ALL instruction indicates that the target instruction does not exhibit temporal locality within the capacity of any level of cache in the memory hierarchy. NTL.ALL is encoded as ADD x0, x0, x5.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The table also recommends which NTL variant that implementation-tuned software should use to avoid allocating in a particular cache level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Furthermore, software tuned for such a system should use NTL.P1 to indicate a lack of temporal locality exploitable by the L1, or should use NTL.ALL indicate a lack of temporal locality exploitable by the L2.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: If the C or Zca extension is provided, compressed variants of these HINTs are also provided: C.NTL.P1 is encoded as C.ADD x0, x2; C.NTL.PALL is encoded as C.ADD x0, x3; C.NTL.S1 is encoded as C.ADD x0, x4; and C.NTL.ALL is encoded as C.ADD x0, x5.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: When an NTL instruction is applied to a prefetch hint in the Zicbop extension, it indicates that a cache line should be prefetched into a cache that is outer from the level specified by the NTL.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: In the event that a trap is taken on the target instruction, implementations are discouraged from applying the NTL to the first instruction in the trap handler.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintntl" Extension for Non-Temporal Locality Hints, Version 1.0

chunks.text: The PAUSE instruction is a HINT that indicates the current hart's rate of instruction retirement should be temporarily reduced or paused.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintpause" Extension for Pause Hint, Version 2.0

chunks.text: The duration of its effect must be bounded and may be zero.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintpause" Extension for Pause Hint, Version 2.0

chunks.text: PAUSE is encoded as a FENCE instruction with pred=`W`, succ=`0`, fm=`0`, rd=`x0`, and rs1=`x0`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihintpause" Extension for Pause Hint, Version 2.0

chunks.text: The Zihpm extension comprises up to 29 additional unprivileged 64-bit hardware performance counters, `hpmcounter3-hpmcounter31`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: When XLEN=32, the upper 32 bits of these performance counters are accessible via additional CSRs `hpmcounter3h- hpmcounter31h`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: The Zihpm extension depends on the Zicsr extension.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: Accessing an unimplemented counter may cause an illegal-instruction exception or may return a constant value.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: If the configuration used to select the events counted by a counter is misconfigured, the counter may return a constant value.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: The execution environment should provide a means to determine the number and width of the implemented counters, and an interface to configure the events to be counted by each counter.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Zihpm" Extension for Hardware Performance Counters

chunks.text: Operands containing `src` for store instructions and `dest` for load instructions are held in aligned `x`-register pairs, i.e., register numbers must be even.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: As the access size is 64-bit, accesses are only considered naturally aligned for effective addresses that are a multiple of 8.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: In this case, these instructions are guaranteed to not raise an address-misaligned exception.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: Even if naturally aligned, the memory access might not be performed atomically.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: If the effective address is a multiple of 4, then each word access is required to be performed atomically.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: | |Alignment |Word accesses guaranteed atomic? |Can cause misaligned trap? |8{nbsp}B |yes |no |4{nbsp}B not 8{nbsp}B |yes |yes |else |no | yes |
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: To ensure resumable trap handling is possible for the load instructions, the base register must have its original value if a trap is taken.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions

chunks.text: For the purposes of RVWMO and exception handling, LD and SD instructions are considered to be misaligned loads and stores, with one additional constraint: an LD or SD instruction whose effective address is a multiple of 4 gives rise to two 4-byte memory operations.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Exception Handling

chunks.text: If instructions with 4-byte-aligned effective address are decomposed into two 32b operations, there is no constraint on the order in which the operations are performed and each operation is guaranteed to be atomic.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Exception Handling

chunks.text: These decomposed sequences are interruptible.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Exception Handling

chunks.text: Exceptions might occur on subsequent operations, making the effects of previous operations within the same instruction visible.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Exception Handling

chunks.text: [insns-ld,reftext="Load doubleword to register pair, 32-bit encoding"]
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Instructions

chunks.text: The effective address is obtained by adding register rs1 to the sign-extended 12-bit offset.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Instructions > ld

chunks.text: [insns-sd,reftext="Store doubleword from register pair, 32-bit encoding"]
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Instructions > ld

chunks.text: The effective address is obtained by adding register rs1 to the sign-extended 12-bit offset.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zilsd[] Extension for Load/Store Pair Instructions > Use of x0 as operand > Instructions > sd

chunks.text: Unless redefined by another extension, these instructions simply write 0 to `x[rd]`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zimop[] Extension for May-Be-Operations

chunks.text: Unless redefined by another extension, these instructions simply write 0 to `x[rd]`.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zimop[] Extension for May-Be-Operations

chunks.text: The extension that redefines a MOP may define an alternate assembly mnemonic.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zimop[] Extension for May-Be-Operations

chunks.text: The MOPs defined in the ext:zimop[] extension do not carry a syntactic dependency from `x[rs1]` or `x[rs2]` to `x[rd]`, though an extension that redefines the MOP may impose such a requirement.
chunks.section: Preamble > ext:zimop[] Extension for May-Be-Operations

chunks.text: All load operations behave as if they have an acquire-RCpc annotation * All store operations behave as if they have a release-RCpc annotation. * All AMOs behave as if they have both acquire-RCsc and release-RCsc annotations.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Ztso" Extension for Total Store Ordering, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Additionally, if the Ztso extension is implemented, then vector memory instructions in the V extension and Zve family of extensions follow RVTSO at the instruction level.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Ztso" Extension for Total Store Ordering, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Binaries compiled to run only under Ztso should indicate as such via a flag in the binary, so that platforms which do not implement Ztso can simply refuse to run them.
chunks.section: Preamble > "Ztso" Extension for Total Store Ordering, Version 1.0

chunks.text: Round according to the frm register.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zvfbfmin` Extension for Vector BF16 Conversions > vfncvtbf16.f.f.w

chunks.text: The sum is rounded according to the frm register.
chunks.section: Preamble > `Zvfbfwma` Extension for Vector BF16 Widening Multiply-Accumulation > vfwmaccbf16

chunks.text: The maximum size in bits of a vector element that any operation can produce or consume, ELEN {ge} 8, which must be a power of 2. .
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Implementation-defined Constant Parameters

chunks.text: The number of bits in a single vector register, VLEN {ge} ELEN, which must be a power of 2, and must be no greater than 2^16^.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Implementation-defined Constant Parameters

chunks.text: Standard vector extensions () and architecture profiles may set further constraints on ELEN and VLEN.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Implementation-defined Constant Parameters

chunks.text: The vector extension supports writing binary code that under certain constraints will execute portably on harts with different values for the VLEN parameter, provided the harts support the required element types and instructions.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Implementation-defined Constant Parameters

chunks.text: The vector extension adds 32 vector registers, and seven unprivileged CSRs (`vstart`, `vxsat`, `vxrm`, `vcsr`, `vtype`, `vl`, `vlenb`) to a base scalar RISC-V ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model

chunks.text: | 0x008 | URW | vstart | Vector start element index | 0x009 | URW | vxsat | Fixed-Point Saturate Flag | 0x00A | URW | vxrm | Fixed-Point Rounding Mode | 0x00F | URW | vcsr | Vector control and status register | 0xC20 | URO | vl | Vector length | 0xC21 | URO | vtype | Vector data type register | 0xC22 | URO | vlenb | VLEN/8 (vector register length in bytes) |
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model

chunks.text: The vector extension adds 32 architectural vector registers, `v0`-`v31` to the base scalar RISC-V ISA.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Registers

chunks.text: Each vector register has a fixed VLEN bits of state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Registers

chunks.text: A vector context status field, `VS`, is added to `mstatus[10:9]` and shadowed in `sstatus[10:9]`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: It is defined analogously to the floating-point context status field, `FS`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: Attempts to execute any vector instruction, or to access the vector CSRs, raise an illegal-instruction exception when `mstatus.VS` is set to Off.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: When `mstatus.VS` is set to Initial or Clean, executing any instruction that changes vector state, including the vector CSRs, will change `mstatus.VS` to Dirty.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: Implementations may also change `mstatus.VS` from Initial or Clean to Dirty at any time, even when there is no change in vector state.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: If `mstatus.VS` is Dirty, `mstatus.SD` is 1; otherwise, `mstatus.SD` is set in accordance with existing specifications.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: Implementations may have a writable `misa.V` field.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: Analogous to the way in which the floating-point unit is handled, the `mstatus.VS` field may exist even if `misa.V` is clear.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `mstatus`

chunks.text: When the hypervisor extension is present, a vector context status field, `VS`, is added to `vsstatus[10:9]`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `vsstatus`

chunks.text: It is defined analogously to the floating-point context status field, `FS`.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `vsstatus`

chunks.text: When V=1, both `vsstatus.VS` and `mstatus.VS` are in effect: attempts to execute any vector instruction, or to access the vector CSRs, raise an illegal-instruction exception when either field is set to Off.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `vsstatus`

chunks.text: When V=1 and neither `vsstatus.VS` nor `mstatus.VS` is set to Off, executing any instruction that changes vector state, including the vector CSRs, will change both `mstatus.VS` and `vsstatus.VS` to Dirty.
chunks.section: Preamble > "V" Extension for Vector Operations, Version 1.0 > Vector Extension Programmer's Model > Vector Context Status in `vsstatus`
